io_uring: A Linux asynchronous I/O interface (Jens Axboe, kernel 5.1, 2019) built on two shared-memory rings, a submission queue and a completion queue, letting an application hand the kernel batches of work with very few system calls. Fast, but a large and fast-growing attack surface: around six in ten Linux kernel exploits submitted to Google's 2022 bounty went through it.
POSIX AIO: The POSIX standard's asynchronous I/O interface, an older way for a program to ask the kernel to perform input and output without blocking. On Linux it is the fourth of five accreted I/O interfaces, after select, poll and epoll and before io_uring.
Jens Axboe: Linux kernel engineer, maintainer of the block layer and author of io_uring (merged in Linux 5.1, 2019) and the fio benchmark tool.
Jonathan Lemon: FreeBSD developer who designed and wrote kqueue, the unified event-notification interface shipped in FreeBSD 4.1 in 2000 and later adopted by macOS and the other BSDs.
PinTheft: A 2026 Linux local-privilege-escalation vulnerability (CVE-2026-43494) that chains a reference-counting bug in the kernel's RDS sockets with io_uring to overwrite a setuid-root binary in memory and obtain a root shell.
dependency confusion: A supply-chain attack in which a package is published to a public registry under the name of a company's private internal package, so that a misconfigured build resolves the public impostor in preference to the intended private one.
devDependencies: The packages in an npm package.json needed only to develop or build a project, not to run it in production. A plain npm install pulls them in and runs them with the same permissions as everything else, which is when a post-install script fires; Snyk's default scan omits them.
Miasma: A June 2026 npm supply-chain attack that republished thirty-two packages under the @redhat-cloud-services scope through a hijacked continuous-integration pipeline identity. The packages carried valid signed provenance and stole credentials all the same.
node-ipc: A widely used Node.js inter-process-communication library, around 822,000 weekly downloads. In May 2026 its maintainer account was taken over after the domain behind its recovery email was allowed to lapse and re-registered by an attacker for the price of a registration.
provenance: A signed attestation recording how a software artifact was built and from which source. Useful but not a trust boundary: it certifies the build, not that the identity permitted to publish is still in the right hands.
remote-access trojan: Malware (a RAT) that opens a covert remote-control channel to an attacker, allowing arbitrary commands, file access and data exfiltration from an infected machine. The axios npm compromise of March 2026 shipped a cross-platform one through a post-install script.
Reproducible Builds: An effort to make a build produce a bit-for-bit identical artifact from the same source, so an independent party can rebuild a release and confirm the binary on disk matches the published source. A pillar of the responsibility line node_modules lacks.
SLSA: Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts, a framework of provenance and integrity guarantees for build pipelines (pronounced salsa). A valid SLSA attestation certifies the pipeline that built an artifact, not that its publishing identity is uncompromised.
Socket: A behavioural supply-chain security tool that inspects package code for risky behaviour rather than waiting for a published advisory. It caught the node-ipc compromise, but is itself installed through npm into the environment it audits.
SaaS: Software as a Service. Renting access to software hosted by the vendor, paid via recurring subscription rather than a perpetual licence.
IaaS: Infrastructure as a Service. Virtual machines, storage, and networking rented from a cloud provider instead of running your own hardware.
PaaS: Platform as a Service. A managed environment where developers deploy code without managing servers, containers, or operating systems.
ORM: Object-Relational Mapping. A layer that translates between programming-language objects and relational database rows, abstracting SQL behind method calls.
MVC: Model-View-Controller. An architectural pattern that separates data (Model), presentation (View), and input handling (Controller).
SPA: Single-Page Application. A web app that loads one HTML document and rewrites it dynamically via JavaScript, avoiding full page reloads.
SSR: Server-Side Rendering. Generating the full HTML of a page on the server before sending it to the browser, as opposed to client-side rendering.
CSR: Client-Side Rendering. Building the page DOM entirely in the browser via JavaScript after an initial empty HTML shell is delivered.
REST: Representational State Transfer. An architectural style for APIs using standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) on resource URLs.
JSON-RPC: JSON Remote Procedure Call. A lightweight protocol that encodes function calls and responses as JSON objects, transported over HTTP or WebSockets.
CI/CD: Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment. Automated pipelines that build, test, and deploy code on every commit.
OKLCH: A perceptually uniform colour space using Lightness, Chroma, and Hue. Produces consistent perceived brightness across all hues, unlike HSL.
OOCSS: Object-Oriented CSS. A methodology that separates structure from skin and container from content, promoting reusable CSS classes.
CSS Layers: The @layer rule in CSS, allowing authors to define explicit cascade priority between groups of styles, eliminating specificity wars.
Specificity: The algorithm browsers use to determine which CSS rule wins when multiple rules target the same element. Based on selector weight: ID > class > element.
Fetch API: The modern browser interface for making HTTP requests, replacing XMLHttpRequest. Returns Promises and supports streaming.
ES Modules: The native JavaScript module system using import/export syntax, loaded by browsers without a bundler.
WebSocket: A persistent, full-duplex communication channel over a single TCP connection, enabling real-time data exchange between browser and server.
Electron: A framework for building cross-platform desktop applications using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Each app bundles its own copy of Chromium (~150 MB). Released by GitHub in 2014.
Tauri: A framework for building cross-platform desktop apps using web technologies with the operating system's native WebView instead of bundling Chromium. Installer: 2.5 MB vs Electron's 85 MB. Written in Rust.
Chromium: The open-source browser engine behind Google Chrome, Microsoft Edge, and Electron apps. Each Electron application bundles its own full copy.
Zed: A code editor written in Rust by the creators of Atom. 45 MB idle, 0.12s startup. No bundled browser engine. GPU-accelerated rendering.
WebView: A native operating system component that renders web content. Used by Tauri and modern Teams instead of bundling a full browser engine like Electron.
Docker: A container runtime that packages applications with their dependencies into isolated filesystem images, sharing the host kernel.
Kubernetes: An orchestration platform for managing containerised workloads across clusters. Handles scheduling, scaling, and self-healing.
FreeBSD: An open-source Unix operating system descended from the Berkeley Software Distribution. Known for ZFS, Jails, and its licence model.
ZFS: Zettabyte File System. A combined filesystem and volume manager with checksumming, snapshots, clones, and built-in RAID.
Jails: FreeBSD's OS-level virtualisation. Lightweight isolation of processes, filesystems, and network stacks without a hypervisor.
WireGuard: A modern VPN protocol using state-of-the-art cryptography. One C file, ~4,000 lines, replaces IPsec and OpenVPN.
Caddy: A web server with automatic HTTPS via built-in ACME client. Written in Go. Often used as a reverse proxy.
Rust: A systems programming language emphasising memory safety without garbage collection, using ownership and borrowing rules enforced at compile time.
Lua: A lightweight embeddable scripting and configuration language designed at PUC-Rio's Tecgraf in 1993 by Roberto Ierusalimschy, Luiz Henrique de Figueiredo and Waldemar Celes. Originally born as a configuration language for Petrobras, evolved from earlier prototypes DEL (data-entry language) and Sol (simple object language). The name is Portuguese for 'moon', a counterpart to Sol meaning 'sun'. Statically linked Lua 5.4 interpreter is roughly 270 KB.
Lua table: Lua's only compound data structure: simultaneously an array, a hash map, a record, an object, and a namespace. The same { ... } syntax expresses all five, and any single table can mix them. The basis of both Lua's data model and its configuration-file format.
PUC-Rio: Pontifical Catholic University of Rio de Janeiro. Brazilian private research university; home of the Tecgraf laboratory where Lua was designed in 1993.
Tecgraf: The Computer Graphics Technology Group at PUC-Rio. Brazilian university research laboratory specialising in scientific software, where Lua was designed in 1993 originally as a configuration language for Petrobras.
Petrobras: Brazilian state-controlled oil and gas company (Petróleo Brasileiro S.A.), founded 1953. The original customer for Sol and DEL, the configuration prototypes that were later merged into Lua at PUC-Rio.
Roberto Ierusalimschy: Brazilian computer scientist, professor at PUC-Rio, co-designer of the Lua programming language since 1993. Principal author of the language reference manual and of the HOPL III history paper.
Luiz Henrique de Figueiredo: Brazilian computer scientist, co-designer of Lua since 1993, originally at PUC-Rio's Tecgraf. Co-author of the Lua reference manual and the implementation.
Waldemar Celes: Brazilian computer scientist, professor at PUC-Rio, co-designer of Lua since 1993 originally at Tecgraf. Co-author of the Lua reference manual and the implementation.
Sol (language): A 'simple object language' developed at PUC-Rio's Tecgraf in 1992 as a configuration format for Petrobras. Merged with DEL in 1993 to form Lua. Sol is Portuguese for 'sun', which prompted the moon-naming pun for Lua.
DEL (language): A 'data-entry language' developed at PUC-Rio's Tecgraf, predecessor of Lua. Merged with Sol in 1993 to form the more general-purpose Lua language.
loader.lua: FreeBSD's boot loader configuration script, located at /boot/lua/loader.lua. Reads /boot/loader.conf, draws the boot menu, decides between kernels, and hands control to the chosen one. Default since FreeBSD 12 (December 2018); replaced the FORTH-based loader entirely in FreeBSD 13 (April 2021).
FORTH: A stack-based programming language created by Charles Moore in the late 1960s. Used in firmware and boot loaders for decades; FreeBSD's earlier boot loader was implemented in FORTH and was replaced by Lua-based loader.lua in FreeBSD 13.
Neovim: A fork of Vim started in 2014. Made Lua a first-class scripting and configuration language from the start; init.lua became the recommended configuration entry point in Neovim 0.5 (July 2021), supplanting Vimscript.
Awesome WM: Awesome Window Manager. A tiling window manager for X11, configured in Lua via rc.lua since version 3.0 in 2008. The window manager is itself a Lua interpreter with X11 bindings.
Wireshark: A free open-source network-protocol analyser. Packet dissectors can be written as Lua scripts, allowing analysts to add protocol decoders without recompiling the C codebase.
OpenResty: An nginx variant with embedded Lua, originally developed by Yichun Zhang at Taobao and later commercialised by OpenResty Inc. The platform behind Cloudflare's edge stack and a substantial fraction of high-traffic web infrastructure.
HAProxy: An open-source TCP/HTTP load balancer originally written by Willy Tarreau in 2000. Has supported Lua scripting since version 1.6 (2015) for custom routing and inspection logic that exceeds what its native configuration grammar can express.
Kong: An open-source API gateway built on top of nginx and OpenResty. Plugins are written as Lua modules; configuration is structured around Lua tables.
Roblox: An online game platform and game-creation system released in 2006. Roblox games are scripted in Luau, a Lua dialect with optional static typing and sandboxing developed by Roblox.
Luau: A typed dialect of Lua developed by Roblox, with optional static typing, performance optimisations, and security sandboxing. The scripting language for Roblox games and tooling.
World of Warcraft: Massively-multiplayer online role-playing game launched by Blizzard Entertainment in November 2004. The entire user-interface customisation system, including all addons and most of the in-game UI, has been scripted in Lua since launch.
pandoc: A document-conversion tool by John MacFarlane, supporting an extensive set of input and output formats. Document filters and custom writers can be authored as Lua scripts.
EVAL (Redis): Redis's server-side scripting command, available since Redis 2.6 (2012). Executes a Lua script atomically against the Redis dataset; widely used for atomic multi-step operations that the standard command set cannot express directly.
Norway problem: A class of YAML configuration bugs in which the country code NO is silently parsed as the boolean false by YAML 1.1 implicit typing, causing Norway to disappear from country lists. Widely cited as evidence that implicit type coercion in configuration formats is a footgun.
YAML: A data-serialisation language designed for human-friendly configuration. Specification 1.1 (2005) introduced extensive implicit type coercion (the source of the Norway problem); 1.2 (2009) narrowed the boolean grammar but is not the default in many widely deployed parsers, including PyYAML's load function.
TOML: Tom's Obvious, Minimal Language. A configuration-file format designed by Tom Preston-Werner. Hierarchical key-value structure with comments and explicit typing; used by Cargo (Rust), pyproject.toml (Python) and many smaller projects.
JSON Schema: A specification for describing the structure of JSON documents, with a mature ecosystem of validators, code generators and IDE integrations. Adopted across many cross-team contracts and OpenAPI tooling.
Teal: A statically typed dialect of Lua, developed by Hisham Muhammad. Adds optional type annotations and a type checker that compiles down to ordinary Lua. One of several Lua-side approaches to schema-style validation.
SQLite: A self-contained SQL database engine stored in a single file. No server process. Handles terabytes with zero configuration.
PostgreSQL: An advanced open-source relational database with MVCC, JSON support, full-text search, and extensibility via custom types and functions.
Tales from the Bare Metal: A Vivian Voss series of forensic, narrative pieces on real production incidents, told to extract architectural lessons rather than to assign blame. Cyberpunk-noir framing, calm tone, named sources.
In the Net: A Vivian Voss series on the documented mechanics of vendor lock-in. Each episode dissects one platform's promise, the lock-in mechanisms wired into the architecture, the documented exits that do not work as advertised, and the realistic escape routes. Forensic tonality, named sources, four dimensions: fiscal, mechanical, market power, contractual stance.
LinkedIn: Professional network founded in 2002 by Reid Hoffman and co-founders. Acquired by Microsoft in 2016. ~1.1 billion registered users in 2026. Regulated as a Very Large Online Platform under the EU Digital Services Act since April 2023; not designated a gatekeeper under the Digital Markets Act despite Microsoft's gatekeeper status.
Reid Hoffman: American entrepreneur and venture capitalist. Co-founder and executive chairman of LinkedIn (2002), partner at Greylock since 2009, board member of OpenAI, Microsoft and other companies. Author of 'The Start-Up of You' and 'Blitzscaling'.
360Brew: LinkedIn's foundation recommendation model, published by Firooz et al. in January 2025 (arXiv:2501.16450). A 150-billion-parameter decoder-only model built on top of Mixtral 8x22, trained on LinkedIn's Economic Graph. Performs more than 30 predictive tasks (feed ranking, job matching, people-you-may-know, advertising) across five-plus surfaces using a single shared architecture.
Economic Graph: LinkedIn's internal data model representing the global workforce: members, companies, jobs, skills, schools, content. The training substrate for 360Brew and most of LinkedIn's machine-learning systems.
Mixtral 8x22: An open-weights mixture-of-experts large language model released by Mistral AI in 2024, with eight 22-billion-parameter experts. The base architecture on top of which LinkedIn's 360Brew is built.
FAIT (LinkedIn): Foundation AI Technologies, the LinkedIn team responsible for the 360Brew foundation model and the broader shift from task-specific to shared-architecture machine-learning systems on the platform.
DMA (Digital Markets Act): EU regulation in force since 2 May 2023, designed to ensure contestable and fair digital markets. Designates 'gatekeepers' (large platforms meeting thresholds of size, user count and market role) and imposes obligations including data portability, interoperability, and constraints on self-preferencing. Six gatekeepers were initially designated in September 2023.
DSA (Digital Services Act): EU regulation governing online platforms' content moderation and user protections, in force since February 2024. Imposes transparency, content-moderation, and risk-management obligations on Very Large Online Platforms (VLOPs) and Very Large Online Search Engines (VLOSEs). Distinct from the DMA: the DSA applies broadly; DMA gatekeeper obligations apply only to designated gatekeepers.
VLOP: Very Large Online Platform under the EU Digital Services Act. A platform with more than 45 million average monthly active users in the EU. Subject to additional obligations on systemic risk assessment, algorithmic transparency for content moderation, and external auditing. LinkedIn was designated a VLOP in April 2023.
Gatekeeper (DMA): Designation under the EU Digital Markets Act for large platforms that meet quantitative and qualitative thresholds. Six gatekeepers were initially designated in September 2023: Apple, Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, ByteDance, and Microsoft for its core platforms (Windows, LinkedIn was not separately designated). Obligations took full effect on 7 March 2024.
Xing: German-language professional network, originally launched in 2003 as openBC and renamed Xing in 2006. ~22.5 million members across Germany, Austria and Switzerland. Operated by New Work SE; revenue declined 12 per cent year-on-year in H1 2024. Took fully private by Burda Digital SE in June 2025.
New Work SE: The German parent company of Xing, listed on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange under the ticker NWO until its delisting in August 2024. Majority shareholder was Burda Digital SE; squeeze-out completed in June 2025 at EUR 105.65 per share.
Burda Digital SE: The digital subsidiary of Hubert Burda Media, a German publishing and media group. Completed the squeeze-out and full private acquisition of New Work SE (Xing) in June 2025.
Open Graph: A protocol introduced by Facebook in 2010 (Open Graph Protocol) that lets web pages declare structured metadata (og:title, og:description, og:image, og:url) so that any social platform sharing the URL can render a consistent preview. The basis on which a personal domain can present itself with controlled metadata across third-party platforms.
Mailcow: A self-hosted, dockerised mail-server stack including Postfix, Dovecot, SOGo, Rspamd and a web administration interface. Common choice for organisations or individuals who want full SMTP control under their own infrastructure.
Listmonk: A self-hosted, high-performance newsletter and mailing-list manager written in Go, with a single-binary deployment and no external dependencies beyond a database. Common choice for self-hosted newsletter publishing.
ActivityPub: A decentralised social-networking protocol (W3C Recommendation, 2018) that powers Mastodon, Friendica, Pleroma and the broader Fediverse. Provides client-to-server and server-to-server APIs for federated content distribution.
AT Protocol: An open networking protocol developed for Bluesky, designed for federated social applications with portable user accounts and content addressing. Released for public use in 2023; intended successor to siloed social platforms.
Bluesky: A federated social-networking platform built on the AT Protocol. Originated as a project sponsored by Twitter in 2019; spun out as an independent benefit corporation in 2021; opened to the public in February 2024.
Mastodon: A federated, ActivityPub-based microblogging platform first released by Eugen Rochko in 2016. Server-instances are operated independently and federate with each other; users can move accounts between instances with their follower graph intact.
Richard van der Blom: Dutch LinkedIn analyst and author of the annual Algorithm Insights Report. The 2026 edition, drawn from approximately 1.3 million posts, is widely cited for measured findings on LinkedIn algorithmic distribution including the 18.8 per cent external-link reach reduction.
Topic Authority: LinkedIn's algorithmic concept of a creator's topical focus, derived from posting history, engagement patterns, and audience overlap. Narrowly focused content distributes more confidently than thematically broad content; the corollary is a structural penalty on diversification of voice.
Not in the Brief: A Vivian Voss series on the documented mechanics of software added to a user's machine without explicit consent. Each episode dissects one product's default behaviour, the awareness path, the security and fingerprinting implications, and where the same pattern lives across competing products. Forensic tonality, named sources, calm tone.
Edsger Dijkstra: Dutch computer scientist (1930-2002), Turing Award 1972. Introduced disciplined layering in 'The Structure of the THE-Multiprogramming System' (CACM, May 1968). Originator of structured programming, the shortest-path algorithm bearing his name, and a long list of essays on programming as a discipline.
NTP: Network Time Protocol. A networking protocol for synchronising clocks across networks of variable latency, designed by David L. Mills at the University of Delaware in 1985. RFC 958 (original), RFC 1305 (v3, 1992), RFC 5905 (v4, 2010). Uses time-stamped multi-source triangulation and a clock-discipline algorithm to steer the local oscillator without abrupt jumps.
doas: Dedicated OpenBSD application subexecutor. A privilege-escalation tool written by Ted Unangst, imported into the OpenBSD CVS tree on 16 July 2015 and shipped as the default in OpenBSD 5.8 in October 2015. Roughly 1,100 lines of C plus a small yacc grammar. One configuration file (/etc/doas.conf), one rule per line, first match wins, default deny.
sudo: The traditional Unix privilege-escalation tool, originally written by Bob Coggeshall and Cliff Spencer at SUNY Buffalo around 1980 and maintained by Todd Miller since 1994. A multi-package codebase in the high tens of thousands of lines including plugin loader, policy engines, environment management, audit hooks, LDAP back-end, SSSD integration, and a yacc grammar for sudoers.
doas.conf: The single configuration file for doas (/etc/doas.conf on OpenBSD, /usr/local/etc/doas.conf on FreeBSD). Grammar fits on a postcard: permit | deny [nopass] [keepenv] [setenv {...}] identity [as user] [cmd command]. One rule per line, first match wins, default deny.
sudoers: The configuration file for sudo (/etc/sudoers and /etc/sudoers.d/). Has its own yacc grammar with Defaults blocks, override semantics, alias declarations, and tag specifications. Edited via visudo to validate before saving.
Ted Unangst: American software engineer and OpenBSD developer. Author of doas (2015), various contributions to OpenBSD's malloc, libc and rc subsystems, and a long-running technical blog at flak.tedunangst.com. Wrote doas because the sudo configuration on OpenBSD had become tiresome.
Todd Miller: American software engineer, maintainer of sudo since 1994. Affiliated with the Courtesan Consulting venture. Has been the principal upstream maintainer of sudo for over three decades.
OpenDoas: A portable fork of OpenBSD's doas, originally maintained by Duncaen, packaged as doas on Debian, Ubuntu, Arch, Alpine, Void and other Linux distributions. Configuration syntax is identical to OpenBSD's. Maintenance cadence is slower than upstream OpenBSD.
sudo-rs: A memory-safe reimplementation of sudo in Rust, originally funded by the Internet Security Research Group's Prossimo project, jointly developed by Tweede Golf and Ferrous Systems from December 2022. First stable release August 2023. Long-term home at the Trifecta Tech Foundation since June 2024. Default in Ubuntu 25.10 (October 2025).
PAM: Pluggable Authentication Modules. A framework for centralising authentication, account management, session setup and password updating across Unix services. Loaded by sudo and many other privileged programmes; not used by doas.
LDAP: Lightweight Directory Access Protocol. A protocol for accessing and maintaining distributed directory information services over IP. Used by sudo via its LDAP back-end to centralise sudoers rules; not used by doas.
SSSD: System Security Services Daemon. A daemon for accessing remote authentication and identity providers (LDAP, Active Directory, Kerberos) on Linux. Integrated with sudo via the SSSD back-end; not used by doas.
TIOCSTI: A Unix terminal ioctl that pushes characters back into the input queue of a tty. Historically a privilege-escalation vector when one process can inject characters into another's terminal. The basis of OpenDoas's CVE-2023-28339.
Baron Samedit: Common name for CVE-2021-3156, a heap-based buffer overflow in sudo's de-escape loop in sudoedit -s. Disclosed by Qualys in January 2021; the bug had been quietly present since commit 8255ed69 in July 2011. Affected default sudo configurations on Linux, BSDs, AIX, Solaris and macOS.
Trifecta Tech Foundation: American non-profit foundation established to host long-term maintenance of memory-safety-critical infrastructure projects. Adopted sudo-rs in June 2024 as one of its founding projects.
Prossimo: A project of the Internet Security Research Group (ISRG, the parent of Let's Encrypt) focused on funding memory-safe rewrites of foundational internet infrastructure. Original sponsor of sudo-rs.
Ferrous Systems: German Rust-focused software-engineering consultancy, co-developer of sudo-rs alongside Tweede Golf.
Tweede Golf: Dutch Rust-focused software-engineering consultancy, co-developer of sudo-rs alongside Ferrous Systems.
wheel group: Traditional Unix administrative group, members of which are permitted (by convention and by the configuration of su, doas or sudo) to escalate privileges to root. The name is a pun on 'big wheel' and dates to the Berkeley Unix tradition.
SNTP: Simple Network Time Protocol. The same wire format as NTP used naively: query one server, accept one answer, set the clock. No multi-source triangulation, no peer weighting, no clock-discipline algorithm. Adequate for desktops and containers; insufficient for servers that need accurate, fault-tolerant time.
ntpd: The reference implementation of NTP, written by David L. Mills and his students at the University of Delaware. Part of the FreeBSD base system since 4.x in 2000; was for thirty years the standard time daemon on Linux. Configuration in /etc/ntp.conf; diagnostic ntpq -p.
chrony: An NTP implementation by Richard Curnow (1997), now developed under Red Hat sponsorship by Miroslav Lichvar. Default on Fedora, RHEL, CentOS Stream, Rocky, Alma, openSUSE and Arch by 2026. Faster convergence than ntpd, supports NTS (RFC 8915), behaves better on virtual machines and intermittent links. Configuration in /etc/chrony.conf or /etc/chrony/chrony.conf; diagnostic chronyc tracking.
systemd-timesyncd: An SNTP client shipped as part of systemd since version 213 (December 2014). Single-source, no triangulation, cannot serve. Default on Ubuntu desktop since 18.04. Configuration in /etc/systemd/timesyncd.conf; ignores /etc/ntp.conf. Status via timedatectl status.
OpenNTPD: A small full-NTP implementation written by Henning Brauer for OpenBSD in 2003. Included in OpenBSD base, available on FreeBSD via the net/openntpd port. Configuration file the size of a postcard; particularly useful in jails because it binds comfortably to specific addresses.
NTS: Network Time Security. RFC 8915 (2020). An authenticated time-transfer protocol that protects NTP queries against tampering without the operational burden of NTP autokey. Supported in chrony and a growing number of NTP servers.
David L. Mills: American computer scientist (1938-2024), professor at the University of Delaware. Designer of the Network Time Protocol (1985) and author of its reference implementation, ntpd. Member of the Internet Architecture Board.
Richard Curnow: British software engineer, original author of chrony (1997), originally written to synchronise laptops with intermittent network connectivity. Stepped back from active development in the 2010s; chrony is now maintained by Miroslav Lichvar at Red Hat.
Miroslav Lichvar: Czech software engineer at Red Hat, current maintainer of chrony. Author of much of chrony's modern feature set including NTS support.
rc.conf: FreeBSD's master service-configuration file at /etc/rc.conf. A flat key-value file controlling which services start at boot, their options, and selected system-wide settings. The single source of truth for service enablement on FreeBSD.
ntpq: The diagnostic command-line client for ntpd. ntpq -p prints the list of configured peers, their measured offsets, and the daemon's current opinion about which peer it is using.
timedatectl: The systemd command-line tool for querying and configuring the system clock and time-zone settings. timedatectl status reports the active time daemon (where applicable), the current synchronisation state, and the configured time zone.
David Parnas: Canadian software engineer and computer scientist. Author of the 1972 paper 'On the Criteria To Be Used in Decomposing Systems into Modules' (CACM), which introduced the principle of Information Hiding: a module should hide what is likely to change behind a stable interface.
Information Hiding: A software-design principle, due to David Parnas (1972), holding that a module should encapsulate the design decisions most likely to change, exposing only a stable interface to the rest of the system. The intellectual basis for layered architecture, modular design, and most subsequent decomposition principles.
Manny Lehman: British computer scientist (1925-2010), formulator of the laws of software evolution. Worked at IBM in the early 1970s; his second law observes that the complexity of an evolving software system increases unless explicit work is done to maintain or reduce it.
Lehman's Laws of Software Evolution: A set of empirical laws describing how long-lived software systems change over time. The second law (1974, refined later): the complexity of a continuously evolving system increases unless explicit work is done to reduce it. Often paraphrased as the second law of thermodynamics applied to code.
Phil Karlton: American software engineer (1947-1997), computer scientist at Carnegie Mellon, Silicon Graphics and Netscape. Credited (around 1970) with the observation, popularised by Tim Bray ~1996, that there are only two hard things in computer science: cache invalidation and naming things.
Tim Bray: Canadian software developer, co-author of XML and Atom, and long-time technology essayist. Popularised Phil Karlton's 'two hard things in computer science' observation in writings around 1996.
Kubernetes Operator: A custom Kubernetes controller, typically packaged with one or more Custom Resource Definitions, that encodes domain-specific operational knowledge for a managed application (database, message broker, certificate manager). A common abstraction layer above raw YAML manifests.
Retry Decorator: A programming-language pattern that wraps a function call in retry logic, typically with backoff and a limit on attempts. Defensive code added to compensate for an upstream service that fails intermittently; once installed, rarely removed even after the underlying fault is fixed.
awk: A pattern-matching and text-processing language created by Alfred Aho, Peter Weinberger and Brian Kernighan at Bell Labs in 1977. The name is an acronym of the authors' surnames. The language has remained substantially unchanged for nearly fifty years and ships in every Unix-like operating system.
Alfred Aho: Canadian computer scientist, co-author of awk (1977), the Dragon Book on compilers, and many influential papers on parsing and string algorithms. Long-time professor at Columbia University; Turing Award 2020 (with Jeffrey Ullman).
Peter Weinberger: American computer scientist, co-author of awk at Bell Labs (the W in awk). Subsequent work on databases and large-scale systems at Renaissance Technologies and Google.
Brian Kernighan: Canadian computer scientist, co-author of awk (the K), co-author of The C Programming Language with Dennis Ritchie, and a long list of formative Unix tools. Long-time Bell Labs researcher, currently at Princeton.
Bell Labs: American industrial research laboratory, originally part of AT&T, where Unix, C, awk, and a long list of other foundational technologies were developed in the 1970s.
THE Operating System: An early multiprogramming operating system designed by Edsger Dijkstra and his team at Eindhoven University of Technology, published in CACM in 1968. The first published example of strictly layered system design, with each layer providing a defined interface to the layer above.
Gemini Nano: Google's on-device foundation language model, deployed inside Chrome and other Google products. A multi-gigabyte model that runs locally without sending inference to the cloud; the first widely deployed on-device LLM in a mainstream browser.
Chrome Built-in AI: Chrome's set of seven on-device AI APIs (LanguageModel, Summarizer, Translator, Writer, Rewriter, Proofreader, LanguageDetector), all backed by Gemini Nano. Generally available for extensions since Chrome 138 (May 2025); Origin Trial for ordinary web pages. No permission prompt at any point.
Prompt API: Chrome's free-form prompt API for the on-device LanguageModel. Available as LanguageModel.create() and session.prompt() in JavaScript without a user permission dialog.
LanguageModel.availability(): A capability probe in Chrome's Prompt API returning 'available', 'downloading' or 'unavailable'. Exposes the visitor's hardware class to any caller without a permission prompt; a fingerprinting input.
Origin Trial: A Chrome programme that lets developers test an experimental web platform feature on production sites by registering an origin and embedding a token. Does not require user consent at the page level.
Google Chrome: Google's web browser, market leader by usage share, the reference implementation for many web platform features. Ships Built-in AI, Privacy Sandbox replacements, and a long list of in-flight standards.
Microsoft Edge: Microsoft's Chromium-based browser. Ships a Copilot Sidebar that is enabled by default for many users (toggle at edge://settings/copilot). Default browser on Windows 11 and the mandated browser in many corporate, healthcare and government estates. Disclosed in April 2026: Edge's password manager loads the entire saved-credential vault into the msedge.exe process and decrypts it eagerly at launch, holding cleartext credentials in process memory for the entire session, regardless of whether any of the corresponding sites is visited. Microsoft characterises the behaviour as 'by design'. Of the major Chromium browsers (Chrome, Brave, Opera, Vivaldi), Edge is the only one with this architecture.
Brave: Privacy-focused Chromium-based browser by Brave Software. Built-in ad and tracker blocking; built-in Leo AI assistant with a cloud-mode toggle in Settings → Leo.
Firefox: Mozilla's open-source browser, the principal non-Chromium engine. Ships an AI Chat sidebar with a configurable provider in about:preferences#general → AI Chatbot.
Arc: A browser by The Browser Company (formerly independent), now part of Atlassian. Ships AI features in Settings → AI.
Safari: Apple's browser, default on macOS, iOS and iPadOS. Integrates Apple Intelligence on supported versions, configured under System Settings → Apple Intelligence.
Apple Intelligence: Apple's on-device generative AI integration shipped with macOS Sequoia and iOS 18 (autumn 2024) and later. Composed of multiple models running locally on supported Apple silicon, with optional Private Cloud Compute fallback.
Privacy Sandbox: Google's umbrella initiative for replacing third-party cookies with privacy-preserving alternatives (Topics API, Protected Audience, Attribution Reporting). Discontinued in April 2025 without delivering the cookie deprecation it was meant to enable.
Browser fingerprinting: The technique of identifying a browser (and the user behind it) by combining many small signals (canvas rendering quirks, available fonts, audio fingerprints, language, GPU, time zone, capability probes) into a high-entropy identifier. In production use across roughly 13 per cent of the top 20,000 websites by 2025 ACM study estimates.
Indirect Prompt Injection: An attack class against LLM-based features in which adversarial instructions are smuggled into model context via the content the model is asked to process (a webpage, a document, an email). OWASP found indirect prompt injection in 73 per cent of audited production AI deployments in 2024.
OWASP: Open Worldwide Application Security Project. A non-profit foundation publishing community-maintained security guidance, including the OWASP Top Ten and the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications.
User Alignment Critic: A separate AI model Google deployed in Chrome to watch the agentic Gemini sidebar and flag actions that diverge from the user's stated intent. Part of a five-layer defence against prompt injection in agentic browsing.
CVE-2026-0628: A Chrome vulnerability disclosed in early January 2026 and fixed by Google in mid-January, allowing extensions with basic permissions to hijack the Gemini Live panel and inherit camera, microphone and file-access privileges through the panel's surface.
Microsoft Copilot: Microsoft's brand for its generative AI assistants across Windows, Edge, Microsoft 365 and other products. The Edge Copilot Sidebar is enabled by default for many users.
Brave Leo: Brave Software's built-in AI assistant in the Brave Browser. Toggle in Settings → Leo, with a separate option for cloud-mode requests.
ChromeOS: Google's Linux-based operating system for Chromebook devices. Ships Chrome as its primary user surface; ChromeOS Plus is the configuration in which Chrome's Built-in AI features are eligible.
Adobe: Adobe Inc. American multinational software company founded in 1982, dominant vendor in design, photography, print and video tooling. Holds over eighty per cent of the creative-software market by industry-aggregator estimates (2024).
Adobe Creative Suite: Adobe's traditional perpetual-licence software bundle, sold from 2003 to 2012. The final release (CS6, May 2012) is the last perpetual Adobe Creative product. Replaced by Creative Cloud subscription in May 2013.
Adobe Creative Cloud: Adobe's subscription-only successor to Creative Suite, launched May 2013. Bundles Photoshop, Illustrator, InDesign, Premiere, Lightroom and others, with synchronisation through Creative Cloud Libraries and Adobe Fonts. Annual plans carry an Early Termination Fee equal to 50 per cent of the remaining contract.
Photoshop: Adobe's raster graphics editor. First released in 1988, holds approximately 42 per cent of the graphic-design segment by industry estimates. The PSD file format is the de-facto industry standard for layered raster work.
Illustrator: Adobe's vector graphics editor. First released in 1987, holds approximately 12 per cent of the graphic-design segment. The AI file format is the de-facto industry standard for editable vector work.
InDesign: Adobe's desktop-publishing application. First released in 1999, holds approximately 26 per cent of the graphic-design segment. The INDD file format has effectively no portable equivalent; IDML exists as an interchange format but loses live links, master-page state, and complex layout work.
Premiere Pro: Adobe's video editing application. First released as Adobe Premiere in 1991, rebranded to Premiere Pro in 2003.
Lightroom: Adobe's photo-management and raw-processing application. Cloud-synchronised with Lightroom Cloud, locally installable as Lightroom Classic.
PSD: Photoshop Document. Adobe's proprietary native file format for Photoshop. Carries Smart Objects, Layer Effects, Adjustment Layers, and a depth of state that no open format mirrors completely.
AI (file format): Adobe Illustrator's proprietary native file format. Carries Illustrator's specific path semantics and is the standard exchange format for editable vector artwork in design.
INDD: Adobe InDesign's proprietary native file format. Effectively no portable equivalent exists. IDML serves as an interchange format but loses live links, master-page state, and complex layout work.
IDML: InDesign Markup Language. Adobe's interchange XML format for InDesign documents, defined in the IDML Specification. Lossy: live links, master-page state, and complex layout work do not survive the round trip.
Smart Objects: A Photoshop layer feature that wraps a layer in a non-destructive container, preserving its source data and allowing transformations and filters to be re-edited. Smart Objects flatten on export to non-PSD formats.
Layer Effects: Photoshop's parameterised non-destructive layer styling (drop shadows, strokes, glows, bevels). Stored in PSD as parameters; bake into pixels when exported to most other formats.
Adjustment Layers: Photoshop's non-destructive image-adjustment layers (curves, levels, hue/saturation, etc.). Stored in PSD as referenced operations; bake into the underlying pixels on flattened export.
Creative Cloud Libraries: Adobe's synchronised asset system (fonts, brushes, colour palettes, shared assets) that bridges the Creative Cloud applications. Local availability of assets depends on an active subscription; cancellation breaks the sync.
Adobe Fonts: Adobe's licensed-font service, formerly Typekit. Fonts are activated for the duration of an active Creative Cloud subscription; cancellation removes access to the activated fonts.
Early Termination Fee: A contractual fee charged when a customer cancels a service before the agreed term ends. Adobe's annual plan (billed monthly) charges 50 per cent of the remaining months on cancellation. Central to the FTC v. Adobe complaint of June 2024.
ROSCA: Restore Online Shoppers' Confidence Act (US, 2010). Federal law regulating online subscription billing and cancellation. Cited in the June 2024 DOJ/FTC complaint against Adobe alleging deceptive cancellation practices and hidden Early Termination Fees.
FTC v. Adobe (2024): United States Department of Justice complaint filed in June 2024 on behalf of the Federal Trade Commission, alleging that Adobe hid Early Termination Fees behind layered cancellation flows and violated the Restore Online Shoppers' Confidence Act. As of 2026, still in motion.
FTC: Federal Trade Commission. The US federal agency responsible for consumer protection and antitrust enforcement.
Canva: Australian online graphic-design platform founded in 2013. Acquired Serif (the maker of the Affinity suite) in March 2024 for approximately 300 million pounds.
Affinity: Professional design suite by Serif, originally three separate applications (Photo, Designer, Publisher) with perpetual licences. Acquired by Canva in March 2024; consolidated to a single application called Affinity by Canva, version 3.0, in October 2025; new model is free with a Canva account, with AI features behind Canva Pro.
Pixelmator: Lithuanian software company that produced the Pixelmator Pro raster image editor for macOS. Acquired by Apple, announced November 2024, completed February 2025.
Pixelmator Pro: Professional raster image editor for macOS, formerly by Pixelmator, now an Apple property. Standalone perpetual licence still listed on the Mac App Store; new features increasingly tied to the Apple Creator Studio subscription bundle.
Apple Creator Studio: Apple's subscription bundle (announced 2026), priced at 12.99 USD per month or 129 USD per year, including Pixelmator Pro, Final Cut Pro, Logic Pro and the iWork suite. Some Pixelmator Pro features are exclusive to the bundle.
DaVinci Resolve: Professional video editing, colour grading, visual effects and audio post-production suite by Blackmagic Design. Free version highly capable; Studio version available as a one-time perpetual purchase. Industry-standard for colour grading.
Blackmagic Design: Australian manufacturer of broadcast and post-production hardware and software. Maker of DaVinci Resolve and the Blackmagic line of cameras.
Krita: Free and open source professional digital painting application, maintained by the KDE community. Strong brush engine, deep tablet support, scriptable.
GIMP: GNU Image Manipulation Program. Free and open source raster image editor, first released in 1996. Robust and feature-complete; UX is dated and migration cost from Adobe is non-trivial.
Inkscape: Free and open source vector graphics editor using SVG as its native format. Robust and feature-complete; UX is dated.
Capture One: Professional raw-image processing application by Capture One A/S (formerly Phase One Software). Direct alternative to Adobe Lightroom. Individual perpetual licences still offered; multi-user studios moved to subscription-only in 2024 with a reported 344 per cent multi-user pricing change.
Vendor lock-in: An architecture or contractual arrangement that makes leaving a vendor disproportionately costly compared to staying. May be technical (proprietary file formats, closed APIs), economic (cancellation fees, sunk training cost) or market-structural (de-facto industry standards held by one vendor).
Quasi-Monopoly: A market position short of legal monopoly in which one vendor holds enough share to set the industry's de-facto standards (file formats, exchange protocols, conventions). The lock-in extends past the direct customer to the customer's customers.
GitLab: A web-based DevOps platform offering Git repository hosting, CI/CD pipelines, issue tracking, and related tooling. Hosted at gitlab.com and as self-managed software. Notable for the publicly documented 31 January 2017 database incident on which Tales from the Bare Metal Episode 01 is based.
GitLab Database Incident (31 January 201: An rm -rf executed on the wrong host removed approximately 300 GB of GitLab.com production data at 23:30 UTC on 31 January 2017. Eighteen hours of recovery, live-streamed on YouTube. Five backup mechanisms had silently failed in different ways. The published postmortem remains one of the most cited operational documents in software engineering.
Amazon S3: Amazon Simple Storage Service. AWS's foundational object-storage product, launched in March 2006. Stores objects in regions; objects are addressed by bucket and key; the index and placement subsystems manage metadata and new-object allocation respectively.
S3 US-EAST-1 outage (28 February 2017): A four-hour-and-seventeen-minute service disruption in Amazon S3's North Virginia region triggered at 09:37 PST when a routine capacity-removal command in the billing subsystem received a mistyped input and removed servers from the index and placement subsystems too. The AWS Service Health Dashboard could not update for the first two hours because its console depended on S3 in US-EAST-1.
US-EAST-1: AWS's Northern Virginia region, the oldest and largest of AWS's regions. Hosts a disproportionate share of foundational AWS services and customer workloads; an outage in US-EAST-1 typically affects a wide swath of the public web.
S3 index subsystem: The internal AWS layer that manages the metadata and location information of all S3 objects in a region. Consulted on every GET, LIST, PUT and DELETE request before any byte of object data is touched. After the 2017 incident, partitioned into smaller cells to limit the blast radius of capacity-removal mistakes.
S3 placement subsystem: The internal AWS layer that allocates storage for new S3 objects. Depends on the index subsystem to function; if the index is below capacity, placement is also unavailable.
AWS Service Health Dashboard: AWS's public service-status page (now under health.aws.amazon.com). Historically depended on S3 in US-EAST-1, which made it unable to communicate during the 2017 S3 outage. Subsequently rebuilt to span multiple AWS regions.
An American cyber-risk modelling firm acquired by Guidewire Software in 2017. Estimated the financial impact of the 28 February 2017 AWS S3 outage at approximately $150M for S&P 500 companies, with 54 of the top 100 retailers seeing 20+ per cent slowdowns.
Facebook BGP Outage (4 October 2021): A six-hour outage of Facebook, Instagram and WhatsApp triggered when a maintenance command withdrew Facebook's BGP routes from the public internet. The internal tooling needed to recover the network depended on the network being recoverable from outside; engineers had to be physically dispatched to data centres. The architectural shape (control surface sharing fate with data plane) is the same as the 2017 AWS S3 dashboard problem.
BGP: Border Gateway Protocol. The protocol by which autonomous systems on the internet announce IP prefixes and exchange routing information. The protocol that decides where on the public internet a given IP address can be reached.
Game Day: A periodic operational drill in which a recovery procedure is exercised against a meaningful fraction of production load, typically with controlled fault injection. Surfaces the unspoken integrity checks and runbook gaps that accumulate when a procedure has not been rehearsed at scale.
Cellularisation: An architectural pattern in which a large system is partitioned into many small, independently failable units (cells), so that a fault or operational mistake against one cell does not cascade across the whole system. The same pattern as bulkheads in shipbuilding and as containers and jails in operating systems.
Bulkhead pattern: An architectural pattern, named after the watertight compartments in ship hulls, in which independent failure domains are kept structurally separated so that flooding in one compartment does not sink the vessel. In software, expressed as cells, shards, jails or microservices, depending on the granularity.
kubectl: The command-line client for Kubernetes. Accepts commands such as kubectl delete, kubectl scale, kubectl apply against a configured context and namespace. Like the AWS S3 capacity tool, kubectl does what its arguments say, including against the wrong context if that is what was selected.
pg_dump: PostgreSQL's logical-backup utility. Produces a SQL or custom-format dump of a database; widely used for off-site backups. Silently produces zero-byte output if invoked against a server with a higher major version than the binary, a behaviour central to the GitLab 2017 incident.
WAL archiving: PostgreSQL's continuous-archiving mode for write-ahead log segments. Enables point-in-time recovery to any moment within the archive window. Must be configured explicitly via archive_command and an off-site target; absent by default.
pgpool-II: A connection-pooling and replication-management proxy for PostgreSQL. The staging configuration of pgpool-II was the unrelated work that produced GitLab's only operational backup on 31 January 2017.
LVM: Logical Volume Manager. The Linux subsystem that abstracts physical block devices into logical volumes, supporting resizing, snapshots, and online reconfiguration. LVM snapshots are commonly used for crash-consistent point-in-time copies of database storage.
DMARC: Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance. An email-authentication protocol that tells receiving servers what to do with messages failing SPF or DKIM checks. A misconfigured DMARC policy can cause legitimate alert emails to be silently rejected, as happened in the GitLab 2017 incident.
Postmortem: A structured retrospective of an operational incident, focused on root causes, contributing factors, and corrective actions rather than on individual blame. The GitLab 2017 postmortem is widely studied as a model of the form.
AWS RDS: Amazon Relational Database Service. AWS's managed-database offering, supporting MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, and SQL Server with automated snapshots, point-in-time recovery, and read replicas.
Cloud SQL: Google Cloud Platform's managed-database service, supporting MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server with automated backups, replication, and point-in-time recovery.
Azure Database for PostgreSQL: Microsoft Azure's managed PostgreSQL offering, with automated backups and point-in-time restore. Distinct from raw VM-hosted PostgreSQL on Azure, which is the configuration GitLab.com used in 2017.
Backblaze B2: A low-cost cloud object-storage service from Backblaze, S3-compatible at the API level, frequently used for backup and archival workloads.
Cloudflare R2: Cloudflare's S3-compatible object-storage service, notable for charging zero egress fees, which makes large restore-test operations economically practical.
nginx: A high-performance HTTP server and reverse proxy using an event-driven, non-blocking architecture. Serves static files and proxies upstream applications.
Webpack: A JavaScript module bundler that resolves dependency graphs and outputs optimised bundles. Largely replaced by Vite and native ES Modules.
npm: Node Package Manager. The default registry and CLI for JavaScript packages. Manages dependency trees in node_modules.
Scrum: An agile framework using fixed-length Sprints, daily stand-ups, and defined roles (Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team).
Event Sourcing: A pattern that stores every state change as an immutable event rather than overwriting current state. The event log becomes the source of truth.
CQRS: Command Query Responsibility Segregation. Separates read models from write models, allowing independent optimisation of each path.
Microservices: An architecture that decomposes an application into small, independently deployable services communicating over network protocols.
Monolith: A single deployable unit containing all application logic. Often contrasted with microservices but not inherently inferior.
TypeScript: A typed superset of JavaScript that compiles to plain JS. Adds static type checking at build time.
Sass: A CSS preprocessor adding variables, nesting, and mixins. Largely obsoleted by native CSS custom properties, nesting, and @layer.
CSS-in-JS: A pattern where CSS is authored inside JavaScript files, generating styles at runtime. Adds bundle weight and runtime overhead.
Serverless: A cloud execution model where the provider allocates resources per request. You write functions, the vendor manages infrastructure.
Unix Philosophy: Design principles favouring small, composable programs that do one thing well, connected via text streams.
Demoscene: A computer art subculture producing real-time audiovisual presentations (demos) within extreme size constraints, often 64 KB or less.
CAST: The templating language used by CASTD. Five delimiter pairs for variables, conditions, slots, comments, and expressions.
light-dark(): A CSS function that returns one of two values depending on the computed color-scheme. Eliminates media-query duplication for dark mode.
Capsicum: A capability-based security framework in FreeBSD. One syscall (cap_enter) permanently removes all access to global namespaces. The process keeps only its existing file descriptors, each restricted via cap_rights_limit(). Irreversible. Compiled into FreeBSD 10.0 by default since 2014.
Buchpreisbindungsgesetz: The German Book Price Binding Act (BuchPrG). Obliges every commercial seller in Germany, including the publishing house in its own direct sales, to charge the single end price set by the publisher for a given book. §3 binds retailers, §5 obliges the publisher to set a price including VAT, §7 limits ancillary benefits to goods of small value.
BuchPrG: Short form of the German Buchpreisbindungsgesetz, the Book Price Binding Act. Fixes the retail price of every German book across all commercial channels.
ISBN: International Standard Book Number. A 13-digit identifier assigned to a specific edition of a book (hardcover, paperback, PDF, ePub each carry their own). Registered through the national ISBN agency, in Germany via MVB.
KDP: Kindle Direct Publishing. Amazon's self-publishing platform. Returns 60 per cent of list price as royalty above the per-region pricing threshold and 50 per cent below it, less printing costs.
World Book Day: UNESCO World Book and Copyright Day, observed on 23 April. Marks the death of Cervantes, Shakespeare and Inca Garcilaso de la Vega in 1616 (by different calendars) and is a common launch date for new titles.
One-Stop-Shop: An EU VAT scheme (OSS) allowing a publisher or merchant in one member state to remit VAT for digital sales to all twenty-six other member states through a single quarterly return at home, instead of registering for VAT in each destination country.
OSS: Short form of the EU One-Stop-Shop VAT scheme. Registration becomes effective on the first day of the calendar quarter following the application, regardless of when within the previous quarter it was filed.
Bundeszentralamt für Steuern: The German Federal Central Tax Office (BZSt). Administers OSS registrations, withholding tax certificates, and other cross-border tax arrangements at federal level.
BZSt: Short form of the Bundeszentralamt für Steuern, the German Federal Central Tax Office.
Stripe: An online payment processor founded by Patrick and John Collison in 2010. Supports roughly 50 countries as merchant locations, accepts customer payments worldwide in principle, with local payment methods (SEPA, iDEAL, Bancontact, BLIK, Klarna, OXXO, FPX) configured per market.
SEPA: Single Euro Payments Area. The EU framework for euro-denominated bank transfers and direct debits, treating cross-border euro payments the same as domestic ones.
iDEAL: A Dutch online banking payment method. The dominant e-commerce payment rail in the Netherlands.
Bancontact: The dominant Belgian debit-card payment scheme, also widely used for online purchases in Belgium.
Klarna: A Swedish payment provider offering buy-now-pay-later, instalments, and direct bank payments. Common across the Nordic countries and parts of Europe.
Vossscher Verlag: Vivian Voss's self-publishing imprint. First title: Integrated by Design, a 371-page book on FreeBSD. Direct shop at vossscher-verlag.com.
Vosges du Nord: The northern range of the Vosges mountains in Alsace, France. A UNESCO biosphere reserve of low wooded hills, brooks and small valleys.
pf: Packet Filter. The firewall in FreeBSD (and OpenBSD). Stateful, with anchors, tables, and a concise rule syntax. Written by Daniel Hartmeier in 2001 to replace IPFilter, shipped in OpenBSD 3.0 on 1 December 2001 and now the default firewall across the BSD family and macOS.
iptables: The packet classification framework that replaced ipchains in Linux 2.4 (2001), written by Rusty Russell. Imperative tuple-of-flags grammar with chains (INPUT, OUTPUT, FORWARD, PREROUTING, POSTROUTING) and tables (filter, nat, mangle, raw, security). Being superseded by nftables since 2014.
nftables: The Linux packet classification framework replacing iptables. Uses a unified syntax for filtering, NAT, and mangling. Released 19 January 2014, borrows pf's design philosophy of a single grammar for all packet operations.
ipchains: The Linux 2.2 packet filter (1999), successor to ipfwadm and predecessor to iptables. Replaced by iptables in Linux 2.4.
IPFilter: A BSD-world packet filter written by Darren Reed since the mid-1990s. Removed from OpenBSD on 30 May 2001 over a licensing dispute, triggering the creation of pf.
conntrack: The Linux kernel module that tracks the state of network connections. A separate component from iptables, requiring its own configuration. In pf, equivalent state tracking is built into the rule grammar via the keep state keyword.
Drawbridge: An Ethernet-layer packet filter from Texas A&M University that Daniel Hartmeier studied as a starting point when writing pf. A single C module with a small kernel interface.
pf.conf: The configuration file for pf. Plain-text grammar of rules, read top to bottom, last match wins unless quick short-circuits. A pf.conf written in 2003 still mostly parses on a current OpenBSD machine.
pfSense: An open-source firewall and router distribution based on FreeBSD and pf. Web UI for rule configuration, widely deployed in enterprises and homelabs.
OPNsense: An open-source firewall distribution forked from pfSense in 2014. Based on FreeBSD and pf, with an updated UI and release cadence.
CARP: Common Address Redundancy Protocol. The OpenBSD high-availability protocol that lets multiple machines share an IP address for failover. Integrates with pf for stateful firewall pairs.
DragonFly BSD: A BSD-derived operating system forked from FreeBSD 4.x in 2003 by Matthew Dillon. Known for the HAMMER and HAMMER2 filesystems. Adopted pf in version 1.1.
NetBSD: A BSD-derived operating system known for portability across architectures. Adopted pf in NetBSD 3.0 (2005).
macOS Lion: macOS 10.7, released July 2011. The first macOS release to include pf, replacing the older ipfw firewall.
Daniel Hartmeier: Swiss software developer who wrote pf for OpenBSD in 2001, twenty-five days after the IPFilter licensing dispute, despite having never written kernel code. Author of the original pf engine and rule grammar.
Henning Brauer: OpenBSD developer and primary later maintainer of pf. Redesigned the rule evaluation engine and contributed extensive grammar extensions. Also lead developer of OpenBGPD.
Ryan McBride: OpenBSD developer and primary later maintainer of pf, particularly the queueing and CARP integration work.
Darren Reed: Author of IPFilter, the BSD-world packet filter from the mid-1990s. The licensing dispute he raised with the OpenBSD project in May 2001 directly triggered the creation of pf.
Theo de Raadt: Co-founder and leader of the OpenBSD project. Removed IPFilter from the OpenBSD CVS tree on 30 May 2001 over the licensing dispute with Darren Reed, prompting Hartmeier's pf rewrite.
Rusty Russell: Australian Linux kernel developer, author of iptables and ipchains. Also co-author of the Linux netfilter framework.
Redis: An in-memory data structure store used as database, cache, and message broker. Single-threaded, sub-millisecond latency.
SSH: Secure Shell. A cryptographic protocol for secure remote login, command execution, and tunnelling over untrusted networks.
TLS: Transport Layer Security. The cryptographic protocol securing HTTPS, email, and other network traffic. Successor to SSL.
HTTP/2: The second major version of HTTP. Binary framing, multiplexed streams over a single TCP connection, header compression.
HTTP/3: HTTP over QUIC. Replaces TCP with UDP-based transport, eliminating head-of-line blocking. Built-in encryption.
ACME: Automatic Certificate Management Environment. The protocol Let's Encrypt uses to issue and renew TLS certificates without manual intervention.
IPsec: Internet Protocol Security. A suite of protocols for authenticating and encrypting IP packets. Complex configuration, largely replaced by WireGuard for VPNs.
OpenVPN: An open-source VPN solution using SSL/TLS for key exchange. Flexible but verbose configuration compared to WireGuard.
AWS: Amazon Web Services. The dominant cloud computing platform offering IaaS, PaaS, and managed services.
systemd: The init system and service manager in most Linux distributions. Manages processes, logging, networking, and device events. Controversial for its scope.
newsyslog: FreeBSD's built-in log rotation daemon. One line in newsyslog.conf replaces entire logrotate configurations. Runs hourly via cron. Part of the base system since 4.4BSD.
journalctl: The log viewer for systemd's journal. Structured logging with native filtering by priority, time range, and service. Built into every systemd-based Linux since 2012.
logrotate: Linux's log rotation utility. Configured via separate config blocks per log file. Typically 7 lines for what FreeBSD's newsyslog does in one.
cron: The Unix job scheduler. Executes commands at specified times using a five-field time expression (minute, hour, day, month, weekday).
rsync: A fast, incremental file transfer utility. Transfers only the differences between source and destination using a rolling checksum algorithm.
tmux: A terminal multiplexer. Runs multiple shell sessions in one terminal, supports detaching and reattaching, split panes, and session persistence.
POSIX: Portable Operating System Interface. A family of IEEE standards defining Unix-compatible APIs, shell behaviour, and utility conventions.
ACID: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability. The four guarantees a database transaction must provide for reliability.
MVCC: Multi-Version Concurrency Control. A database technique where readers see a consistent snapshot without blocking writers. Used in PostgreSQL.
GraphQL: A query language for APIs where the client specifies exactly which fields it needs. Developed by Facebook, alternative to REST.
gRPC: A high-performance RPC framework using Protocol Buffers and HTTP/2. Supports streaming, code generation, and strong typing.
Vite: A frontend build tool using native ES Modules for instant dev server startup and Rollup for production builds. Replaces Webpack.
Bun: An all-in-one JavaScript runtime, bundler, transpiler, and package manager. Written in Zig, designed for speed.
Deno: A secure JavaScript/TypeScript runtime by the creator of Node.js. Built-in TypeScript support, URL imports, and sandboxed permissions.
CNCF: Cloud Native Computing Foundation. The Linux Foundation project hosting Kubernetes, Prometheus, Envoy, and other cloud-native infrastructure projects.
PyCon: The annual Python community conference. Held in multiple countries, featuring talks, sprints, and community gatherings.
KubeCon: The Cloud Native Computing Foundation's flagship conference for Kubernetes and cloud-native technologies.
FOSDEM: Free and Open Source Developers' European Meeting. Annual two-day conference in Brussels, free to attend, no registration.
GDPR: General Data Protection Regulation. EU law governing the collection, processing, and storage of personal data. Effective since 2018.
WebAssembly: A binary instruction format for a stack-based virtual machine. Runs near-native speed in browsers alongside JavaScript.
HAProxy: A high-performance TCP/HTTP load balancer and reverse proxy. Event-driven, widely used for traffic distribution.
Terraform: An infrastructure-as-code tool by HashiCorp. Declares cloud resources in HCL files and manages their lifecycle. Relicensed from MPL to BSL in August 2023. Acquired by IBM in February 2025.
HCL: HashiCorp Configuration Language. A declarative configuration format used by Terraform. Not a programming language: no conditionals, no loops, no first-class functions.
HashiCorp: Software company founded in 2012. Created Terraform, Vault, Consul, and Nomad. Switched Terraform from MPL to BSL in 2023. Acquired by IBM for $6.4 billion in 2025.
IaC: Infrastructure as Code. Managing and provisioning infrastructure through machine-readable configuration files rather than manual processes or interactive tools.
Pulumi: An infrastructure-as-code tool that uses general-purpose programming languages (TypeScript, Python, Go) instead of a proprietary DSL. Real conditionals, real loops, real functions.
MPL: Mozilla Public Licence. A permissive open-source licence that allows proprietary use while requiring modifications to MPL-licensed files to remain open. Terraform's original licence before the BSL switch.
Ansible: An agentless automation tool for configuration management, application deployment, and orchestration. Uses YAML playbooks over SSH. No state file, no drift by design.
Prometheus: A time-series monitoring system with a pull-based model, PromQL query language, and built-in alerting. At one million time series: 3 GB RAM. Scraping increases Kubelet CPU by 4x.
Grafana: A visualisation platform for metrics, logs, and traces. Connects to Prometheus, Loki, InfluxDB, and dozens of other data sources.
OpenTelemetry: A vendor-neutral observability framework for generating, collecting, and exporting telemetry data (traces, metrics, logs). The Collector baseline: 2 CPU cores and 2 GB RAM at 20,000 spans per second.
Datadog: A cloud monitoring and observability platform. Charges per host, per GB ingested, per custom metric. Vendor lock-in documented by Evereve (90% cost reduction on exit) and Deductive AI (access revoked overnight).
syslog: The standard logging protocol for Unix systems, written by Eric Allman in 1983. Predates every commercial observability vendor by decades.
Loki: A log aggregation system by Grafana Labs. Indexes only metadata, not full text. Designed to be cost-effective and integrate with Grafana.
Eric Allman: Author of sendmail and syslog. Wrote the Unix logging system in 1983 that remains the foundation of system logging on every Unix and Linux machine.
Alan Turing: Mathematician who formalised the concept of computation with the Turing machine in 1936. Settled the Entscheidungsproblem. The theoretical foundation of every computer.
Alonzo Church: Mathematician who invented lambda calculus in 1932. The formal basis for functional programming, from Lisp to Haskell to every arrow function in JavaScript.
John Backus: Creator of FORTRAN (1957) and BNF notation. His 1977 Turing Award lecture asked: 'Can Programming Be Liberated from the von Neumann Style?'
John McCarthy: Creator of Lisp (1958), built on Church's lambda calculus. Coined the term 'artificial intelligence'. Lisp introduced garbage collection, recursion, and homoiconicity.
Tony Hoare: Computer scientist who introduced formal verification and the null reference, which he later called 'my billion-dollar mistake'. Also invented Quicksort.
Edsger Dijkstra: Pioneer of structured programming. Proved programs could be treated as mathematical proofs (1976). Authored 'Go To Statement Considered Harmful'.
Grace Hopper: Designed COBOL to read like English. Pioneered the idea that programming languages should be human-readable, not mathematical notation. Rear Admiral, US Navy.
BNF: Backus-Naur Form. A formal notation for describing the syntax of programming languages, invented by John Backus in 1959. Every language grammar since has used it or a descendant.
Haskell: A purely functional programming language based on lambda calculus and category theory. Monads, lazy evaluation, strong static types. Market share: 0.1%. Exquisite. Unused.
COBOL: COmmon Business-Oriented Language, designed by Grace Hopper in 1959. Still processes 95% of ATM transactions. Reads like English by design.
Linus Torvalds: Creator and principal developer of the Linux kernel. Also wrote Git. Maintains kernel development from Portland, Oregon.
Martin Fowler: Software author and Chief Scientist at ThoughtWorks. Coined 'Microservices' with James Lewis. Wrote Refactoring, Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture, and the 'Monolith First' essay.
Rich Harris: Creator of Svelte and Rollup. Former graphics editor at The New York Times. Demonstrated that the Virtual DOM is 'pure overhead'.
David Heinemeier Hansson: Creator of Ruby on Rails. Co-founder of Basecamp and HEY. Now advocates software ownership over SaaS subscriptions with ONCE.
Greg Kroah-Hartman: Senior Linux kernel maintainer responsible for stable releases. In 2024, removed Russian-affiliated maintainers from the MAINTAINERS file on compliance grounds.
Greg Young: Popularised Event Sourcing and CQRS as architectural patterns. Author of Versioning in an Event Sourced System.
Ken Schwaber: Co-creator of Scrum with Jeff Sutherland. Founded the Scrum Alliance, later Scrum.org. Admitted that 75 per cent of organisations using Scrum will not succeed.
Ron Jeffries: Co-creator of Extreme Programming. Agile Manifesto signatory. In 2018, wrote 'Developers Should Abandon Agile' — a rare recantation from an original author.
Dave Thomas: Agile Manifesto signatory. Co-author of The Pragmatic Programmer. Declared 'Agile is Dead' in 2014, arguing the word had been hijacked by consultancies.
James Lewis: Software architect at ThoughtWorks. Co-coined the term 'Microservices' with Martin Fowler in their 2014 article defining the architectural style.
Rich Felker: Author of musl libc, a clean-room lightweight C standard library for Linux. Vocal advocate for POSIX correctness and minimal PID 1 design.
Lennart Poettering: Creator of systemd and PulseAudio. His replacement of SysVinit with a monolithic init system remains one of the most divisive decisions in Linux history.
Copy-on-Write: A storage strategy where data is never overwritten in place. New data is written to a new location, then the pointer is updated. Guarantees consistency after power failure.
Merkle tree: A hash tree where every leaf node is labelled with the hash of a data block and every non-leaf node is labelled with the hash of its children. Used by ZFS for end-to-end checksumming.
DKMS: Dynamic Kernel Module Support. A framework for building kernel modules that automatically recompile when the kernel is updated. Used by ZFS on Linux.
CDDL: Common Development and Distribution Licence. Sun Microsystems' open-source licence, intentionally incompatible with the GPL. The licence under which ZFS was released.
Jeff Bonwick: Engineer at Sun Microsystems who designed the Slab Allocator and co-created ZFS with Matthew Ahrens. Built the filesystem that trusts mathematics over hardware.
Salvatore Sanfilippo: Sicilian developer known as antirez. Created Redis in 2009, a single-threaded in-memory data structure server. Maintained it personally for fifteen years.
Theo de Raadt: Founder and leader of the OpenBSD project. Forked OpenSSH in 1999. Three decades of security-first development, auditing every line, removing attack surface instead of adding features.
Privilege Separation: A security architecture that splits a process into a small privileged monitor and a larger unprivileged worker. Limits damage when the worker is compromised. Pioneered by OpenSSH in 2002.
OpenBSD: A security-focused open-source Unix operating system. Produced OpenSSH, LibreSSL, OpenBGPD, and the pledge()/unveil() system calls. Known for code audits and minimal defaults.
Andrew Tridgell: Australian computer scientist. Created rsync (1996), Samba (Windows file sharing on Unix), ccache, and co-developed Git's delta compression with Linus Torvalds.
Rolling Checksum: A hash that can be updated incrementally as a window slides across data. Enables rsync to find matching blocks in O(n) instead of O(n squared). Based on Adler-32.
FFmpeg: A universal multimedia framework handling over 100 audio and video codecs. 1.5 million lines of C. Powers YouTube, Netflix, VLC, Spotify, Chrome. Created by Fabrice Bellard in 2000.
Fabrice Bellard: French computer scientist. Created FFmpeg, QEMU, TinyCC, JSLinux, and Bellard's formula. Computed 2.7 trillion digits of pi on a desktop PC. Builds fundamental tools, then moves on.
VNET: Virtual Network Stack in FreeBSD. Gives each jail its own interfaces, routing table, and firewall rules. Kernel-native network isolation without overlay networks.
Poul-Henning Kamp: Danish FreeBSD developer. Created Jails (1999), the GEOM storage framework, and Varnish Cache. Known for phk's bikeshed argument and decades of kernel-level systems work.
Cracktro: A signature animation attached to cracked software by pirate groups in the 1980s and 1990s. Scrolling text, chiptune music, and vector effects as calling cards. The art form that evolved into the demoscene.
Dubmood: Swedish chiptune musician and demoscene legend. Two decades of tracker music across Amiga, C64, and PC platforms. Composed the soundtrack for Razor 1911's 'The Scene Is Dead' (2012).
Linuxulator: FreeBSD's Linux binary compatibility layer. Translates Linux system calls to FreeBSD equivalents in the kernel, running Linux binaries natively without emulation. Active since 1995.
ABI: Application Binary Interface. The low-level contract between a binary and the operating system: syscall numbers, calling conventions, data layout. Determines which binaries run on which kernel.
DTrace: A dynamic tracing framework for real-time analysis of running systems. Originated in Solaris, ported to FreeBSD and macOS. Traces kernel and userspace with near-zero overhead when idle.
Goodhart's Law: When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure. Named after economist Charles Goodhart (1975). Explains why velocity tracking corrupts estimation.
Kent Beck: Creator of Extreme Programming and co-inventor of story points with Ron Jeffries. Wrote the SUnit testing framework that became JUnit. Advocate of simplicity in software design.
Paul Vixie: Author of Vixie cron (1987), BIND (the DNS server running a third of the internet), and numerous RFCs. Internet Hall of Fame inductee (2014). The five-field crontab syntax is his.
Ken Thompson: Co-creator of Unix (1969), the B programming language, and the first cron (1975) at Bell Labs. Co-designed UTF-8 and Plan 9. Turing Award winner (1983) with Dennis Ritchie.
DataLoader: A batching and caching utility for GraphQL resolvers. Collects individual field-level data requests within a single tick and executes them as one batch query. Mitigates the N+1 problem but adds a layer REST does not need.
Apollo Client: A comprehensive GraphQL client library for JavaScript. 137 KB minified. Provides caching, state management, and query orchestration. The client-side cost of choosing GraphQL over fetch().
N+1 Problem: A data-fetching antipattern where a query for N items triggers N additional queries for related data. GraphQL resolvers are structurally prone to it; REST with SQL JOINs avoids it by default.
Introspection: A GraphQL feature that allows clients to query the schema itself, discovering types, fields, and relationships at runtime. Useful for tooling, dangerous when exposed in production without access controls.
Lee Byron: Co-creator of GraphQL at Facebook (2012) with Nick Schrock and Dan Schafer. Led the open-source release in 2015 and founded the GraphQL Foundation. Also created Immutable.js.
CDN: Content Delivery Network. A geographically distributed network of servers that cache and serve content closer to the user. Reduces latency and origin load. Relies on HTTP caching semantics (GET, Cache-Control).
seccomp: Secure Computing mode. A Linux kernel facility that restricts which system calls a process may invoke. Strict mode (2005) permits four calls. seccomp-bpf (2012) allows programmable filters via BPF programmes.
BPF: Berkeley Packet Filter. Originally a packet capture mechanism, extended in Linux as eBPF for programmable kernel-level filtering. Used by seccomp-bpf to inspect and decide on every syscall at runtime.
