SAML: Security Assertion Markup Language. An XML-based standard for exchanging authentication and authorisation data between an identity provider and a service provider. Predates OIDC and remains the de facto standard in enterprise single sign-on.
argon2: A password hashing function and winner of the 2015 Password Hashing Competition. Memory-hard and parameterised against GPU and ASIC attacks. The recommended default for storing user password hashes. Typically a four-line dependency in any modern stack.
Keycloak: An open-source identity and access management solution from Red Hat. Provides OIDC, SAML, and OAuth 2.0, user federation, social login, and admin UI. Java-based, self-hosted, production-grade for over a decade.
Ory Kratos: An open-source, headless user management and authentication system written in Go. Provides REST APIs for login, registration, account recovery, and MFA without shipping a UI, leaving presentation to the application developer.
Authentik: An open-source identity provider supporting OIDC, SAML, and OAuth 2.0. Often chosen as a self-hosted alternative to Auth0 or Keycloak for its cleaner administrative interface.
FusionAuth: An authentication and user management platform distributed as a single binary. Offers a generous free tier for self-hosted deployments and provides SSO, MFA, and user lifecycle management.
Authelia: An open-source authentication and authorisation server designed as forward auth middleware for reverse proxies such as nginx, Traefik, and Caddy. Lightweight and single-purpose.
Lucia: A lightweight authentication library for Node.js and the Web platform. Unopinionated about sessions and database access, designed to be embedded in the application rather than run as a separate service.
Session token: An opaque, server-issued identifier (commonly a UUID or cryptographically random string) associating an authenticated client with server-side session state. Live tokens stolen via HAR files enabled the 2023 Okta breach lateral movement.
Moore's Law: The observation by Gordon Moore in 1965 that the number of transistors on an integrated circuit doubles approximately every two years. Treated for decades as a law of physics, it was always an economic and engineering trend; single-thread performance scaling has been flattening since roughly 2005.
Premature optimisation: A phrase from Donald Knuth's 1974 paper 'Structured Programming with go to Statements'. The full sentence reads: 'We should forget about small efficiencies, say about 97 per cent of the time: premature optimisation is the root of all evil. Yet we should not pass up our opportunities in that critical 3 per cent.' The 3 per cent clause is almost universally omitted.
Erlang: A concurrency-oriented functional programming language developed at Ericsson in 1986 for telecom switches. Lightweight processes, message passing, and supervisor trees. The language behind WhatsApp's ability to serve 900 million users with fifty engineers.
BEAM: Bogdan's Erlang Abstract Machine. The virtual machine that executes Erlang and Elixir code. Scheduler-per-core architecture, preemptive scheduling, and sub-microsecond process creation. Used by WhatsApp, Discord (for chat), and RabbitMQ.
Mnesia: A distributed, real-time database management system built into Erlang/OTP. Tables can be stored in RAM, on disk, or replicated across nodes. Part of the stack that allowed WhatsApp to scale to 900 million users.
ejabberd: An open-source XMPP messaging server written in Erlang. Originally built by Alexey Shchepin in 2002. Used by WhatsApp as the messaging layer alongside Erlang and Mnesia during its 2015 peak efficiency period.
WhatsApp: A messaging application founded in 2009 by Jan Koum and Brian Acton. Served 900 million users with fifty engineers in 2015, on a stack of FreeBSD, Erlang, ejabberd, the BEAM VM, and Mnesia. A ratio of one engineer per 18 million users. Acquired by Facebook in 2014; the FreeBSD stack was later migrated to Linux to fit Facebook's containerised operations.
Discord: A voice, video, and text messaging platform founded in 2015 by Jason Citron and Stanislav Vishnevskiy. In 2020, rewrote its Read States service from Go to Rust; memory dropped 40 per cent, worst-case latency improved 160x. The chat backend runs on Elixir/BEAM.
Read States (Discord): The Discord service that tracks, for every user, the most recent message each channel has been read up to. Originally Go, rewritten to Rust in 2020 to eliminate garbage-collection latency spikes. Memory down 40 per cent, latency 6.5x best case and 160x worst case.
Home Mixer: Twitter's (later X's) recommendation and timeline ranking service, rewritten from Scala to Rust and Python in 2022-2023. Reported to run approximately 10x faster than its predecessor, Timeline Mixer. Part of the infrastructure overhaul that followed the $1 billion cost-cutting target.
IEA: International Energy Agency. Paris-based intergovernmental organisation established in 1974. Publishes the annual 'Energy and AI' reports that track global data centre electricity consumption. Projected 415 TWh in 2024 rising to 945 TWh by 2030.
TWh: Terawatt-hour. One trillion watt-hours, or one billion kilowatt-hours. The unit used for national and global electricity consumption figures. Global data centres consumed 415 TWh in 2024, roughly 1.5 per cent of total worldwide electricity production.
HTTP Archive: A permanent repository of web performance information. Crawls millions of URLs monthly and records their page weight, resource counts, and rendering metrics. Its annual Web Almanac documents the inflation of median page weight from 500 KB (2010) to 2.9 MB (2025).
DoubleClick: An advertising technology company founded in January 1996 in New York by Kevin O'Connor and Dwight Merriman. Introduced the third-party cookie via its DART platform (Dynamic Advertising, Reporting, and Targeting). Acquired Abacus Direct in 1999. Acquired by Google in 2007 for $3.1 billion; the EU approved the acquisition in March 2008.
DART: Dynamic Advertising, Reporting, and Targeting. The original DoubleClick platform, launched in 1996. Introduced the third-party cookie for cross-site tracking. The technical mechanism was mundane; the architectural consequence was the foundation of modern web surveillance.
Third-party cookie: A small text file set by a server the user never directly visited, carried in requests from any site that embeds that server's resources (a pixel, a script, an iframe). The mechanism by which DoubleClick, and its descendants, follow users across the web.
Kevin O'Connor: Co-founder and first CEO of DoubleClick, alongside Dwight Merriman, in January 1996. Left DoubleClick in 2001 to run ScOp Venture Capital, through which he funds early-stage technology companies.
Dwight Merriman: Co-founder and CTO of DoubleClick (1996). Later co-founded MongoDB Inc. in 2007 alongside Eliot Horowitz, applying the scaling lessons of DoubleClick's ad infrastructure to database design.
Abacus Direct: A consumer-purchasing data broker acquired by DoubleClick in 1999. DoubleClick proposed merging offline purchase records with online browsing data, prompting a US Federal Trade Commission investigation in 2000. DoubleClick backed down at the time; Google acquired the combined entity in 2007.
Real-time bidding: The auction protocol used to sell online ad impressions in real time. For each page load, an ad exchange solicits bids from advertisers (each of whom may be given user data), then serves the winning creative. Operates at approximately 600 billion bid requests per day, 6.9 million per second, across the open web.
App Tracking Transparency: An Apple iOS feature introduced in iOS 14.5 in April 2021. Requires apps to obtain the user's permission via a system dialog before tracking them across other companies' apps and websites. Opt-in rates settled at 15 to 25 per cent; Meta's CFO David Wehner estimated 2022 revenue loss at approximately $10 billion.
Consent Management Platform: A third-party service that generates, displays, and records consent for cookie banners on websites. 67 per cent of cookie banners on top sites are served by a CMP; three firms hold 37 per cent of the CMP market between them.
Cookie banner: A dialog shown to a website visitor to collect consent for cookies and similar tracking technologies, primarily driven by the EU's GDPR and ePrivacy Directive. When the 'Reject all' button is hidden behind additional clicks, up to 90 per cent of users accept.
David Wehner: Chief Financial Officer of Meta Platforms (formerly Facebook) from 2014 to 2022. In 2022 he disclosed on an earnings call that Apple's App Tracking Transparency would cost Meta approximately $10 billion in revenue that year.
WhoTracks.me: A public dataset and website maintained by Ghostery and formerly by Cliqz. Crawls the top sites on the web and reports, per site, which third-party trackers are present. Core data source for most external analyses of web tracking prevalence.
Ghostery: A privacy browser extension that blocks third-party trackers and ads. Originally developed in 2009, later acquired by Cliqz, and subsequently relaunched as an independent company. Maintains the WhoTracks.me dataset.
Eliot Horowitz: Co-founder and former CTO of MongoDB Inc. Previously a software engineer at DoubleClick, he launched MongoDB in 2007 alongside Dwight Merriman and Kevin Ryan, applying the scaling lessons of the DoubleClick ad infrastructure to a general-purpose document database.
ScOp Venture Capital: An early-stage venture capital firm founded by Kevin O'Connor after his 2001 departure from DoubleClick. Invests primarily in technology companies, continuing the O'Connor thread through the DoubleClick alumni network.
Prodigy: An early online service operated jointly by IBM, Sears, and CBS, active from 1984 to 2001. One of the first commercial services to cache data on or near users' personal computers to reduce networking and server costs. An early demonstration that any data path optimised for cost eventually becomes commercially interesting.
MongoDB: A document-oriented database released in 2009 by MongoDB Inc., founded in 2007 by former DoubleClick engineers Dwight Merriman, Kevin Ryan, and Eliot Horowitz. Stores JSON-like documents in collections; popular for web applications despite ongoing debates about its consistency guarantees.
zfs snapshot: An atomic, block-level, immutable view of a ZFS dataset at a given instant. Created in milliseconds on active datasets, holds no lock, copies no files. The unit of backup, replication, and branching on ZFS.
zfs send: A ZFS command that serialises a snapshot (or the delta between two snapshots) into a byte stream. Block-level, not file-level. Typically piped over SSH to a remote 'zfs receive' for disaster-recovery replication and read-replica provisioning. Incremental sends of a 40 TB dataset with small deltas complete in seconds.
zfs clone: A writable copy of a ZFS snapshot created with copy-on-write semantics. Costs no additional disk space until the clone diverges from the parent, at which point only the changed blocks are written. The primitive behind Git-like branching on a multi-terabyte filesystem.
zfs diff: A ZFS command that compares two snapshots (or a snapshot and its active dataset) and emits a file-level, path-based list of additions, removals, modifications, and renames. File-level, not block-level.
zfs rollback: A ZFS command that reverts a dataset to the state captured in a named snapshot. Single-command; execution time is measured in the duration of typing it, not in the size of the dataset.
zfs promote: A ZFS command that swaps the parent-child relationship between a clone and its origin dataset. Used when a clone is intended to become the new canonical dataset and the former parent is to be retired.
Copy-on-write: A storage and memory technique where a shared block is not duplicated until one of its users modifies it, at which point only the modifying user pays for a new copy. The basis for ZFS snapshots, clones, and most modern filesystems.
btrfs: A Linux copy-on-write filesystem merged into the mainline kernel in 2009. Offers subvolume snapshots, clones, and send/receive. RAID 5/6 remains officially discouraged for production use in 2026; send requires the source subvolume to be read-only; deduplication is offline only.
Subvolume: A named, independently mountable and snapshottable region of a btrfs filesystem, broadly analogous to a ZFS dataset. Multiple subvolumes share the underlying storage pool.
Paweł Jakub Dawidek: FreeBSD committer who ported OpenSolaris ZFS to FreeBSD in 2007, shipping first in FreeBSD 7.0 (2008). His work brought inline snapshots, copy-on-write clones, and block-level replication to the FreeBSD base system.
sanoid: A ZFS snapshot management policy engine written by Jim Salter. Manages scheduled snapshots, retention, and pruning on top of the zfs snapshot primitive. Typically paired with syncoid for replication.
syncoid: A companion tool to sanoid that automates zfs send and zfs receive, including incremental replication, recursive datasets, and resumable transfers. Reduces the send/receive workflow to a single command.
zrepl: A ZFS replication daemon written in Go. Provides scheduled, resumable replication over SSH or TLS, with pull and push modes and encrypted datasets. Sits above the zfs send and zfs receive primitives.
Aaron Swartz: American programmer and internet activist (1986-2013). Co-author of the RSS 1.0 specification at age fourteen, principal architect of the Creative Commons technical infrastructure, co-founder of Reddit, founder of the web.py framework, and the 'sole beta-tester' whose feedback shaped Markdown's syntax during its 2003-2004 design. Died in January 2013 during federal prosecution for downloading academic papers from JSTOR.
atx: A plain-text shorthand for HTML created by Aaron Swartz in 2002. Introduced the hash-prefix heading convention (#, ##, ###) that was subsequently adopted by Markdown in 2004. Paired with Swartz's html2text, a reverse converter that turns HTML back into atx/Markdown syntax.
html2text: A Python tool by Aaron Swartz that converts HTML into atx/Markdown-style plain text. Released December 2002 as the reverse complement to atx, enabling round-trip editing of HTML in a plain-text editor.
Daring Fireball: John Gruber's long-running personal blog, launched in 2002. The venue where Markdown was announced on 15 March 2004 and where the original reference implementation Markdown.pl was first distributed.
GitHub Flavored Markdown: A Markdown dialect introduced by GitHub in 2009 and formally specified in 2017. Extends CommonMark with tables, task lists, strikethrough, and autolinks. The flavour most developers actually mean when they say 'Markdown'.
John MacFarlane: Philosopher at the University of California, Berkeley and author of Pandoc, the universal document converter. Co-led the CommonMark effort with Jeff Atwood from 2012, producing the first formal Markdown specification in 2014.
Jeff Atwood: Software developer and co-founder of Stack Overflow (2008) and Discourse (2013). Co-led the CommonMark standardisation effort with John MacFarlane from 2012 and authored the 'Coding Horror' blog.
Pandoc: A universal document converter written by John MacFarlane, first released in 2006. Translates between dozens of markup formats (Markdown, reStructuredText, HTML, LaTeX, DOCX, EPUB, and many more). The reference implementation for most of the extensions that CommonMark later absorbed.
Discourse: An open-source internet forum platform released in 2013 by Jeff Atwood, Robin Ward, and Sam Saffron. Uses CommonMark-based Markdown for post authoring and was one of the early drivers of the CommonMark standardisation effort.
Notion: A note-taking, knowledge management, and collaboration application launched in 2016. Stores block-structured content internally but exports and imports in Markdown; widely credited with popularising Markdown-native personal knowledge management in the mainstream market.
Obsidian: A local-first personal knowledge management application released in 2020. Stores notes as plain Markdown files on disk with a graph view over internal links. Popular in the Zettelkasten and second-brain communities.
Logseq: An open-source, block-based, Markdown-native personal knowledge management application. Combines outlining with backlinks and graph views, storing all content as Markdown files in a local folder.
web.py: A minimalist Python web framework written by Aaron Swartz, first released in 2006. Provided the foundation for early Reddit and a number of other small-to-mid-scale web applications of the period.
@scope: A CSS at-rule that fences a block of style rules to a region of the DOM bounded by an upper scope root and an optional lower scope limit. Shipped in Chrome 118 (October 2023), Safari 17.4 (March 2024), and Firefox 146 (early 2026); reached Baseline 'Newly available' status in December 2025 with approximately 91 per cent global usage.
Scoping proximity: A sorting tier introduced to the CSS cascade by CSS Cascade Level 6. When two @scope blocks produce conflicting declarations for the same element, the rule whose scope root is fewer DOM hops away from that element wins. Sits between specificity and source order in the cascade order.
Donut scope: The informal name for the @scope (root) to (limit) pattern: style the doughnut (everything inside the root), leave the hole alone (everything inside the limit). Borrowed from the Oddbird @scope explainer by Miriam Suzanne.
:scope: A CSS pseudo-class that, inside an @scope block, refers to the current scope root. Allows rules to target the root itself, for example 'padding' or 'border' on the component container.
:where(): A CSS pseudo-class functional notation that takes a selector list and matches any of those selectors, with zero specificity. Used in the @scope specification to explain how bare selectors inside a scope retain their original specificity.
CSS Cascade Level 6: The current W3C Working Draft that formalises @layer, @scope, and scoping proximity as part of the CSS cascade. Revises the cascade order to: importance, cascade layers, specificity, scoping proximity, source order.
Miriam Suzanne: CSS specification editor, member of the CSS Working Group, and co-founder of Oddbird. Authored the current @scope explainer and has co-edited specifications including Cascade Layers and Container Queries.
Oddbird: A web development consultancy and CSS specification contributor co-founded by Miriam Suzanne. Host of the public @scope explainer and a long-running source of upstream CSS work.
CSS Modules: A JavaScript-based scoping approach that hashes CSS class names at build time to produce locally-scoped class identifiers. Solves the global-namespace problem at the cost of a build step and a runtime.
styled-components: A React-specific CSS-in-JS library that allows CSS to be written inline with component definitions, producing hashed class names at runtime. Introduced in 2016; typical runtime cost is around 25 KB plus a Babel transform.
Emotion: A CSS-in-JS library for React, similar in concept to styled-components. Offers template-string and object-syntax styling with runtime-generated class names.
BEM: Block, Element, Modifier. A CSS naming convention developed at Yandex that encodes component structure into class names (for example, 'product-card__heading--featured'). An attempt to fake scoping via naming discipline in the absence of native scope support.
Integrated by Design: First book by Vivian Voss, published by Voss'scher Verlag on 23 April 2026 (UNESCO World Book Day). 371 pages, full colour. Subtitle: 'Why the Best Systems Are the Ones You Don't Notice'. FreeBSD from philosophy to practice, in four parts and 22 chapters.
KDP: Kindle Direct Publishing. Amazon's self-publishing platform for paperback, hardcover, and Kindle editions. Handles printing, distribution, royalty calculation, and marketplace listings. Notorious among first-time European publishers for a pricing form that accepts what it calls 'list price' and treats it as net, adding local book VAT on top at checkout.
JetBrains Mono: A free and open-source monospaced programming font released by JetBrains in 2020. Ships in sixteen styles across four weights with matching italics. Designed for long reading sessions at small sizes, with tall lowercase and open counters. Distributed under the SIL Open Font Licence 1.1, which permits renamed derivatives.
Counter (typography): The enclosed or partially enclosed blank space inside a letterform, such as the two openings in the numeral 8 or the opening in a lowercase 'o'. Open counters print well at small sizes; counters that are slightly unclosed in the outline can fill in at matte-paper ink gain, which is how an 8 quietly becomes a 0.
UNESCO World Book Day: An annual observance on 23 April, declared by UNESCO in 1995 to celebrate books, reading, and copyright. The date commemorates the anniversary of the deaths of Shakespeare, Cervantes, and Inca Garcilaso de la Vega. Traditional launch day for new books in many markets.
EPUB: Electronic Publication. An open e-book file format standardised by the International Digital Publishing Forum (now W3C) based on XHTML, CSS, and ZIP packaging. Reflowable by default, readable on most non-Kindle e-readers and on Kindle after conversion.
ASIN: Amazon Standard Identification Number. A ten-character alphanumeric identifier assigned by Amazon to each product variant in its catalogue. For books, Amazon uses the ISBN-10 as the ASIN where available. The canonical URL form is amazon.com/dp/<ASIN>, which resolves to the local marketplace for the visitor.
Voss'scher Verlag: Publishing imprint founded by Vivian Voss. First title: Integrated by Design, published 23 April 2026. Publishes technical non-fiction in English with bilingual supporting material. Offers direct-order editions (secured EPUB, secured PDF, direct-printed) alongside Amazon marketplace distribution.
Service mesh: An infrastructure layer that moves cross-cutting concerns (mTLS, retries, timeouts, traffic shifting, observability) out of application code and into a proxy beside each pod. Archetype: Istio. CNCF 2024 Annual Survey recorded adoption at 42 per cent, down from 50 per cent in 2023, the category's first full-year decline.
Sidecar proxy: A separate container injected into every application pod, intercepting the pod's inbound and outbound network traffic. Envoy is the archetypal sidecar. A 2025 peer-reviewed benchmark measured Istio sidecars at +166 per cent mTLS latency overhead; the Istio 1.24 docs report approximately 60 MB of RAM per sidecar at 1,000 RPS.
Envoy: An open-source service proxy originally developed at Lyft and released in 2016. Written in C++, configured via the xDS API. Used as the data-plane sidecar in Istio and several other meshes, and frequently as a stand-alone ingress gateway.
Linkerd: A service mesh project graduated in the Cloud Native Computing Foundation. Sidecar is written in Rust (linkerd2-proxy) and was built specifically for the job rather than adapted from a general proxy. Measured at +33 per cent mTLS overhead in the 2025 DeepNess Lab benchmark, compared with +166 per cent for Istio sidecars.
Cilium: A CNCF-graduated networking and security project based on eBPF. Offers a service-mesh mode that, per the 2025 DeepNess Lab benchmark, adds +99 per cent mTLS latency overhead between pods.
Istio Ambient: A sidecar-free data-plane architecture released as generally available in Istio 1.23 in August 2024. Replaces per-pod Envoy sidecars with a shared node-level ztunnel and optional per-namespace waypoint proxies. Measured at +8 per cent mTLS overhead in the 2025 DeepNess Lab benchmark.
ztunnel: The node-level L4 proxy introduced by Istio Ambient mode. Written in Rust. Handles mTLS, identity, and basic traffic routing shared across all pods on a node, with approximately 12 MB of RAM and 0.06 vCPU per node at 1,000 RPS.
istiod: The Istio control-plane component. Responsible for distributing configuration to sidecars via the xDS API, issuing and rotating mTLS certificates via SDS, and validating the mesh's custom resources.
CRD: Custom Resource Definition. A Kubernetes extension mechanism that lets projects register new API types (for example, Istio's VirtualService and DestinationRule). Each CRD contributes to the mental load of operating the cluster.
VPC: Virtual Private Cloud. A logically isolated network on a public cloud provider. A VPC was a trust boundary before sidecars were a marketing category, and for most workloads it remains the appropriate place to terminate inter-service authentication.
CNCF: Cloud Native Computing Foundation. A Linux Foundation sub-project that hosts Kubernetes, Prometheus, Envoy, Linkerd, Istio (graduated July 2023), and most of the infrastructure code the cloud-native industry depends on. Publishes an annual survey of technology adoption.
DeepNess Lab: A networking research group that published a 2025 peer-reviewed paper titled 'Performance Comparison of Service Mesh Frameworks: the mTLS Test Case'. The primary open citation for the +166 / +99 / +33 / +8 per cent mTLS overhead figures for Istio sidecar, Cilium, Linkerd, and Istio Ambient respectively.
xDS API: The set of discovery APIs Envoy consumes for dynamic configuration: listeners (LDS), clusters (CDS), endpoints (EDS), routes (RDS), and secrets (SDS). Istio's istiod acts as the xDS server to every sidecar in the mesh.
XZ Utils: An open-source compression library and command-line toolset providing the LZMA2 algorithm. Ships in essentially every Linux distribution. Backdoored in versions 5.6.0 and 5.6.1 in early 2024 in a multi-year supply-chain attack (CVE-2024-3094, CVSS 10.0).
CVE-2024-3094: The XZ Utils backdoor disclosed by Andres Freund on 29 March 2024. Malicious code in xz-utils 5.6.0 and 5.6.1 was linked into liblzma at build time and pulled into sshd via systemd-notify, enabling remote code execution on every machine running the affected OpenSSH builds. CVSS score 10.0.
Andres Freund: PostgreSQL maintainer at Microsoft. On 28 March 2024 noticed his SSH login on a Debian unstable system was approximately 500 milliseconds slower than usual, ran the login under valgrind, traced the cause to liblzma, found the obfuscated XZ payload, and disclosed it to the oss-security mailing list on 29 March.
Lasse Collin: Long-time sole maintainer of XZ Utils. By his own account burnt out and unpaid, he was pressured by sock-puppet accounts (Jia Tan, Dennis Ens, Jigar Kumar) into granting commit rights to Jia Tan, the account that subsequently inserted the CVE-2024-3094 backdoor.
Jia Tan: GitHub account created in January 2021. Submitted patches to xz-utils for over two years before being granted maintainer rights in 2022, then committed the CVE-2024-3094 backdoor in early 2024. Now widely interpreted as a long-running supply-chain operation rather than an individual.
liblzma: The shared library implementation of the LZMA2 compression algorithm distributed as part of XZ Utils. Linked into many binaries across Linux distributions. The CVE-2024-3094 backdoor was injected into liblzma at build time via test fixtures and autotools.
systemd-notify: A systemd integration that allows long-running services to signal readiness to the init system. The CVE-2024-3094 attack chain depended on systemd-notify pulling liblzma into sshd's address space on systemd-based distributions, providing the route from xz-utils into OpenSSH.
Russ Cox: Software engineer at Google, member of the Go core team. Authored the canonical day-by-day timeline of the XZ Utils backdoor at research.swtch.com/xz-timeline, widely cited in subsequent supply-chain analyses.
Express: A minimalist web framework for Node.js, originally written by TJ Holowaychuk in 2010. Version 5.2.1 declares 28 direct dependencies, resolves 65 packages transitively, and produces a 3.6 MB node_modules on a fresh install.
Next.js: A React-based web framework developed by Vercel. The default create-next-app scaffold (with TypeScript, Tailwind, ESLint, App Router) resolves 644 packages and writes 463 MB of node_modules to render its initial 'Welcome to Next.js' page.
node_modules: The directory in which npm and compatible package managers materialise the resolved dependency tree of a Node.js project. A common indicator of supply-chain surface area; sizes ranging from a few megabytes to several hundred are routine.
hyper: A low-level HTTP implementation library for Rust. Provides the foundation for higher-level frameworks such as axum and reqwest. Designed to be small, explicit, and visible in Cargo.toml/Cargo.lock for review.
Cargo.toml: The declarative manifest file used by Rust's Cargo package manager. Lists the project's direct dependencies in writing; Cargo.lock pins the full transitive graph. Designed so that every dependency is reviewable in a single sitting.
FreeBSD base: The FreeBSD base system: kernel plus a curated userland (init, sshd, libc, the standard Unix toolkit) maintained as one source tree, audited together, and released together. Distinct from the ports collection, which provides third-party packages with named maintainers.
SBOM: Software Bill of Materials. A machine-readable inventory of the components and dependencies in a software product, typically in SPDX or CycloneDX format. SBOM tools produced clean reports for the backdoored XZ versions, illustrating that bills of materials list packages but do not audit them.
Goodhart's Law: The observation by British economist Charles Goodhart in a 1975 footnote on UK monetary policy: 'Any observed statistical regularity will tend to collapse once pressure is placed upon it for control purposes.' Twenty-two years later restated by Marilyn Strathern as 'When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.'
Charles Goodhart: British economist, formerly of the Bank of England and the London School of Economics. Author of the 1975 paper containing the footnote that became Goodhart's Law: any observed statistical regularity tends to collapse once it becomes a control target.
Marilyn Strathern: British anthropologist, Cambridge. In her 1997 essay 'Improving Ratings: Audit in the British University System' she condensed Goodhart's footnote into the version now universally quoted: when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.
Watertown Arsenal strike: A summer 1911 walk-out by foundry workers at the US Army's Watertown Arsenal in Massachusetts, triggered by the introduction of Frederick Winslow Taylor's stopwatch-based time studies. The US Congress investigated and subsequently banned time studies and pay premiums tied to them on US Government work.
Story Points Revisited: A 2019 article by Ron Jeffries on ronjeffries.com in which he wrote 'I may have made the name-changing suggestion. If I did, I'm sorry now', and recommended abandoning story-point estimation entirely.
Made in Germany: A country-of-origin label introduced by the British Merchandise Marks Act of 1887, originally as a stigma to warn consumers of inferior German imports. Within roughly thirty years it had inverted into a guarantee of quality, because German firms (Solingen, Carl Zeiss, Steinway, Leica) used the label not to perform productivity but to ship goods that lasted.
Number 5 Crossbar Switching System: An electromechanical telephone switching system designed at Bell Labs and introduced in 1948. Deployed in central offices across North America until the late 1980s, with individual installations running thirty years or more. A standard reference for designed-to-last engineering in the pre-software era.
Volvo three-point seatbelt: Designed by Nils Bohlin at Volvo and introduced in 1959. Volvo released the patent freely to other manufacturers on the grounds that the safety benefit of universal adoption outweighed exclusive licensing revenue. Estimated to have saved over a million lives by 2009.
Solingen: A city in North Rhine-Westphalia, Germany, historically the centre of European cutlery and bladed-tool manufacture. The 'Solingen' geographical indication is legally protected and signifies blades produced in the city to a defined craftsmanship standard.
Carl Zeiss: German optical manufacturer founded in Jena in 1846. Synonymous with precision optics from microscopes through camera lenses to lithography systems. One of the canonical examples of the post-1887 inversion of the 'Made in Germany' label.
Sheffield cutlery: Cutlery produced in Sheffield, England, the centre of British steel and cutlery manufacture from roughly the 17th century onward. The Sheffield manufacturers' complaints about German imitations were a primary driver of the British Merchandise Marks Act of 1887.
Bootloader: The small program that runs after firmware (BIOS or UEFI) and before the operating system kernel. Its job is to locate, load, and hand control to a kernel image. The bootloader's design quietly encodes what the operating system above believes itself to be.
loader(8): FreeBSD's third-stage bootloader, around 600 KB on amd64. A Forth interpreter that understands UFS and ZFS natively, mounts a ZFS dataset as root, knows what a Boot Environment is, and presents the list as a menu before the kernel starts. Configured via loader.conf(5).
boot0: The 446-byte FreeBSD master boot record. The first stage of the FreeBSD boot chain, just large enough to fit in the legacy MBR and locate the next stage on disk.
boot1: FreeBSD's second-stage bootloader. Reads the partition table and locates loader(8). Sits between boot0 and the Forth-interpreter loader.
Forth: A small, deterministic, stack-based programming language designed by Charles H. Moore in 1970. Used in firmware and embedded systems where a programmable runtime is needed without the assumptions of a full operating system. The implementation language of FreeBSD's loader(8).
LILO: The Linux Loader. Written by Werner Almesberger at ETH Zürich in 1992; maintained by John Coffman until 2007 and then Joachim Wiedorn. Used a block-list approach that required /sbin/lilo to be re-run after any kernel change. Last release 24.2 in December 2015; development never resumed.
Werner Almesberger: Swiss software engineer at ETH Zürich. Wrote LILO, the original Linux Loader, in 1992 and maintained it until 1998. Subsequently contributed to the Linux kernel's networking and ATM stacks.
GRUB 2: The GRand Unified Bootloader, version 2. A complete rewrite of the original GRUB shipped from 2005 onward. ~200 KB core plus several megabytes of loadable modules: filesystem drivers for ext, btrfs, XFS, F2FS, FAT, NTFS, ISO9660, ZFS (read-only), and many others; scripting; themes; UEFI; network boot; multiboot.
Erich Boleyn: American software engineer who started GRUB in 1995 as a research bootloader for the GNU Hurd. The project was adopted by the GNU project in 1999 and rewritten from scratch as GRUB 2 in 2005.
Block list (LILO): A bootloader addressing approach in which the kernel's location on disk is recorded as a list of physical sectors at install time. If the kernel image moves (a new build, a filesystem reshuffle, an LVM operation), the bootloader points at gibberish. Required LILO users to re-run /sbin/lilo after every kernel change.
beadm: A FreeBSD command-line tool for managing ZFS Boot Environments, written in 2012 by the Polish admin vermaden in POSIX sh and awk. Mimics the Solaris and Illumos beadm interface; ~1500 lines, dependency-free, runnable on any FreeBSD since 9.x. Notable for the REROOT option that swaps userspace into a prior Boot Environment without a full reboot.
vermaden: Polish FreeBSD administrator and author of beadm(8). Long-running blog at vermaden.wordpress.com documents ZFS Boot Environments, FreeBSD jails, and the Unix-Way philosophy from a working admin's perspective.
manageBE: An early shell-script tool for managing ZFS Boot Environments on FreeBSD. The functional but not particularly pleasant predecessor to beadm.
REROOT: A beadm(8) option that swaps userspace into a previously-created Boot Environment without rebooting. The kernel stays up; the userspace is reloaded from the chosen ZFS dataset. Recovery in seconds rather than the half-minute of a reboot.
libbe: The FreeBSD library encoding the Boot Environment data model. Provides direct ZFS access for tools that manage Boot Environments, used by bectl(8) to avoid subprocess fan-out.
Solaris: The Unix operating system originally developed by Sun Microsystems, later acquired by Oracle. Introduced ZFS, DTrace, and Zones, all of which subsequently appeared in FreeBSD. Contributed the original beadm interface that vermaden mimicked on FreeBSD.
Illumos: The community fork of OpenSolaris, started in 2010 after Oracle's acquisition of Sun Microsystems closed source development. Carries forward the Solaris kernel, ZFS, DTrace, and Zones. Source of the beadm interface convention that beadm(8) on FreeBSD adopted.
Tom Jøran Sønstebyseter Rønning: Norwegian security researcher at Palo Alto Networks Norway. In April 2026 disclosed that Microsoft Edge's password manager loads the entire saved-credential vault into the msedge.exe process and decrypts it eagerly at launch, holding cleartext credentials in process memory for the duration of the session. Initial disclosure at Palo Alto Networks Norway's 'BIG Bite of Tech' event on 29 April 2026; LinkedIn demonstration video on 4 May 2026, picked up across the security press over the following days.
Palo Alto Networks: American multinational cybersecurity company headquartered in Santa Clara, California, founded by Nir Zuk in 2005. Operates Unit 42 (threat intelligence and incident response) and a global network of regional security-research teams. Runs the BIG Bite of Tech industry event series in Norway and other markets.
BIG Bite of Tech: An industry-facing technical event series organised by Palo Alto Networks Norway, hosted as in-person breakfast or afternoon sessions for security and IT professionals. The 29 April 2026 edition was the venue for Tom Jøran Sønstebyseter Rønning's disclosure of the Microsoft Edge cleartext password-vault behaviour.
Application-Bound Encryption: A defence shipped by Google Chrome on Windows since mid-2024 that ties the decryption keys for the saved-credential and cookie stores to the authenticated Chrome process running as SYSTEM. The mechanism makes the decryption keys non-portable across processes: an attacker with local administrator rights but without SYSTEM cannot trivially extract the keys and decrypt the vault outside Chrome's own process tree. Documented in Google's Chromium Security Blog. Inherited by Chromium-based browsers that pull from upstream; Microsoft Edge does not implement an equivalent.
ABE: Abbreviation for Application-Bound Encryption, the Chrome on-Windows defence that binds saved-credential decryption keys to the SYSTEM-level Chrome process, making them non-portable across processes.
Sysinternals: A suite of advanced Windows system utilities originally created by Mark Russinovich and Bryce Cogswell at Winternals Software, acquired by Microsoft in 2006. Includes Process Explorer, Process Monitor, Autoruns, PsTools and Sysmon among others. Distributed free of charge as Microsoft downloads. The reference toolset for Windows process and memory inspection in administration, forensics and incident response.
Process Explorer: A Microsoft Sysinternals utility (procexp.exe) by Mark Russinovich that displays the full Windows process tree with detailed per-process information: open handles, loaded DLLs, memory regions, performance counters and the strings present in process memory. Standard tool for inspecting what a running process actually contains, including the cleartext strings used by Tom Jøran Sønstebyseter Rønning to verify the Microsoft Edge password-vault behaviour.
System Informer: An open-source Windows system inspection and process-management utility, the active community successor to Process Hacker. Provides process inspection, thread analysis, memory inspection and string search comparable to Microsoft Sysinternals' Process Explorer; in some areas more granular. Distributed under the MIT licence.
Process Hacker: An open-source Windows process inspection and management utility, originally written by Wen Jia Liu and released in 2008. Maintenance went dormant in 2022; the project was effectively succeeded by System Informer, which forked from the same codebase and continues active development.
KeePassXC: A free, open-source, locally-encrypted password manager, a community-maintained cross-platform fork of KeePassX, itself a fork of KeePass. Stores credentials in an AES-256-encrypted KDBX database file under the user's control. Memory hygiene is part of the documented threat model: only the credential being filled is decrypted, and the decrypted buffer is zeroed after the operation. Browser extensions for Edge, Chrome, Firefox and others provide autofill identical to a built-in browser manager.
Proton Pass: A hosted, end-to-end encrypted password manager from the Swiss company Proton AG, the publisher of Proton Mail and Proton Drive. Released in beta in April 2023 and generally available in June 2023. Independently audited; explicitly designed with memory hygiene as a first-class concern in its desktop and browser clients. Free tier is functional; paid tiers add additional features.
Proton AG: A Swiss company headquartered in Geneva, founded in 2014 by researchers from CERN. Publisher of Proton Mail (encrypted email), Proton Drive (encrypted cloud storage), Proton VPN, Proton Calendar and Proton Pass (password manager). Operates as a Swiss non-profit foundation since June 2024.
PasswordManagerEnabled: A Microsoft Edge Group Policy setting that controls whether Edge's built-in password manager is offered to users. Setting it to 0 disables the saved-password store entirely on managed Windows estates: the autofill UI is removed, no new credentials can be stored, and there is no eager-decryption behaviour because there is nothing to decrypt. Documented in Microsoft Learn under the Edge browser policies reference.
Group Policy: A Microsoft Windows feature for centrally managing settings, security and software across an Active Directory estate. Policies are defined in administrative templates and applied to user or computer objects; for browsers like Microsoft Edge, Group Policy is the standard mechanism by which corporate, healthcare and government IT teams configure or restrict browser behaviour across all users in scope.
Vivaldi: A Chromium-based desktop and mobile browser, created in 2016 by former Opera Software CEO Jon Stephenson von Tetzchner and the Vivaldi Technologies team. Targets power users with extensive UI customisation, built-in tab management, mail client and calendar. Inherits Chrome's lazy-decryption model for the saved-credential store; does not implement an Edge-style eager decryption.
Opera (browser): A Chromium-based desktop and mobile browser originally developed in Norway from 1995 by Opera Software, acquired in 2016 by a Chinese consortium led by Kunlun Tech and Qihoo 360. Inherits Chrome's lazy-decryption model for the saved-credential store; does not implement an Edge-style eager decryption.
Cybernews: Lithuanian cybersecurity-news publication operated by Mediatech LT, founded in 2019. Active as a primary outlet for security disclosures, vulnerability research and consumer-grade cybersecurity reporting; carried the initial English-language coverage of the Microsoft Edge cleartext password-vault disclosure in late April 2026.
DarkReading: American cybersecurity-news publication, part of Informa Tech, founded in 2006. Targets enterprise security professionals; carried the enterprise-impact analysis of the Microsoft Edge cleartext password-vault disclosure in early May 2026.
SANS Internet Storm Center: The volunteer-run incident analysis arm of the SANS Institute, monitoring internet-wide attack patterns since 2001. Publishes a daily 'diary' of analysed events and short technical notes by SANS handlers; carried a verification diary of the Microsoft Edge cleartext password-vault disclosure in May 2026.
Heise online: German technology-news publication operated by Heise Medien (Hannover), part of the Heise publishing group. Long-running technical authority in the German-speaking market for IT, programming, security and consumer electronics; carried the German-language coverage of the Microsoft Edge cleartext password-vault disclosure in May 2026.
Not in the Brief Episode 02: The second episode of Vivian Voss's 'Not in the Brief' series, published 9 May 2026. Documents Microsoft Edge's eager decryption of the entire saved-password vault into msedge.exe at launch, with the cleartext credentials remaining in process memory for the duration of the browsing session. Covers the disclosure by Tom Jøran Sønstebyseter Rønning at Palo Alto Networks Norway's BIG Bite of Tech on 29 April 2026, the architectural comparison with Chrome's lazy decryption and Application-Bound Encryption, the threat-model gap on shared workstations and kiosks, and the verification path with Process Explorer or System Informer.
Jesse James Garrett: American information architect and user-experience designer. Co-founded Adaptive Path in 2001. Coined the term AJAX in his February 2005 essay 'Ajax: A New Approach to Web Applications', describing the asynchronous-XMLHttpRequest pattern that Gmail and Google Maps had been using.
AJAX: Asynchronous JavaScript and XML. A pattern coined by Jesse James Garrett in February 2005 for updating parts of a web page without a full reload, using XMLHttpRequest to fetch data in the background. The technique pre-existed the name; Gmail (2004) and Google Maps (2005) were the canonical demonstrators. The term is broader today and rarely involves XML.
XMLHttpRequest: A browser-side JavaScript API introduced by Microsoft in Internet Explorer 5 (1999) for issuing HTTP requests without a page reload. The technical foundation of AJAX. Largely superseded today by the Fetch API.
Jordan Walke: Software engineer at Facebook. Created React in 2011 as an internal Facebook project; first released publicly in May 2013 at JSConf US. The library introduced the virtual-DOM diffing model that defined a generation of frontend development.
Jeremy Ashkenas: Canadian software engineer. Created Backbone.js (2010), CoffeeScript (2009), and Underscore.js (2009) while at DocumentCloud. Backbone was an early model-view-presenter framework that influenced the later wave of JavaScript MV* libraries.
Backbone.js: An early JavaScript MV* framework released by Jeremy Ashkenas in October 2010. Provided models, collections, views, routing and event binding in roughly 1,500 lines. Heavily used at the start of the SPA era; largely superseded by Angular and React.
Misko Hevery (Angular): Co-creator of AngularJS at Google in 2010 alongside Adam Abrons. Distinct from his later work on Angular 2 (2016) and Qwik (2022). The original AngularJS introduced two-way binding and dirty checking to the mainstream JavaScript audience.
Adam Abrons: Co-creator of AngularJS at Google in 2010 alongside Misko Hevery. Worked at Google's Brat Tech LLC subsidiary on the original framework before AngularJS was rebranded as Angular 2.
AngularJS: The original JavaScript framework released by Google in October 2010, designed by Misko Hevery and Adam Abrons. Introduced two-way data binding, dirty checking, and dependency injection on the client. End-of-life since December 2021. Distinct from the later Angular (2+), which was a complete rewrite.
Ember.js: A JavaScript framework released by Yehuda Katz and Tom Dale in December 2011, descended from SproutCore. Heavy convention-over-configuration philosophy; widely used at LinkedIn, Apple Music and DigitalOcean.
React: A JavaScript library for building user interfaces, created by Jordan Walke at Facebook in 2011 and released publicly in May 2013. Introduced the virtual-DOM model, JSX, and unidirectional data flow. The de facto frontend library since approximately 2016.
Node.js: A JavaScript runtime released by Ryan Dahl in May 2009, built on V8 and originally designed for I/O-bound real-time workloads. Single-threaded event loop. Subsequently adopted as a general-purpose server runtime, displacing PHP, Ruby, Python and Go in many shops without a technical case beyond the team's existing language familiarity.
HTTP Archive Web Almanac: An annual report published by the HTTP Archive project that summarises the state of the web from terabytes of crawled data. The 2025 edition (almanac.httparchive.org/en/2025) reports a median page weight of 697 KB of JavaScript on home pages, 632 KB on inner pages, with 90th and 10th percentiles at 1,979 KB and 87 KB respectively.
Carson Gross: Founder of Big Sky Software in Bozeman, Montana. Created HTMX in 2020 as a successor to his earlier intercooler.js. Argues that most of what is built with single-page applications can be expressed as HTML attributes that swap server-rendered fragments. Also lectures in computer science at Montana State University.
Big Sky Software: Software consultancy founded by Carson Gross in Bozeman, Montana. The home of HTMX, hyperscript and intercooler.js, all small client-side libraries oriented around HTML-over-the-wire architectures.
HTMX: A small JavaScript library by Carson Gross at Big Sky Software, first released publicly in 2020 as a rewrite of intercooler.js. 14 KB minified, ~5 KB gzipped. Adds attributes (hx-get, hx-post, hx-swap, hx-trigger and others) to plain HTML so that fragments returned by the server can be swapped into the page without a client-side framework. Implements a hypermedia-driven application model in single-digit kilobytes.
hx-swap: An HTMX attribute that controls how the response from a server fragment is inserted into the DOM (innerHTML, outerHTML, beforebegin, beforeend, afterbegin, afterend, delete or none). Pairs with hx-target to specify the destination element.
hx-trigger: An HTMX attribute that controls when a request is fired (events such as click, change, every n seconds, intersection-observer triggers, and more). Allows full reactivity, including throttling, delay and event filtering, without writing JavaScript.
Hotwire: A set of frameworks released by 37signals in December 2020 (Turbo, Stimulus, Strada) that delivers fully interactive web applications by sending HTML fragments over the wire instead of JSON to a client-side framework. Underpins HEY, Basecamp 4 and many Ruby-on-Rails-driven applications.
Turbo Streams: A Hotwire mechanism for broadcasting fragments of HTML to a page over WebSocket or Server-Sent Events. Used to deliver real-time updates (new chat messages, status changes, collaborative edits) without a client-side framework.
DHH: David Heinemeier Hansson. Danish creator of Ruby on Rails (2004) and co-founder of 37signals. Author of the Hotwire framework (2020) and a long-running advocate for HTML-over-the-wire architectures. Famous for the 2023 cloud-exit move from AWS that saved 37signals approximately 2M USD per year.
HEY: An email service released by 37signals in June 2020. Built on Ruby on Rails with Hotwire on the front end. Demonstrates a fully interactive modern email client without a client-side JavaScript framework.
Basecamp: Project-management software by 37signals, originally released in 2004 as a flagship product of the Ruby on Rails framework. Basecamp 4 (2024) runs on Hotwire with no client-side framework.
MediaWiki: The wiki software that powers Wikipedia and other Wikimedia projects, originally written in PHP by Magnus Manske at the University of Cologne in 2002. Runs on PHP 8.3 (2026) with MySQL/MariaDB, server-rendered with aggressive caching at the Wikimedia infrastructure layer.
Wikimedia Foundation: American non-profit organisation founded by Jimmy Wales in 2003 to operate Wikipedia and the related Wikimedia projects. Maintains MediaWiki and the global infrastructure that serves Wikipedia at hundreds of thousands of views per second.
GOV.UK: The official portal for United Kingdom government services, launched in October 2012 by the Government Digital Service. The flagship reference implementation of progressive enhancement, mandated for every government service in the GOV.UK Service Manual. HTML first, CSS second, JavaScript only as enhancement.
Government Digital Service: A unit of the United Kingdom Cabinet Office, founded by Mike Bracken and Tom Loosemore in 2011. Designed and built GOV.UK and the underlying GOV.UK Design System and Service Manual. Influential on civic-tech practice in many other countries.
Progressive enhancement: A web-design philosophy in which a page is built to work first as plain HTML over the network, then enhanced with CSS, then optionally with JavaScript. The page remains functional at every layer if the enhancement above fails. Mandated for GOV.UK; the inverse of the SPA-first reflex.
Time to Interactive (TTI): A web performance metric defined by Google as the time at which a page is fully interactive: visually rendered, page handlers registered, and the main thread quiet for at least five seconds. Frequently 8 seconds or more on mid-range Android over 4G for SPAs in the median page-weight range.
Hacker News: A social-news site for the technology and start-up community, launched in February 2007 by Paul Graham at Y Combinator. Server-rendered, written in Arc (a Common Lisp dialect) by Paul Graham and Robert Morris. Famous for the austerity of its design.
Paul Graham: American computer scientist, essayist and venture capitalist. Co-founder of Viaweb (acquired by Yahoo in 1998 to become Yahoo! Store), co-founder of Y Combinator (2005), and creator of the Arc programming language. Designed and wrote Hacker News in 2007.
Robert Morris: American computer scientist, MIT professor, and co-author of Hacker News with Paul Graham. Earlier known as the author of the 1988 Morris worm while a graduate student at Cornell. Co-founded Viaweb with Graham and later Y Combinator.
Arc (language): A Lisp dialect designed by Paul Graham from approximately 2001, with the explicit goal of remaining small and minimal. The language used to write Hacker News. Distinct from any commercial Lisp implementation.
Common Lisp: A standardised, multi-paradigm Lisp dialect formalised in ANSI INCITS 226-1994. Successor to Maclisp and predecessor of Scheme's standardisation effort. Notable for its CLOS object system, condition system, and the wide divergence between implementations.
Phoenix LiveView: A library by Chris McCord (2019) for the Elixir Phoenix web framework that delivers reactivity by streaming HTML diffs over a persistent WebSocket. Renders templates server-side and synchronises the DOM by patches. Single-digit kilobytes on the client.
Laravel Livewire: A full-stack framework by Caleb Porzio (2019) for the Laravel PHP framework. Sends HTML diffs over standard HTTP requests for partial-update reactivity, with a small client-side runtime. Used widely in the Laravel ecosystem to avoid the SPA architecture.
ASP.NET Blazor Server: A Microsoft web framework (2020) that runs C# components on the server and synchronises DOM changes to the browser over a persistent SignalR (WebSocket) connection. Full reactivity without shipping a JavaScript framework to the client.
Cloudflare Pingora: An HTTP proxy framework written in Rust at Cloudflare, replacing the company's previous nginx-and-Lua proxy. Disclosed in a September 2022 blog post. Serves over a trillion requests per day with approximately 70 per cent less CPU and 67 per cent less memory than the previous architecture. Open-sourced as a Rust crate in February 2024.
Cloudflare: American internet-infrastructure company founded in 2009, operating one of the largest content-delivery and edge networks in the world. Author of Pingora, Workers, R2 and the OpenResty-based predecessor to Pingora. Notable for the engineering blog at blog.cloudflare.com.
Pingora: Cloudflare's Rust-based HTTP proxy. See Cloudflare Pingora.
Sonatype: American software-supply-chain security vendor, founded by Jason van Zyl in 2008. Publishes the annual State of the Software Supply Chain report; the 2024 edition counted more than 500,000 malicious packages observed across public registries, with npm carrying the bulk.
Snyk: Israeli-American software-supply-chain security vendor founded by Guy Podjarny, Assaf Hefetz and Danny Grander in 2015. Scans dependency manifests for known vulnerabilities and opens pull requests to upgrade. Part of the supervisory cloud that surrounds public package registries.
Renovate: A dependency-update bot, originally developed by Rhys Arkins as Renovate Bot and acquired by WhiteSource (now Mend.io) in 2019. Comparable in scope to Dependabot but supports more package managers and finer scheduling control. Also part of the supervisory cloud.
left-pad: An eleven-line npm module by Azer Koçulu that prepended a string with a given character. In March 2016, the maintainer unpublished it (and 250 other packages) after a name dispute with another company. The unpublication broke thousands of build pipelines worldwide and triggered npm's policy change forbidding unpublication of widely-depended-on packages.
event-stream: An npm streams library by Dominic Tarr. In November 2018, after a maintainer handover to a new contributor, the package was modified to inject a credential-stealing dependency targeting the Copay Bitcoin wallet. The supply-chain attack ran for two months before discovery.
ua-parser-js: A widely-used npm User-Agent parsing library by Faisal Salman. In October 2021, the npm account of the maintainer was compromised; three malicious versions were published containing a credential stealer and a cryptocurrency miner, with millions of weekly downloads exposed before remediation.
Jake Archibald: British software engineer at Google's Chrome Developer Relations team. Author of the canonical 2017 case study 'Netflix and React', documenting the +50 per cent Time to Interactive improvement Netflix achieved on its logged-out homepage by removing client-side React entirely.
Netflix logged-out homepage: The 2017 case study by Jake Archibald documenting how Netflix removed client-side React from its logged-out landing page and rebuilt the language switcher in fewer than 300 lines of vanilla JavaScript. Time to Interactive improved by more than 50 per cent. Frequently cited as the canonical case for the cost of unnecessary client-side rendering.
Sustainable Web Manifesto: A statement of principles for web professionals committing to a sustainable web, drafted by Tim Frick, Tom Greenwood, Chris Adams and Jack Lenox in 2019. Hosts a reference dataset on the energy consumption of typical web traffic and informed the W3C Web Sustainability Guidelines.
W3C Web Sustainability Guidelines: A draft set of recommendations published by the W3C Sustainable Web Design Community Group at w3c.github.io/sustyweb. Documents the relationship between page weight, client-side rendering, datacentre energy use and the embodied carbon of web infrastructure.
On Second Thought Episode 07: The seventh episode of Vivian Voss's 'On Second Thought' series, published 10 May 2026. Examines why every modern web project ships JavaScript to render content the server already had. Covers the origin (Brendan Eich's ten-day language in 1995, AJAX in 2005, the Backbone-Angular-React generation, the SPA as default), the cost (697 KB median in 2025, terawatt-hours of unnecessary draw, the dependency cloud and the supervisory cloud above it), and seven proofs in production: Wikipedia on PHP, GOV.UK with progressive enhancement, Hacker News in Arc, HEY on Hotwire, HTMX at 14 KB, Netflix removing client React for +50 per cent TTI, and Cloudflare Pingora replacing nginx-and-Lua at 70 per cent less CPU.
netstat: A Unix command-line tool for listing sockets, the routing table, network interfaces and protocol statistics, shipped with the 4.2BSD networking release in August 1983. On FreeBSD, netstat asks the kernel through sysctl pcblist and formats the answer at its output; on Linux, the net-tools netstat reads /proc/net/tcp and similar pre-rendered ASCII files. Same name, two architectures.
ss (tool): A Linux command-line tool for socket statistics, part of the iproute2 package, written by Alexey Kuznetsov. Replaces the older net-tools netstat by asking netlink (NETLINK_INET_DIAG / NETLINK_SOCK_DIAG) for structured queries instead of reading and re-parsing /proc/net/tcp. Survives in production on workloads of sixty thousand sockets or more where the old netstat sits and thinks.
sockstat: A FreeBSD command-line tool for listing open sockets, in the base system since FreeBSD 3.0 (1998). Reads the same sysctl pcblist tree as netstat but presents a focused socket-and-process view rather than netstat's routing-and-statistics surface area. Commonly invoked as sockstat -4l for IPv4 listeners or sockstat -p 443 for everything on a given port.
/proc filesystem: A pseudo-filesystem on Linux (mounted at /proc) that exposes kernel and process state as readable text files. Inherited from Plan 9 and SVR4 and adopted by Linux in 1992. Useful as a debugging and scripting surface; not designed for structured queries, which is why /proc/net/tcp can be cat-ed and grep-ed but cannot be asked.
/proc/net/tcp: A Linux pseudo-file under /proc that lists every active TCP socket, one per line, in a fixed ASCII format. Readable by humans and the old net-tools netstat alike; not askable, in the sense that there is no syntax for 'sockets on port 443'. The tool behind it must read the file whole, parse every line, and filter in userspace. The reason ss had to be written.
sysctl: A BSD interface for reading and modifying kernel state through a name-tree, available as a C library (sysctl(3)) and a command (sysctl(8)). On FreeBSD the network stack exposes protocol control blocks under net.inet.tcp.pcblist, net.inet.udp.pcblist and the net.local.* tree. A sysctl call returns a structured binary array, designed to be queried by a tool. Linux has a sysctl namespace too, but historically used /proc for socket data.
netlink: A Linux kernel-to-userspace IPC mechanism, designed by Alexey Kuznetsov for structured queries against kernel subsystems. Implemented as a socket family (AF_NETLINK). Used for routing (NETLINK_ROUTE), socket diagnostics (NETLINK_INET_DIAG / NETLINK_SOCK_DIAG), audit (NETLINK_AUDIT), and many others. The askable interface that ss talks to and that /proc/net is not.
NETLINK_INET_DIAG: A netlink family added in Linux 2.6.14 (October 2005) that exposes IPv4 and IPv6 socket information as a queryable interface. Replaces the read-the-whole-file pattern of /proc/net/tcp with a structured request/response model. Used by ss and a handful of other tools.
NETLINK_SOCK_DIAG: A netlink family added in Linux 3.3 (March 2012) that generalises NETLINK_INET_DIAG to all socket families, including AF_UNIX (Unix-domain sockets). Allows kernel-side filtering of socket queries and returns only the matching records. Documented in sock_diag(7).
iproute2: A Linux networking-tools package started by Alexey Kuznetsov in the late 1990s as a replacement for the BSD-style net-tools. Includes ip, ss, tc and a handful of related utilities. Talks to the kernel through netlink rather than /proc, and is the package most distributions now recommend over net-tools.
net-tools: A Linux networking-tools package containing netstat, ifconfig, arp, route and a handful of other tools, written in the early 1990s by Fred N. van Kempen and contributors as a BSD-shaped userland for Linux. Reads /proc/net for its data. No new release since 2011. Maintainer-driven deprecation attempt in 2009; most distributions now default to iproute2.
Alexey Kuznetsov: Russian Linux kernel developer, widely credited with much of the modern Linux network stack: substantial portions of the IPv4 and IPv6 implementations, the QoS (traffic control) subsystem, and the netlink IPC mechanism. Started the iproute2 package and wrote ss as the netlink-based replacement for the net-tools netstat.
Fred N. van Kempen: Dutch Linux developer, principal author of much of the early Linux networking userland (net-tools, including netstat and ifconfig), in the early 1990s. Tools modelled on the BSD originals but implemented from scratch against the Linux /proc filesystem.
4.2BSD: The fourth Berkeley Software Distribution of the Unix operating system, released in August 1983 by the Computer Systems Research Group at the University of California, Berkeley. Introduced TCP/IP networking, the Berkeley sockets API, the Fast File System, signals, and a long list of administrative tools including netstat and netstat's vocabulary.
Sam Leffler: American software engineer. Member of the Computer Systems Research Group at Berkeley in the early 1980s, contributing to the 4.2BSD networking work alongside Bill Joy. Later author of libtiff and a major contributor to FreeBSD's wireless networking stack.
CSRG: Computer Systems Research Group at the University of California, Berkeley. Active from 1980 to 1995, responsible for the Berkeley Software Distribution (BSD) releases including 4.2BSD (TCP/IP, 1983), 4.3BSD (1986) and 4.4BSD-Lite (1994). The progenitors, by descent, of FreeBSD, NetBSD, OpenBSD, DragonFly BSD and macOS's BSD-derived userland.
Protocol Control Block (PCB): A kernel-internal data structure that holds the per-socket state for a network protocol: addresses, ports, sequence numbers, references to the protocol implementation and so on. FreeBSD exposes its in-kernel PCB lists through sysctl (net.inet.tcp.pcblist, net.inet.udp.pcblist, net.local.stream.pcblist and so on), allowing netstat to fetch all sockets in one structured call.
KISS principle: Keep It Simple, Stupid. Design maxim attributed to Kelly Johnson at Lockheed Skunk Works in 1960, widely adopted in software engineering. In Unix practice, applied as one tool, one job; one ask, one answer; the smallest interface that solves the problem and no more.
kvm (BSD library): On BSD and FreeBSD, the kernel virtual memory access library (kvm(3)), used by tools such as ps, w and the older netstat to read kernel data structures directly. Largely superseded by sysctl for everyday work, though kvm remains the path for crash-dump analysis and similar low-level tooling.
The Unix Way Episode 16: The sixteenth episode of Vivian Voss's 'The Unix Way' series, published 11 May 2026. Compares the two architectures of netstat: the FreeBSD tool that has been in base since 4.2BSD shipped TCP/IP in August 1983, asking the kernel through sysctl pcblist; and the Linux net-tools netstat that reads /proc/net/tcp as a kernel-rendered ASCII dump. Documents Alexey Kuznetsov's restoration of the Unix pattern with ss (NETLINK_INET_DIAG in Linux 2.6.14, October 2005, generalised to NETLINK_SOCK_DIAG in Linux 3.3, March 2012). The architectural lesson: the Unix text interface lives in the tool's mouth, not in a file that looks like one.
tcpdump: A command-line packet analyser, written in 1988 by Van Jacobson, Craig Leres and Steven McCanne at the Network Research Group of Lawrence Berkeley Laboratory. Captures and prints traffic on a network interface according to a small filter language. A thin userspace tool sitting on libpcap and BPF; the technical beauty is the layering, not the tool. Source maintained today at tcpdump.org by The Tcpdump Group.
libpcap: A C library extracted from tcpdump around 1995 by the Network Research Group at Lawrence Berkeley Laboratory. Provides a portable API for opening packet-capture devices, compiling filter expressions into BPF programs, reading captured packets and parsing the .pcap file format. The shared capture backend of Wireshark, tshark, Zeek, snort, suricata, nmap, ngrep, dsniff and most modern observability tooling.
BSD Packet Filter (BPF): A register-based in-kernel pseudo-machine for evaluating predicates on incoming packets, designed by Steven McCanne and Van Jacobson in 1992. Replaces Sun's stack-based Network Interface Tap and similar predecessors. A libpcap-compiled BPF program is uploaded to the kernel once at the start of a capture session; the kernel runs it on every packet and copies only matches to userspace. Documented in 'The BSD Packet Filter: A New Architecture for User-level Packet Capture' (USENIX Winter 1993, Best Student Paper). The architectural foundation under tcpdump, libpcap and every modern descendant including eBPF.
Van Jacobson: American computer scientist. Co-author of tcpdump (1988) and BPF (1992) at the Network Research Group of Lawrence Berkeley Laboratory. Author of the SIGCOMM '88 paper 'Congestion Avoidance and Control' which introduced slow start, fast retransmit and the congestion-window heuristics that keep TCP from collapsing under load. One of the principal architects of the modern Internet.
Craig Leres: American computer scientist at the Network Research Group of Lawrence Berkeley Laboratory. Co-author with Van Jacobson and Steven McCanne of tcpdump in 1988, and a long-time maintainer of libpcap and the LBL networking utilities. Co-founded the Tcpdump Group around tcpdump.org in 1999.
Steven McCanne: American computer scientist. As a graduate student at Lawrence Berkeley Laboratory, co-author of tcpdump in 1988 and lead author with Van Jacobson of 'The BSD Packet Filter' (USENIX Winter 1993, Best Student Paper). Later co-founded the streaming-media company Inktomi-spin-off FastForward Networks (1998) and Riverbed Technology (2002).
Alexei Starovoitov: Russian-American kernel engineer, formerly at PLUMgrid and Facebook, now at Meta. Principal architect of extended BPF (eBPF); shipped the first eBPF infrastructure in Linux 3.18 in December 2014 with a wider 64-bit register file, new instructions, an in-kernel verifier, and attachment points beyond packet capture (system calls, tracepoints, kprobes, network hooks).
Lawrence Berkeley Laboratory (LBL): United States Department of Energy national laboratory in Berkeley, California, managed by the University of California. Home of the Network Research Group, where Van Jacobson, Craig Leres, Steven McCanne and their colleagues produced tcpdump, libpcap, BPF and a substantial fraction of the operational toolset for the early Internet. Now formally Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory (LBNL).
tcpdump.org: The project home for tcpdump and libpcap since 1999, founded by Michael Richardson and Bill Fenner after the original LBL maintainers stepped back. The Tcpdump Group is the umbrella under which both source trees are maintained today.
Michael Richardson: Canadian networking engineer at Sandelman Software Works. Co-founder of tcpdump.org in 1999 and long-time maintainer of tcpdump and libpcap. Active contributor to the IETF on routing, IoT and security topics.
Bill Fenner: American computer scientist, co-founder of tcpdump.org in 1999. Active IETF contributor; chair of the OSPF working group; author or co-author of several IPv4 multicast RFCs (PIM, IGMP). Previously at AT&T Labs Research.
Sun NIT: Network Interface Tap. A packet-capture mechanism in early SunOS, ancestor of the BPF approach. Evaluated a stack-based filter expression inside the kernel. The 1993 BPF paper measured McCanne and Jacobson's register-based pseudo-machine at up to a hundred times faster than NIT on the same hardware.
etherfind: An early Sun packet-capture utility for SunOS. The first version of tcpdump borrowed code from etherfind before McCanne's rewrites introduced the BPF model and libpcap factored the capture path out into a portable library.
Wireshark: A free open-source network-protocol analyser, originally released as Ethereal by Gerald Combs in 1998 and renamed Wireshark in 2006. Reads and writes the libpcap .pcap and .pcapng file formats; uses libpcap (or its Windows counterpart) as the capture backend. Packet dissectors can be written as C plugins or as Lua scripts.
tshark: The command-line counterpart to Wireshark, shipping in the same source tree. Reads and writes .pcap and .pcapng files, dissects packets to text or PDML, and uses libpcap as the capture backend.
dumpcap: A small dedicated capture utility shipped with Wireshark. Uses libpcap, writes .pcap or .pcapng files; designed to run with the elevated privileges needed for capture so that the heavier dissection engines (Wireshark, tshark) can run unprivileged.
Zeek: An open-source network-security monitor, originally released as Bro by Vern Paxson at Lawrence Berkeley Laboratory in 1995, renamed Zeek in 2018. Provides a high-level scripting language for traffic analysis on top of libpcap. Widely deployed in research, government and enterprise security-operations environments.
snort: An open-source intrusion-detection and intrusion-prevention system, originally released by Martin Roesch in 1998 and acquired by Cisco via Sourcefire in 2013. Uses libpcap for capture; carries one of the largest community signature sets in network security.
suricata: An open-source intrusion-detection, intrusion-prevention and network-security-monitoring engine, released in 2010 by the Open Information Security Foundation (OISF) as a multi-threaded alternative to snort. Compatible with snort's rule syntax; uses libpcap and AF_PACKET for capture, with AF_XDP and PF_RING options on Linux.
nmap: A network exploration and security-auditing tool released by Gordon Lyon (Fyodor) in 1997. Uses libpcap and raw sockets for OS fingerprinting, port scanning and service detection. Famously cameos in The Matrix Reloaded, Trinity using nmap and SSH to compromise a power-grid host.
ngrep: A text-search tool for packet payloads, by Jordan Ritter, released in 1999. Combines libpcap capture with grep-style regular-expression matching on application-layer bytes. Useful when one wants to see what is on the wire as text rather than as a protocol decode.
dsniff: A network auditing and penetration-testing toolkit by Dug Song, released in 2000. Built on libpcap; reconstructs application-layer streams from captured packets for protocols such as HTTP, FTP, POP, IMAP and SMTP. Influential in early 2000s practical demonstrations of why unencrypted protocols were unwise.
PF_PACKET: The Linux socket family for raw access to incoming and outgoing link-layer frames. Used by libpcap on Linux for capture in the absence of a /dev/bpf device. Sometimes spelled AF_PACKET, the two are interchangeable as constants. Documented in packet(7).
/dev/bpf: The BSD character-device interface to the BSD Packet Filter, present on FreeBSD, OpenBSD, NetBSD and macOS as /dev/bpfN entries. libpcap on these systems opens a /dev/bpf device, attaches it to a network interface, uploads a compiled BPF program, and reads matching packets back. The kernel side lives in sys/net/bpf.c.
pcap (file format): The de facto packet-capture file format, defined by the libpcap library and used by tcpdump, Wireshark and the bulk of the modern observability ecosystem. A simple binary container: a global header, then one record header plus packet bytes per captured packet. A .pcap file recorded on FreeBSD in 1996 can still be opened by Wireshark on a Linux laptop in 2026. The newer .pcapng format adds metadata (per-interface info, comments, name resolution) while remaining backward-compatible in spirit.
Linux Socket Filtering (LSF): The Linux kernel's original BPF-derived filter mechanism, predating eBPF. Allows BPF programs to be attached to sockets to filter incoming packets in the kernel, structurally borrowed from McCanne and Jacobson's 1992 design and explicitly credited as such in the kernel documentation. Superseded by eBPF for most uses but still present in the kernel as the classic-BPF execution path.
Linux 3.18: The Linux kernel release of December 2014. Notable for shipping the first version of extended BPF (eBPF) by Alexei Starovoitov, with a wider register file, a verifier and new instructions; the foundation of contemporary Linux observability and networking tools such as bpftrace, bcc and Cilium.
bpftrace: A high-level tracing language for Linux, modelled on DTrace's D language and implemented on top of eBPF. Released by Alastair Robertson (then at the University of Manchester) in 2018 and now maintained by the IO Visor Project under the Linux Foundation. Brendan Gregg published the canonical material on its use.
bcc: BPF Compiler Collection. A toolkit for building BPF-based tracing tools on Linux, released by the IO Visor Project (originally from PLUMgrid) in 2015. Includes Python and Lua bindings and a long list of ready-made command-line tools (execsnoop, opensnoop, biolatency, tcptop and many more). Predecessor in spirit to bpftrace and still widely used where a Python wrapper around a custom BPF program is appropriate.
Cilium: An open-source networking, security and observability platform for Kubernetes and Linux, released by Isovalent in 2017 (now part of Cisco). Built entirely on eBPF; replaces kube-proxy, provides L7 policy, transparent encryption, service-mesh-grade observability and load balancing. The flagship eBPF success story in the cloud-native ecosystem.
Technical Beauty Episode 35: The thirty-fifth episode of Vivian Voss's 'Technical Beauty' series, published 12 May 2026. Tcpdump as a thin command-line filter sitting on libpcap, sitting on the BSD Packet Filter. Documents the origin (Van Jacobson, Craig Leres, Steven McCanne at LBL in 1988), the BPF paper (McCanne and Jacobson, December 1992; USENIX Winter 1993 Best Student Paper; up to 100x faster than Sun's NIT), the descendants on libpcap (Wireshark, tshark, dumpcap, Zeek, snort, suricata, nmap, ngrep, dsniff), the FreeBSD base-system home (/dev/bpfN, /usr/src/contrib/tcpdump, /usr/src/contrib/libpcap) and the Linux branch (PF_PACKET, then eBPF by Alexei Starovoitov in Linux 3.18, December 2014). The technical beauty is the layering, not the tool.
WAF: Web Application Firewall. An inline filter, usually deployed in front of HTTP services, that inspects requests and responses against a rule set to block common attack classes (SQL injection, cross-site scripting, path traversal, command injection). Implemented as either a reverse proxy (Cloudflare, AWS WAF, F5, ModSecurity behind nginx or Apache) or as a library inside the application.
Cloudflare WAF: Cloudflare's managed Web Application Firewall, layered into the Cloudflare reverse proxy. Implemented in Lua atop OpenResty and PCRE for regex matching, with managed rulesets distributed to the global edge via Quicksilver. The 2 July 2019 outage was triggered by one such managed rule whose forty-five-character XSS-detection pattern contained a catastrophically backtracking sub-expression.
Cloudflare 2 July 2019 outage: A 27-minute global outage of the Cloudflare network on Tuesday 2 July 2019, 13:42 UTC to 14:09 UTC, caused by the deployment of one managed WAF rule whose XSS-detection regex (?:.*=.*) triggered exponential PCRE backtracking on inputs without an equals sign. CPU saturation on every edge core; control plane and dashboard sharing fate with the saturated network; WAF re-enabled at 14:52 UTC after rollback. Documented in the John Graham-Cumming postmortem of 12 July 2019.
ReDoS: Regular Expression Denial of Service. A class of denial-of-service attack in which an attacker crafts input that triggers exponential backtracking in a regex engine, exhausting CPU and starving the affected service. Possible only with backtracking matchers (PCRE, JavaScript's RegExp, Python's re); linear-time engines such as RE2 and the Rust regex crate refuse exponential patterns at compile time.
Catastrophic backtracking: A pathological execution path in a backtracking regex matcher in which the engine, after an initial failure, retraces through every possible decomposition of the input.
