Triggered classically by patterns of the shape .*.*=.* against inputs that do not contain the literal: two greedy quantifiers in sequence give O(n^2) splits to try; deeper variants of the pattern escalate the worst case to exponential. The name 'catastrophic' is a term of art in regex engineering, not hyperbole.
PCRE: Perl Compatible Regular Expressions. A C regex library by Philip Hazel, originally released in 1997 and modelled on the Perl 5 regex syntax. The dominant backtracking matcher in production: nginx, OpenResty, Apache mod_rewrite, PHP, R, and a long tail of others. Powerful (supports backreferences, lookaround, named captures) but has no built-in CPU budget and can take exponential time on adversarial inputs.
RE2: An open-source regular-expression engine released by Google in 2010, written by Russ Cox and others. Implements a Thompson-NFA execution model that runs in time linear in the input and refuses at compile time to accept patterns whose worst case is exponential. Does not support backreferences (the price of the linearity guarantee). Used by Google's search infrastructure and now widely adopted in security-sensitive code paths.
Rust regex crate: The standard regular-expression library for the Rust programming language, maintained by Andrew Gallant. Combines a Thompson-NFA primary engine with a backtracking fallback for very small patterns and uses a careful set of literal optimisations; runs in linear time and refuses exponential patterns at compile time. The crate is the regex backend in many security-sensitive Rust services, including Cloudflare's post-2019 WAF stack.
Quicksilver: Cloudflare's distributed key-value store for edge configuration, deployed to every Cloudflare data centre and used to propagate configuration changes worldwide in seconds. Documented in the Cloudflare engineering blog 'Introducing Quicksilver: configuration distribution at internet scale' (2020). A feature for emergency kill switches; a liability when used as the default rollout mechanism for ordinary changes, as the 2 July 2019 outage demonstrated.
rctl(8): FreeBSD's resource-control utility, introduced in FreeBSD 9.0 (2012). Enforces per-process, per-user, per-jail and per-login-class limits on CPU time, memory, file descriptors, open files, swap usage and more. Rules are written as a triple of subject, resource and action (deny, log, throttle); for example rctl -a jail:waf:cputime:deny=200ms kills any process inside the named jail that consumes more than 200 milliseconds of CPU.
ulimit: A POSIX shell built-in for setting per-process resource limits at the time of process creation. ulimit -t bounds CPU time in seconds; ulimit -v bounds virtual memory; ulimit -n bounds file descriptors. Coarser than FreeBSD rctl but available on every Unix; the standard way to cap an unbounded subprocess from its parent shell.
John Graham-Cumming: British computer scientist, CTO of Cloudflare from 2017 onward (previously vice-president of engineering). Author of the public Cloudflare postmortem of the 2 July 2019 WAF outage, dated 12 July 2019. Earlier in his career, the principal author of the open-source POPFile spam filter and the campaign that led to the 2009 UK government apology to Alan Turing.
Philip Hazel: British software engineer at the University of Cambridge, author of PCRE (1997) and Exim (1995). Maintained PCRE as a single-handed open-source project for over two decades. PCRE2, the successor library released in 2015, is widely used in nginx, PHP, R, the JIT engines of many runtimes and a large portion of the production internet.
Russ Cox: Software engineer at Google, member of the Go core team. Principal author of the RE2 regular-expression engine (2010) and of the long-form essay series 'Regular Expression Matching Can Be Simple And Fast' (2007 onward) that re-popularised Ken Thompson's 1968 linear-time NFA construction. Also authored the canonical day-by-day timeline of the XZ Utils backdoor at research.swtch.com/xz-timeline.
NFA (regex): Non-deterministic Finite Automaton, the abstract machine class on which linear-time regex matchers are built. Ken Thompson's 1968 construction (CACM, 'Programming Techniques: Regular Expression Search Algorithm') compiles a regex into an NFA that can be simulated in O(n*m) time, where n is the input length and m the pattern size. RE2 and the Rust regex crate descend directly from this work.
Cross-site scripting (XSS): A class of web-application vulnerabilities in which attacker-controlled content is reflected by the server (or stored and later served) without adequate escaping, causing it to be executed as script in the victim's browser. Defended against by output encoding, Content Security Policy (CSP), and, as a second line, WAF rules that block payloads at the edge.
OpenResty: An nginx variant with embedded LuaJIT, originally developed by Yichun Zhang at Taobao in 2009 and later commercialised by OpenResty Inc. Used as the host runtime for many production WAFs, including Cloudflare's pre-2019 WAF; combines nginx's event loop with Lua's scripting flexibility and PCRE for regex matching.
ModSecurity: An open-source Web Application Firewall originally released by Ivan Ristić in 2002. Implemented as a module for Apache, then nginx and IIS; rules are written in a domain-specific language and matched against requests in the request hot path. The OWASP Core Rule Set (CRS) is the canonical community rule set. Reference example of the WAF-on-PCRE pattern that the Cloudflare 2019 outage illustrated.
AWS WAF: Amazon Web Services Web Application Firewall, deployed in front of Application Load Balancers, CloudFront distributions and API Gateway endpoints. Configurable with managed rules from AWS Marketplace vendors as well as customer-authored rules. Same architectural class as Cloudflare and ModSecurity; same exposure to ReDoS in custom regex patterns.
Suricata: An open-source intrusion-detection, intrusion-prevention and network-security-monitoring engine, released by the Open Information Security Foundation in 2010 as a multi-threaded alternative to Snort. Compatible with Snort's rule syntax; uses libpcap and AF_PACKET for capture. Rule sets routinely include PCRE patterns matched against extracted strings, so the same ReDoS exposure applies in the intrusion-detection tier.
Splunk: An American observability and log-analytics platform, founded by Erik Swan, Rob Das and Michael Baum in 2003 and acquired by Cisco in March 2024. Ingests log streams, indexes them and exposes a query language (SPL) over the index. Field extraction relies heavily on regex; a pathological log line can stall the parsing tier.
Elastic: The company behind Elasticsearch, Logstash and Kibana (the ELK stack). Founded by Shay Banon in 2012; renamed Elastic in 2018. The Logstash component performs regex-based parsing of inbound log lines, so a pathological message can melt the ingestion tier in the same shape as Splunk's.
Datadog: An American monitoring, observability and security platform, founded by Olivier Pomel and Alexis Lê-Quôc in 2010. Ingests metrics, traces and logs from instrumented services; the log pipeline performs regex-based parsing and routing in the request path, with the same ReDoS exposure as Splunk and Elastic.
F5 BIG-IP: An American application-delivery controller and Web Application Firewall by F5, Inc. Hardware appliance or virtual edition; the WAF feature (ASM, more recently Advanced WAF) is deployed in front of HTTP services to block common attack classes. Same architectural class as Cloudflare WAF, AWS WAF and ModSecurity.
Tales from the Bare Metal Episode 03: The third episode of Vivian Voss's 'Tales from the Bare Metal' series, published 13 May 2026. Documents the Cloudflare WAF outage of 2 July 2019: 13:42 to 14:09 UTC, 27 minutes of HTTP traffic stopped on a service that fronts a sizeable share of the public internet. Traces the cause to a forty-five-character managed XSS-detection regex whose sub-expression (?:.*=.*) triggers catastrophic PCRE backtracking, and to three systemic conditions: a test harness without CPU-time profiling, a regex engine without a runtime budget after an earlier guard had been removed, and a Quicksilver-based deployment path with no staged rollout. Documents Cloudflare's announced preventive measures (RE2 or Rust regex, reinstated CPU-usage protection, CI profiling, staged rollout SOP, control-plane separation) and the parallel FreeBSD answer (rctl(8) for CPU and memory caps, jails for blast-radius containment, Capsicum for capability sandboxing).
Wire Fire: Vivian Voss's interruptive blog and LinkedIn series, started in May 2026, dedicated to documenting active supply-chain and infrastructure incidents in close to real time. Episode 01 (14 May 2026) chronicles the six-week npm crisis from the 31 March 2026 axios backdoor to the 12 May 2026 publication of the Shai-Hulud worm source. The format pre-empts the regular series schedule when an event warrants immediate coverage.
axios: A widely-used JavaScript HTTP client library, originally released by Matt Zabriskie in 2014, since 2020 maintained by the axios-http organisation on GitHub. Roughly 100 million weekly downloads on npm at the time of the March 2026 compromise. Versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 were backdoored for approximately three hours on 31 March 2026 via a malicious postinstall dependency (plain-crypto-js@4.2.1).
Shai-Hulud: A self-replicating npm supply-chain worm, named after the sandworm of Frank Herbert's 'Dune'. First observed in September 2025 against ~500 packages, escalating in November 2025 to compromise approximately 25,000 GitHub repositories. The 'Mini Shai-Hulud' variant operating from April 2026 onward chains pull_request_target Pwn Requests, GitHub Actions cache poisoning and OIDC token extraction to publish poisoned versions automatically. The fully weaponised source code was published openly on 12 May 2026, making the toolkit off-the-shelf.
Mini Shai-Hulud: A stripped-down 2026 variant of the Shai-Hulud worm, observed first against four SAP-related npm packages on 29 April 2026 and then against the TanStack ecosystem on 11 May 2026. Uses three chained GitHub Actions weaknesses (pull_request_target Pwn Requests, cache poisoning, OIDC token extraction) to spread without manual intervention. The Wiz, StepSecurity and Aikido incident reports document the propagation in detail.
TanStack: A suite of headless JavaScript libraries for state management, routing, querying and table rendering, maintained by Tanner Linsley since 2017. Includes React Query (now TanStack Query), TanStack Router, TanStack Table and TanStack Form. The flagship @tanstack/react-router ships roughly 12 million weekly downloads. The 11 May 2026 Mini Shai-Hulud wave compromised 84 versions across 42 @tanstack/* packages between 19:20 and 19:26 UTC.
@tanstack/react-router: A type-safe JavaScript router for React applications, part of the TanStack suite. Roughly 12 million weekly downloads. The flagship casualty of the 11 May 2026 Mini Shai-Hulud npm wave.
CISA: Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency. A United States federal agency under the Department of Homeland Security, established in 2018. Publishes vulnerability alerts and advisories at cisa.gov; issued the official advisory on the axios npm supply-chain compromise of 31 March 2026.
Sapphire Sleet: Microsoft Threat Intelligence's tracking designation for a Democratic People's Republic of Korea (DPRK) state-aligned threat actor cluster, active since approximately 2020. Attribution for the 31 March 2026 axios npm supply-chain compromise. Microsoft uses a meteorological taxonomy (Sleet, Typhoon, Blizzard, Sandstorm) for nation-state clusters as of the April 2023 naming reform.
UNC1069: Mandiant's tracking designation for an uncategorised North Korea-aligned threat cluster. Mandiant uses UNC (UNCategorised) prefixes for activity sets that have not yet been promoted to a named APT. UNC1069 is the parallel attribution Mandiant assigned to the same actor that Microsoft tracks as Sapphire Sleet for the 31 March 2026 axios incident.
Mandiant: An American cybersecurity firm, founded by Kevin Mandia in 2004, acquired by Google in 2022 and integrated into Google Cloud's threat intelligence stack. Maintains the UNC/APT actor taxonomy widely cited in incident response. Published the parallel attribution of the 31 March 2026 axios compromise to UNC1069.
Wiz: An Israeli-American cloud-security vendor founded by Assaf Rappaport, Yinon Costica, Roy Reznik and Ami Luttwak in 2020, headquartered in Tel Aviv and New York. Specialises in agentless cloud-workload security. Wiz Research published the detailed reconstruction of the 11 May 2026 Mini Shai-Hulud TanStack wave, including the 19:20-19:26 UTC window and the 84-versions-across-42-packages count.
StepSecurity: A United States-based cybersecurity company specialising in GitHub Actions and CI/CD pipeline security. Provides a hardened runner product (Harden-Runner) and publishes incident analyses at stepsecurity.io/blog. Authored the technical reconstruction of the three GitHub Actions weaknesses chained in the 11 May 2026 Mini Shai-Hulud automation.
Aikido Security: A Belgian application-security platform, founded by Willem Delbare and Felix Garriau in 2022 and headquartered in Ghent. Combines SAST, SCA, container scanning and cloud-posture management in a single product. Published the post-incident analysis of the destructive 'rm -rf ~/' payload variant in the May 2026 Mini Shai-Hulud wave.
Unit 42: Palo Alto Networks's threat-research division, founded in 2014. Publishes incident analyses, malware reports and APT tracking at unit42.paloaltonetworks.com. Source for the 2025 baseline statistic that more than 99 per cent of all open-source malware now targets npm.
vx-underground: An online malware-research aggregator and library, run as a not-for-profit project since approximately 2019. Hosts a large public archive of malware samples and incident write-ups; widely consulted as a reference by professional incident-response teams. Reported on 12 May 2026 that the Shai-Hulud worm source code had been published openly.
VuXML: The FreeBSD Vulnerability and Exposure Markup Language, the structured XML feed of security advisories for the FreeBSD base system and Ports collection, maintained by the FreeBSD Ports Security Team at vuxml.freebsd.org. Each advisory is tied to specific port versions and consumed by the pkg audit tool to flag vulnerable packages in production.
FreeBSD Ports collection: The FreeBSD project's curated software collection, containing build recipes for tens of thousands of third-party applications. Each port has a named human maintainer, is reviewed before commit by the Ports Management Team, and is tracked in VuXML for advisories. Distfiles are checksum-verified and increasingly signed. The architectural contrast to npm's open-publish model has existed since the mid-1990s.
pull_request_target: A GitHub Actions workflow trigger that runs in the context of the target repository (not the forking one), giving the workflow access to repository secrets. Designed for legitimate use cases such as automated labelling on pull requests from forks. Frequently misconfigured: when combined with an unsanitised checkout of the pull request's HEAD, an attacker can run arbitrary code with target-repository privileges. The class of misconfiguration is informally known as a 'Pwn Request'.
Pwn Request: An exploit class against GitHub Actions workflows configured with pull_request_target plus an unsanitised checkout of the forked branch. A malicious pull request can run code with the target repository's secrets and credentials, including OIDC tokens. The 11 May 2026 Mini Shai-Hulud automation used this class as its primary foothold.
GitHub Actions cache poisoning: An exploit class against GitHub Actions's per-repository build cache, in which an attacker submits a cache entry under a key that a subsequent legitimate workflow run will read, causing the legitimate run to use attacker-controlled artefacts as if they were trusted. Documented in StepSecurity research from 2024 onward and chained in the 11 May 2026 Mini Shai-Hulud automation.
OIDC token extraction: An attack class in which an attacker with code-execution inside a GitHub Actions workflow extracts the short-lived OpenID Connect token issued by GitHub to authenticate the workflow to external services (npm, AWS, GCP, Azure). The token is used to publish or deploy on behalf of the workflow before its short lifetime expires. One of the three GitHub Actions weaknesses chained in the 11 May 2026 npm worm wave.
PyPI: The Python Package Index, the canonical registry of public Python packages at pypi.org, operated by the Python Software Foundation. Functionally comparable to npm for the Python ecosystem; targeted alongside npm in the 11 May 2026 Mini Shai-Hulud wave (172 packages, 403 versions across both registries within 48 hours).
OpenSearch: An open-source search and analytics suite, forked from Elasticsearch 7.10 in 2021 by Amazon Web Services after Elastic relicensed Elasticsearch under SSPL. Maintained by the OpenSearch Foundation since 2024. The npm packages of the OpenSearch JavaScript client were among the components touched by the 11 May 2026 Mini Shai-Hulud wave.
Guardrails AI: An open-source Python library and platform for validating and structuring large-language-model outputs, founded by Shreya Rajpal in 2023. The npm bridge packages and CLI tooling were among the components touched by the 11 May 2026 Mini Shai-Hulud wave.
npm postinstall hook: A package lifecycle script in the package.json file that npm executes automatically after installing a package, running arbitrary code with the permissions of the user invoking npm install. The standard execution surface for supply-chain malware delivered through compromised packages; the 31 March 2026 axios backdoor and the 11 May 2026 Mini Shai-Hulud wave both used postinstall.
npm ignore-scripts: An npm configuration option (npm config set ignore-scripts true) that disables the execution of all package lifecycle scripts (preinstall, install, postinstall, prepublish and so on) during install operations. The standard defensive measure against postinstall-based supply-chain malware; recommended in Cloudflare, Wiz and StepSecurity guidance after the May 2026 npm worm wave.
DPRK: Democratic People's Republic of Korea. State sponsor of multiple cybercrime and espionage operations tracked under designations including Sapphire Sleet (Microsoft) and UNC1069 (Mandiant). Attribution for the 31 March 2026 axios npm supply-chain compromise.
The Register: A British online technology publication, founded by John Lettice, Mike Magee and Ross Alderson in 1994 in London. Known for its plain-prose technical reporting and dry editorial voice. Carried the early reporting on the 29 April 2026 Mini Shai-Hulud SAP-package wave.
The Hacker News: An Indian online cybersecurity news publication, founded by Mohit Kumar in 2010 and headquartered in New Delhi. Active aggregator for incident reporting; published the cumulative 172-packages-and-518M-downloads figure for the May 2026 Mini Shai-Hulud wave.
Wire Fire Episode 01: The first episode of Vivian Voss's 'Wire Fire' series, published 14 May 2026. Documents the six-week npm supply-chain crisis from the 31 March 2026 axios backdoor through the 29 April Mini Shai-Hulud SAP wave and the 11 May TanStack wave (84 versions across 42 packages in six minutes, scaling to 172 packages and 403 versions across npm and PyPI in 48 hours, ~518M cumulative downloads) to the 12 May watershed when the fully weaponised Shai-Hulud source went public. Covers the 2025 baseline (454,648 malicious npm packages; more than 99 per cent of all open-source malware targets npm), the propagation mechanics of the worm, the three chained GitHub Actions weaknesses (pull_request_target Pwn Request, Actions cache poisoning, OIDC token extraction), the actions for today, and the architectural contrast with the FreeBSD Ports model (VuXML, named maintainers, human review, Capsicum, jails).
Amazon Aurora: A managed relational database engine offered by AWS, launched in 2015, marketed as 'PostgreSQL-compatible' and 'MySQL-compatible'. On the wire and at the SQL surface this is largely true; underneath, Aurora is its own database. Storage is AWS' six-way replicated, log-structured shared-storage fabric, not PostgreSQL's. Aurora-specific features (Babelfish, Aurora Machine Learning, Aurora Limitless Database, Aurora DSQL) have no PostgreSQL equivalent and lock the workload to AWS.
Babelfish for Aurora PostgreSQL: An Aurora module released by AWS in 2021 that enables PostgreSQL-backed Aurora clusters to accept Microsoft SQL Server's T-SQL dialect and the Tabular Data Stream (TDS) wire protocol. Designed to attract Microsoft SQL Server workloads onto Aurora without rewriting client code; the resulting dependency on Aurora's specific T-SQL surface is not portable to any other PostgreSQL distribution.
Aurora Limitless Database: An Aurora feature announced in November 2023 introducing horizontal-sharding semantics on top of Aurora PostgreSQL, allowing a single logical database to scale read and write capacity across multiple compute nodes. Adds a sharding model that has no direct equivalent in upstream PostgreSQL.
Aurora Machine Learning: An Aurora feature that exposes Amazon SageMaker and Amazon Bedrock model invocations as SQL functions inside Aurora PostgreSQL and Aurora MySQL. Encourages ML inference calls inside the database transaction; the dependency on the specific SageMaker / Bedrock endpoint is not portable.
Amazon DynamoDB: AWS's proprietary key-value and document NoSQL database, launched in 2012 as the production successor to the internal Dynamo system described in the 2007 Werner Vogels et al. paper. Single-digit-millisecond latency at any scale; no on-premises equivalent. Closest open-source replacements (Apache Cassandra, ScyllaDB, MongoDB) require non-trivial schema translation and have different consistency and operational models.
AWS Lambda: A managed function-as-a-service runtime offered by AWS, launched in November 2014. Executes short-lived functions in response to events from EventBridge, S3, DynamoDB Streams, SQS, SNS, Kinesis and many others. The tight integration with the AWS event-delivery surface is the main lock-in vector at the runtime layer: portable function runtimes exist, but the event-delivery contract does not.
AWS EventBridge: A managed event bus by AWS (originally launched as CloudWatch Events in 2016 and rebranded EventBridge in 2019), receiving events from AWS services, SaaS partner sources and custom applications, and routing them to Lambda, SQS, SNS, Kinesis and other targets via JSON pattern rules. The default event-delivery layer of the AWS serverless stack.
Amazon SQS: Amazon Simple Queue Service, a managed message-queue service launched in 2004 (one of AWS's earliest services). Supports standard and FIFO queues with at-least-once and exactly-once delivery semantics respectively. The standard glue between Lambda functions and asynchronous workloads on AWS.
Amazon SNS: Amazon Simple Notification Service, a managed publish-subscribe and notification service launched in 2010. Fans messages out to SQS queues, Lambda functions, HTTP endpoints, mobile push services and email. Frequently paired with SQS in the so-called SNS-SQS fanout pattern.
Amazon CloudWatch: AWS's managed monitoring service, launched in 2009. Provides metrics, logs, dashboards, alarms and synthetic canaries for AWS resources and applications. The default observability tier of the AWS stack; OpenTelemetry, Prometheus and Grafana exist as portable alternatives but are not the path of least resistance inside AWS.
Amazon S3: Amazon Simple Storage Service, AWS's flagship object-storage product, launched in March 2006 alongside SQS and EC2. The S3 API has become a de facto portability surface in the wider industry; MinIO, Garage, Cloudflare R2 and many on-prem appliances implement it. AWS itself does not control the standard.
AWS EC2: Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud, AWS's virtual-machine product, launched in August 2006. The earliest of AWS's compute services and still the default building block. Migrations off AWS typically begin by replicating the EC2 footprint on another IaaS provider before the deeper architectural work begins.
AWS IAM: AWS Identity and Access Management. Two things in one service: a JSON policy language that grants or denies actions on Amazon Resource Names (ARNs) under specified conditions, and an account model of organisations, organisational units, service control policies and trust relationships. The policy language is AWS-specific; migrating it to another identity system is the largest part of an AWS exit.
ARN: Amazon Resource Name. The canonical identifier for any AWS resource, in the form arn:partition:service:region:account-id:resource. Referenced by IAM policies, CloudFormation templates, EventBridge rules and most AWS APIs. Not portable; ARNs do not resolve outside AWS.
AWS KMS: AWS Key Management Service, a managed cryptographic key store. Per AWS' own KMS documentation, symmetric keys never leave the service in plaintext; only ciphertext and operations are exposed through the API. Keys are region-bound and cannot be shared across regions, let alone across cloud providers. Re-encrypting a large estate against a different provider's keys is a multi-month exercise.
AWS IAM Identity Center: AWS' successor to AWS SSO (rebranded in 2022), providing centralised workforce identity for an AWS Organization. Permission sets are defined at the organisation level and translated at session time into IAM roles inside individual accounts. Single-region by design; migrating off Identity Center requires reconstructing the permission-set semantics on a different identity provider.
Free Data Transfer Out For Leaving: An AWS programme announced by Jeff Barr on the AWS Blog on 5 March 2024 that waives data-egress fees for customers wishing to migrate all of their data out of AWS. Gated on conditions: account in good standing, more than 100 GB of data, intention to move all data, 90-day completion window, account-level review, additional scrutiny on repeated applications. Comparable programmes were announced earlier by Google Cloud and Microsoft Azure.
EU Data Act: European Regulation (EU) 2023/2854 on harmonised rules on fair access to and use of data, entering into force on 11 January 2024 and becoming applicable on 12 September 2025. Article 25 sets the most far-reaching cloud-switching obligations any major jurisdiction has yet legislated: maximum two-month notice period, transitional period between 30 days and seven months, and a full prohibition of switching charges (including egress charges levied during a switch) from 12 January 2027.
Digital Markets Act: European Regulation (EU) 2022/1925, in force since 1 November 2022 and fully applicable since 2 May 2023. Designates large platforms as 'gatekeepers' and imposes obligations on interoperability, data portability, fair access and self-preferencing. Apple, Alphabet, Amazon, ByteDance, Meta and Microsoft were designated in September 2023, with Booking added in 2024. AWS' core cloud services are not in scope of DMA gatekeeper obligations; the cloud was addressed by the Data Act instead.
Synergy Research Group: An American market-research firm founded by John Dinsdale in 2007, headquartered in Reno, Nevada. Specialises in cloud infrastructure, networking and data-centre markets; its quarterly cloud-infrastructure-spend tables are the most-cited industry source for Big Three market share figures.
Flexera: An American software-asset-management and cloud-spend-management vendor, formed in 2008 from the divestiture of Macrovision's enterprise division. Publishes the annual State of the Cloud report, widely cited for figures on cloud adoption, multi-cloud usage and workload repatriation. The 2025 report recorded that 21 per cent of cloud workloads have been repatriated.
Zitadel: An open-source identity and access management platform written in Go, founded in 2020 by Christoph Bühler and Florian Forster of the Swiss software firm Caos. Event-sourced, cloud-native, multi-tenant by design. Speaks OIDC, OAuth 2.0, SAML 2.0, SCIM and the LDAP protocol; presented as a lighter, multi-tenancy-first alternative to Keycloak.
Open Policy Agent (OPA): A general-purpose policy engine, originally created at Styra in 2016 and a CNCF graduated project since 2021. Uses a declarative policy language called Rego to express authorisation, admission control and infrastructure-provisioning policies as code. Integrates with Kubernetes, Envoy, Terraform, application admission controllers and many other systems.
Rego: The declarative policy language used by Open Policy Agent (OPA). Inspired by Datalog; expresses policies as collections of rules over structured data (typically JSON or YAML inputs). The de facto standard for cloud-native policy as code since OPA's CNCF graduation in 2021.
Crossplane: A Kubernetes-native control plane for provisioning cloud resources across multiple providers through a consistent declarative interface. Originally developed by Upbound, released in 2018, became a CNCF incubating project in 2020 and a CNCF graduated project in 2024. Combined with OPA, the closest the open ecosystem has come to a portable replacement for AWS-specific Infrastructure-as-Code plus IAM.
Scaleway: A French cloud-infrastructure provider founded in 1999 (originally as Online.net) and rebranded Scaleway in 2015, part of the Iliad group. Operates data centres in Paris, Amsterdam and Warsaw. Publishes free data egress where AWS bills it; an independent Callista benchmark in February 2026 found Scaleway delivering approximately 4.8 times AWS' value-per-compute unit.
OVHcloud: A French cloud-infrastructure provider founded by Octave Klaba in 1999, headquartered in Roubaix. The largest European cloud provider by data-centre footprint, with operations in Europe, North America and Asia-Pacific. No US parent and no CLOUD Act exposure.
IONOS: A German hosting and cloud-infrastructure provider, part of United Internet AG since the 1990s and rebranded IONOS in 2018. Operates data centres across Europe and North America. EU-native, no CLOUD Act exposure.
Apache Cassandra: An open-source wide-column distributed database, originally developed at Facebook in 2008 by Avinash Lakshman and Prashant Malik, open-sourced in 2008 and an Apache Software Foundation top-level project since 2010. Designed for linear scalability and tunable consistency; the canonical open replacement for Amazon DynamoDB on the wide-column axis.
ScyllaDB: An open-source, C++ rewrite of Apache Cassandra, released by ScyllaDB Inc. (founded 2013 by Avi Kivity, Dor Laor and Benny Schnaider, ex-KVM creators) in 2014. Cassandra-protocol-compatible; markedly higher per-node throughput. The other canonical open replacement for DynamoDB.
MinIO: An open-source, S3-API-compatible object storage system, released in 2014 by MinIO Inc. (founded by Anand Babu Periasamy and Garima Kapoor). Written in Go, single-binary deployment, used widely as a private S3 substitute. The S3 API surface has become a de facto portability standard that AWS itself does not control.
Garage: An open-source, S3-API-compatible distributed object-storage system written in Rust, released by the Deuxfleurs collective in 2021. Designed for self-hosted, geo-distributed deployments on commodity hardware; lighter than MinIO for small clusters.
Callista (consultancy): A Swedish IT consultancy and research firm, founded in 2006. Published an independent cloud-pricing benchmark in February 2026 that compared AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, Hetzner, Scaleway and OVHcloud on a like-for-like compute-and-storage basket; the benchmark found Hetzner delivering approximately 14.3 times AWS' value-per-compute unit and Scaleway approximately 4.8 times.
Cloud repatriation: The deliberate move of one or more workloads from public-cloud infrastructure back to on-premises, colocated or private-cloud infrastructure, typically motivated by run-cost, latency or data-gravity considerations. Recorded by Flexera's 2025 State of the Cloud report at 21 per cent of cloud workloads, and documented in detail by 37signals across 2023 and 2024.
In the Net Episode 03: The third episode of Vivian Voss's 'In the Net' series, published 15 May 2026. Documents the documented mechanics of vendor lock-in on Amazon Web Services across three layers: the egress layer (Free Data Transfer Out For Leaving, 5 March 2024, in the window before EU Data Act Article 25 prohibits all switching charges from 12 January 2027), the runtime layer (Aurora, Babelfish, Aurora ML, Limitless Database, DynamoDB without an on-prem equivalent, Lambda wired to EventBridge, SQS, SNS, CloudWatch), and the identity layer (IAM policies, ARNs, KMS keys that never leave AWS in plaintext, Identity Center permission sets). Covers AWS's ~30 per cent share of the global cloud infrastructure market (Synergy Research, Q1 2026), the 37signals repatriation (~$3.2M/y on AWS to under $1M on-prem, hardware recouped in year one, ~$2M annual savings), the Flexera 21 per cent repatriated-workloads figure, and the portable escape route (Keycloak, Authentik, Zitadel for identity; Open Policy Agent and Crossplane for policy; Hetzner, Scaleway, OVHcloud, IONOS for EU IaaS; PostgreSQL, Cassandra, ScyllaDB, MongoDB, MinIO, Garage for data services).
Microsoft Recall: A Windows feature on Copilot+ PCs that captures snapshots of the screen at regular intervals, extracts text and semantic embeddings with a local AI model running on the on-device NPU, and exposes natural-language search across the user's past activity. Announced at Microsoft Build May 2024 with default-on activation and mostly-cleartext SQLite storage; withdrawn in June 2024 after security disclosure; redesigned through late 2024; re-released April 2025 with a VBS Enclave, AES-256-GCM at-rest encryption, TPM-bound keys, Windows Hello unlock, and an opt-in setup default.
Copilot+ PC: A Microsoft-defined class of Windows 11 devices certified for on-device AI workloads, requiring a neural processing unit rated at 40 trillion operations per second or higher, 16 GB minimum RAM and 256 GB minimum SSD. OEMs include Microsoft (Surface), Dell, HP, ASUS, Lenovo and Samsung. Hardware platform for Recall, Cocreator, Live Captions and other AI-host features that run locally rather than via cloud round-trip.
NPU: Neural Processing Unit. A specialised co-processor designed to accelerate neural-network inference workloads on-device, typically integrated alongside the CPU and GPU on modern SoCs. Microsoft's Copilot+ PC certification requires an NPU rated at 40 trillion operations per second or higher. Distinct from a GPU in that it is optimised for low-precision tensor operations and integer matrix maths rather than floating-point rasterisation.
TOPS: Tera-Operations Per Second. The standard unit for advertised NPU throughput. The Microsoft Copilot+ PC bar is 40 TOPS, set in May 2024; subsequent Qualcomm Snapdragon X, AMD Ryzen AI and Intel Core Ultra parts have shipped at higher figures. Vendor-published TOPS numbers describe theoretical peak throughput at INT8 precision and do not capture latency or memory-bandwidth characteristics.
Alexander Hagenah: Swiss security researcher based in Zurich. Published the original TotalRecall proof-of-concept tool in June 2024 demonstrating that the original Microsoft Recall feature stored its snapshot database in mostly-cleartext form on disk and could be extracted trivially. Published the TotalRecall Reloaded follow-up in March 2026, demonstrating that an ordinary user-level program with no admin rights and no kernel exploit can inject into AIXHost.exe after Windows Hello unlock and read decrypted Recall content from process memory.
TotalRecall: A proof-of-concept tool published by Alexander Hagenah in June 2024 that demonstrated the original Microsoft Recall feature stored snapshots and the OCR text index in a mostly-cleartext SQLite database accessible to any user-level process in the authenticated session. The disclosure triggered Microsoft's withdrawal of the feature and the subsequent VBS Enclave redesign that shipped in April 2025.
TotalRecall Reloaded: Alexander Hagenah's March 2026 follow-up to the original TotalRecall. An ordinary user-level program with no admin rights, no kernel exploit and no key extraction from the VBS Enclave; instead, injects into AIXHost.exe (the Recall timeline-rendering process) after the legitimate user has authenticated with Windows Hello and reads decrypted snapshots, OCR text and metadata directly from the process's address space. The Hagenah summary: 'The vault door is titanium. The wall next to it is drywall.'
AIXHost.exe: The Windows host process that renders the Recall timeline once a user has authenticated with Windows Hello. Runs in the user's session at ordinary user privilege, outside the VBS Enclave that holds the encryption keys. Decrypted snapshot content, OCR text and metadata live inside its address space for as long as the timeline is open; this is the documented trust boundary that TotalRecall Reloaded reads across.
Microsoft Build: Microsoft's annual developer conference, held since 2011 (descended from the Professional Developers Conference of the 1990s and 2000s). Typical venue: Seattle. The May 2024 edition included the launch of Copilot+ PCs and the first announcement of Microsoft Recall.
VBS Enclave: A Virtualisation-Based Security Enclave on Windows: a virtualised execution context whose memory is invisible to the rest of Windows, including the kernel running outside it. Used by Recall in the post-redesign architecture to hold encryption keys and to perform key-using operations. The data on disk is encrypted with keys that never leave the enclave; the decrypted data, however, must leave the enclave to be rendered to the user.
Windows Hello: Microsoft's biometric and PIN authentication framework for Windows, introduced with Windows 10 in 2015. Supports fingerprint, facial recognition (via infrared cameras) and PIN-based unlock. Recall uses Windows Hello Enhanced Sign-In Security as the gate for decrypting snapshots; the unlock is bound to a session that times out and must be reauthorised.
Trusted Platform Module (TPM): A dedicated hardware cryptoprocessor specified by the Trusted Computing Group (TPM 2.0 since 2014), present in most modern PCs and required for Windows 11. Holds key material that never leaves the chip in plaintext, supports remote attestation and provides hardware-rooted unlocking. Microsoft Recall seals its encryption keys against the device's TPM so that removing the disk from the machine yields nothing decryptable.
AES-256-GCM: Advanced Encryption Standard with 256-bit keys in Galois/Counter Mode, a widely-deployed authenticated-encryption scheme. Combines AES-CTR encryption with a Galois-field-based authentication tag in a single pass. Used by Microsoft Recall to encrypt snapshots and the vector index at rest with per-record keys; used as the default cipher in TLS 1.3, in disk encryption and across the modern web.
David Weston: Corporate Vice President for Microsoft Security, responsible for Microsoft's security engineering across Windows, Azure and the broader Microsoft estate. Author of Microsoft's April 2026 response to the TotalRecall Reloaded disclosure, in which he stated that the demonstrated access pattern was 'consistent with intended protections and existing controls' and 'does not represent a bypass of a security boundary or unauthorised access to data'.
Intune: Microsoft Intune (formerly Microsoft Endpoint Manager Intune), a cloud-based unified endpoint management service for Windows, macOS, iOS, iPadOS, Android and Linux. Configures device policies, deploys applications, enforces compliance and integrates with Entra ID conditional access. Hosts the WindowsAI configuration service provider used to disable Recall on managed devices.
Configuration Service Provider (CSP): A Windows mechanism that exposes device settings to Mobile Device Management (MDM) and Configuration Manager (Intune, third-party MDM) for centralised configuration. Each CSP defines a tree of settings that can be queried and set remotely; the WindowsAI CSP exposes DisableAIDataAnalysis and related Recall controls.
Microsoft Learn: Microsoft's primary documentation portal (learn.microsoft.com), launched in 2018 to consolidate technical content previously spread across MSDN, TechNet and docs.microsoft.com. Hosts the official Recall management documentation, the WindowsAI CSP reference and most other Windows-administration reference material.
Windows Insider: Microsoft's preview programme for Windows, launched in October 2014. Channels (Canary, Dev, Beta, Release Preview) offer increasingly stable builds. The redesigned Recall feature shipped to Insiders in November 2024 before reaching general availability on Copilot+ PCs in April 2025.
OCR: Optical Character Recognition. The conversion of images of text into machine-readable text strings. Modern OCR systems combine CNN-based or transformer-based detection with sequence models or beam-search decoding. Microsoft Recall applies on-device OCR to every captured screen snapshot so that the snapshot becomes searchable as text.
Semantic embedding: A dense vector representation of text or other content produced by a neural model, in which semantically similar inputs map to nearby vectors. Used to support natural-language and conceptual search: a query is embedded, nearest-neighbour search over the document embeddings returns relevant matches. Microsoft Recall uses on-device embedding of OCR text and screen content to support natural-language queries over the snapshot history.
AWS IAM (continued): See entry 'AWS IAM'. Cross-reference for the In the Net Episode 03 identity-layer discussion.
Not in the Brief Episode 03: The third episode of Vivian Voss's 'Not in the Brief' series, published 16 May 2026. Documents Microsoft Recall on Copilot+ PCs: the feature (continuous screen capture, on-device OCR and semantic embedding, natural-language search), the introduction (Build May 2024 with default-on and mostly-cleartext storage; withdrawn June 2024 after Hagenah's TotalRecall; redesigned through late 2024; re-released April 2025), the mechanics (VBS Enclave, AES-256-GCM, TPM-bound keys, Windows Hello as the four pillars; AIXHost.exe as the post-authentication rendering process where TotalRecall Reloaded reads decrypted content), Microsoft's response (Weston, April 2026: 'consistent with intended protections, does not represent a bypass'), the structural risk (third-party catalogue of every second on the user's machine, queryable by software the user did not write), the verification path (Settings, Group Policy, Intune CSP, forensic path) and the series pattern across the three episodes.
FreeBSD Jails: A kernel-native operating-system-level virtualisation mechanism for FreeBSD, designed by Poul-Henning Kamp in 1999 and shipped in FreeBSD 4.0 in March 2000. Confines processes by filesystem view (via chroot), process namespace, network stack, user IDs and accessible system calls in one coherent abstraction with no userland daemon. A jail starts in milliseconds because it is, fundamentally, a process; a FreeBSD host can comfortably run a thousand jails because the additional memory per jail is essentially zero.
Solaris Zones: Operating-system-level virtualisation for Solaris, introduced in 2004 as part of Solaris 10. Followed similar principles to FreeBSD Jails (1999) with a more elaborate administrative wrapping; the Global Zone hosts non-global zones that share the kernel but maintain separate filesystem views, process tables and network stacks.
Linux namespaces: A set of Linux kernel features for partitioning kernel resources so that processes in different namespaces see different sets of resources. Introduced incrementally between 2002 (mount namespaces in 2.4.19) and 2013 (user namespaces in 3.8), spanning eight separate types: mount, UTS, IPC, PID, network, user, cgroup and time. Composed by the caller, unlike FreeBSD Jails which present a single coherent abstraction.
cgroups: Control groups. A Linux kernel feature for limiting, accounting for and isolating the resource usage (CPU, memory, disk I/O, network) of a collection of processes. Originally contributed by Google engineers as 'process containers' in 2006, merged in 2007 and renamed cgroups, with cgroups v2 stabilised in 2016. The other half of the Linux containerisation stack alongside namespaces.
libcontainer: A Go library written by Docker (then dotCloud) in 2014 as the successor to LXC for Docker's container runtime. Provided direct access to Linux namespaces and cgroups without depending on the LXC userland tools. Later split out as the basis of runc, the OCI reference runtime, in 2015.
Podman: An open-source container engine maintained by Red Hat, first released in 2018. Container-compatible with Docker at the CLI level (podman run, podman build) but daemonless and rootless by default; each container is a direct child of the invoking shell rather than of a long-running daemon. Demonstrated that the Docker model does not structurally require a daemon.
Dockerfile: A text file containing instructions (FROM, RUN, COPY, CMD, ENTRYPOINT) for building a Docker container image. Introduced with Docker in 2013; the contract between application code and the container build system. Each instruction creates a new image layer, which is cached and shared between subsequent builds.
dockerd: The Docker daemon. The persistent server process that mediates between the Docker CLI and the Linux kernel isolation primitives (namespaces, cgroups). Required for the classic Docker model; documented on Docker's own forums to consume over five gigabytes of virtual memory while supervising 183 containers. FreeBSD Jails, by contrast, do not have a daemon: the kernel handles isolation directly.
Cluster mode (Node.js): A Node.js feature, stable since Node.js 0.10 (2013), that allows a single Node.js application to spawn child processes (workers) that share the same TCP port. The standard workaround for Node.js's single-threaded event loop on multi-core hardware: one worker per core, with round-robin load distribution by the master process. Later largely superseded by container orchestration.
Conway's law: An adage formulated by Melvin Conway in his 1968 paper 'How Do Committees Invent?': any organisation that designs a system will produce a design whose structure is a copy of the organisation's communication structure. Originally a description of how organisations leak into their architecture; became, in the containerised microservices era, a contract: the architecture is the org chart, rendered in YAML.
Melvin Conway: American computer scientist, born 1937. Author of the 1968 paper 'How Do Committees Invent?' that introduced what became known as Conway's law. Earlier contributor to the design of the COBOL language and to the development of coroutines as a structured programming construct.
Jeff Sutherland: American software engineer, born 1941. Co-creator of Scrum with Ken Schwaber. Co-author of the 1995 OOPSLA paper 'SCRUM Development Process' that codified the framework. Signatory of the 2001 Agile Manifesto.
Ken Schwaber: American software engineer, born 1945. Co-creator of Scrum with Jeff Sutherland. Co-author of the 1995 OOPSLA paper and several Scrum-defining books (including the Scrum Guide, maintained jointly with Sutherland). Signatory of the 2001 Agile Manifesto. Founder of Scrum.org in 2009.
Apache Web Server: An open-source HTTP server originally released by the Apache Group in 1995, descended from the National Center for Supercomputing Applications (NCSA) HTTPd. Used a thread-per-connection or process-per-connection concurrency model that proved difficult to scale to tens of thousands of simultaneous connections; one of the motivating examples for Node.js's event-loop design in 2009.
GIL: Global Interpreter Lock. A mutex in CPython (the reference implementation of Python) that ensures only one thread executes Python bytecode at a time, regardless of the number of available CPU cores. Restricts CPython programmes to one core for CPU-bound work; the canonical multi-core workaround is multi-process deployment (multiprocessing, gunicorn workers, or containers). PEP 703 (2023) introduced an opt-in path to remove the GIL in CPython 3.13 and later.
Amazon Prime Video case study: A March 2023 engineering blog post by Marcin Kolny published on the Amazon Prime Video Tech blog, documenting the migration of the Prime Video video-monitoring service from a distributed microservices architecture (AWS Lambda + Step Functions) to a monolithic application running on EC2 and ECS, with a reported cost reduction of approximately 90 per cent. Widely cited in the post-microservices retrospective literature.
Segment microservices retreat: A July 2018 engineering blog post by Alexandra Noonan at Segment titled 'Goodbye Microservices' documenting the consolidation of 140+ Segment microservices into a single monolith over the course of 2017. Reported outcomes included test runs dropping from hours to milliseconds, simplified deployments and reduced on-call load. One of the canonical microservices-retreat case studies of the late 2010s.
Segment (company): An American customer-data-platform company, founded by Peter Reinhardt, Calvin French-Owen, Ilya Volodarsky and Ian Storm Taylor in 2011 and acquired by Twilio in 2020. Provides a single API for collecting and routing customer event data to downstream analytics, marketing and warehouse systems. Author of the 2018 'Goodbye Microservices' retrospective.
Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF): A Linux Foundation sub-foundation hosting open-source cloud-native projects, founded in 2015 by Google with the donation of Kubernetes. Stewards Kubernetes, etcd, containerd, Prometheus, Envoy, Helm, Crossplane and many others. Lists more than 200 corporate members as of 2026.
Helm: A Kubernetes package manager, originally released by Deis in 2015 and donated to the CNCF in 2018. Bundles related Kubernetes manifests into a single deployable unit (a chart) with templated values for environment-specific configuration. The de facto packaging format for cloud-native applications.
Sidecar pattern: An architectural pattern in which a secondary container is co-deployed in the same pod as the main application container to provide cross-cutting concerns (logging, metrics, mTLS, configuration, secrets) without modifying the application. Popularised by service meshes such as Istio and Linkerd; the source of the per-pod overhead in those meshes.
OpenBSD pledge: An OpenBSD system call, introduced in OpenBSD 5.9 (March 2016, originally as tame in 5.8) by Theo de Raadt and Bob Beck. Allows a process to declare which categories of system calls it will use (stdio, rpath, wpath, inet and so on); the kernel kills the process if it attempts any other call. A capability-based least-privilege primitive at the process boundary.
OpenBSD unveil: An OpenBSD system call, introduced in OpenBSD 6.4 (October 2018) by Bob Beck. Allows a process to declare which filesystem paths it can see, narrowing the filesystem view to exactly the paths the application needs; subsequent attempts to access other paths return ENOENT. Combined with pledge, the two form OpenBSD's capability-based process-isolation model.
AWS Step Functions: An AWS service launched in December 2016 for orchestrating distributed application workflows as state machines defined in Amazon States Language (JSON). Co-deployed with AWS Lambda for serverless workflow execution; the architecture of the pre-migration Amazon Prime Video video-monitoring service before its 2023 consolidation back to a monolith on EC2.
Docker (company): An American software company, founded as dotCloud by Solomon Hykes in 2010 and renamed Docker in 2013 upon open-sourcing the Docker container engine. Raised over 270 million dollars in venture funding; substantially restructured in 2019 with the enterprise division sold to Mirantis. The brand most associated with the post-2013 containerisation default.
Solomon Hykes: French software engineer, born 1983. Founder of dotCloud (2010) and Docker (renamed 2013); released Docker as an open-source project in March 2013 in a lightning talk at PyCon US. Stepped down as CTO of Docker in 2018 and left the company entirely; later founded Dagger in 2018.
Theo de Raadt: South African-Canadian software engineer, born 1968. Founded OpenBSD in 1995 after departing the NetBSD project; remained the project lead through 2026. Also founded OpenSSH (1999), OpenBGPD, OpenNTPD, mandoc and the pledge and unveil security primitives. Known for direct mailing-list style and an uncompromising focus on code-correctness and security defaults.
On Second Thought Episode 08: The eighth episode of Vivian Voss's 'On Second Thought' series, published 17 May 2026. Examines the convergence of three currents (isolation, org-shape, runtime) into the default that every service ships in its own container. Covers the isolation history (Poul-Henning Kamp's FreeBSD Jails in 1999 as a single coherent abstraction with no daemon; Solaris Zones 2004; Linux namespaces and cgroups 2006 to 2008 as eight separate types; LXC 2008; Docker 2013 as the brand), the org-shape current (Scrum codified 1995 to 2001, Conway's 1968 paper as the explanatory mechanism), the runtime current (Node.js single-threaded by design from 2009 onward). Documents the four cost layers (image bloat, supply chain travelling with the image, daemon overhead, network tax) and the four documented retreats (Amazon Prime Video at ~90% cost reduction, Segment from 140 services to one, Istio merging its own control plane back into a single binary, 37signals leaving AWS with hardware recouped in year one). Closes with the alternatives that have been working all along: FreeBSD Capsicum, OpenBSD pledge and unveil, Go and Rust finding their own cores.
Mike Shapiro: American computer scientist. Co-creator of DTrace at Sun Microsystems with Bryan Cantrill and Adam Leventhal, 2001-2003. Co-author of the USENIX 2004 paper 'Dynamic Instrumentation of Production Systems'. Later worked on Solaris Fault Manager and FMA; subsequently co-founded Delphix and Oxide Computer Company.
Adam Leventhal: American computer scientist. Co-creator of DTrace at Sun Microsystems with Bryan Cantrill and Mike Shapiro, 2001-2003. Co-author of the USENIX 2004 paper 'Dynamic Instrumentation of Production Systems'. Later technical lead on Solaris and Fishworks; co-founded Delphix in 2008; subsequently joined Transposit and Oxide Computer Company.
John Birrell: American FreeBSD developer, principal of the DTrace port to FreeBSD that landed in 7.1-RELEASE on 6 January 2009. The port covered the kernel provider, the userland USDT (User-Statically-Defined-Tracing) probes and the full D script language. Died unexpectedly in 2009, shortly after the port shipped; remembered in the FreeBSD project as the engineer who brought DTrace into base.
Jim Mauro: American systems engineer. Co-author with Brendan Gregg of 'DTrace: Dynamic Tracing in Oracle Solaris, Mac OS X, and FreeBSD' (Prentice Hall, 2011), the canonical book on DTrace and the spiritual predecessor of Gregg's 2019 'BPF Performance Tools'. Also co-author of Solaris Internals (2000, 2nd ed. 2006). Long career at Sun Microsystems and later Oracle.
USDT: User-Statically-Defined Tracing. A mechanism, originally from DTrace, by which a userland program declares static probe points inside its own source code that an external tracer can attach to without restarting the program. Implemented on FreeBSD as part of DTrace (since 7.1-RELEASE, 2009); on Linux as part of the eBPF stack via the uprobes mechanism. Used by databases, runtimes and language interpreters (PostgreSQL, MySQL, the JVM HotSpot, Python, Ruby, Node.js) to expose stable internal events.
D (DTrace language): The script language of DTrace, designed by Bryan Cantrill and the DTrace team at Sun Microsystems. C-like syntax, deliberately Turing-incomplete: no unbounded loops, no recursion, no dynamic memory allocation in probe actions. The compiler proves before loading that every probe terminates within a bounded time and uses a bounded amount of memory; a misused script cannot crash production. Distinct from Walter Bright's general-purpose programming language also called D, with which it shares only the letter.
Daniel Borkmann: German Linux kernel engineer. Co-architect with Alexei Starovoitov of eBPF on Linux, merged in 3.18 on 7 December 2014. Formerly at Red Hat and Cisco; now at Isovalent (acquired by Cisco in 2024), where he co-leads kernel-side eBPF and Cilium development. Long-running upstream maintainer of the BPF subsystem.
Joyent: American cloud-infrastructure and OS-engineering company, founded 2004 in San Francisco, acquired by Samsung Electronics in 2016 and divested in 2019. Maintained the SmartOS (illumos-derived) and Triton container runtime; employed Bryan Cantrill and Brendan Gregg in the early 2010s; held the trademark on Node.js for several years before transferring it to the Node.js Foundation in 2015.
Brenden Blanco: American kernel engineer. Initial author and lead of the BPF Compiler Collection (BCC) at IO Visor in 2015. Provided the first ergonomic Python-and-C tooling on top of eBPF; long-time maintainer of bcc and a frequent collaborator with Brendan Gregg on the early eBPF performance toolchain.
IO Visor Project: A Linux Foundation collaborative project, founded in 2015 by PLUMgrid, Cisco, Huawei, Intel and others, that stewards the BPF Compiler Collection (bcc), bpftrace and a handful of related eBPF tools. The neutral home for the eBPF performance-tooling community.
PLUMgrid: An American networking startup founded in 2011 by ex-Cisco engineers, including Pere Monclus. The original employer of Alexei Starovoitov during the eBPF design work in 2013-2014. Acquired in part by VMware in 2016.
David S. Miller: American Linux kernel developer. Long-time maintainer of the Linux networking subsystem (1999 to 2022); accepted the original eBPF rework from Alexei Starovoitov into the networking tree in March 2014, leading to its inclusion in Linux 3.18 in December 2014. Earlier maintainer of the SPARC port. Stepped back from active maintenance in 2022; remains an honorary committer.
Alastair Robertson (bpftrace): British software engineer. Created the initial bpftrace implementation in 2017-2018, when working as a researcher at the University of Manchester. Continued as a core maintainer at the IO Visor Project and Meta. bpftrace was publicly announced by Brendan Gregg in October 2018 as 'DTrace 2.0 for Linux'.
truss (FreeBSD): A FreeBSD command-line utility that traces system calls and signals for a running process or program. Comparable in role to Linux's strace but BSD-native. Interposes on the traced process via ptrace; useful for debugging but heavier than DTrace, which does not stop the traced process.
strace: A Linux command-line utility that traces system calls and signals for a running process. Uses the ptrace mechanism to stop the traced process at each syscall, log the arguments and return value, and continue it. Heavy enough that the act of measurement disturbs what is being measured, which is the canonical motivating example for DTrace's passive-probe design.
ptrace: The Unix system call (originally from Research Unix V8, late 1980s, present on every modern Unix) that allows one process to observe and control the execution of another, including reading and writing its memory and registers. The underlying mechanism of strace, gdb, truss and similar tools. Disturbs the traced process because each event requires the tracer to stop and resume the tracee.
perf (Linux): A Linux performance-analysis tool, integrated with the kernel's perf_events subsystem since 2.6.31 (2009). Originally written by Ingo Molnar, Peter Zijlstra and Arnaldo Carvalho de Melo. Samples CPU profiling, hardware performance counters, software events, kprobes and uprobes. Coexists with eBPF and bpftrace for narrower sampling use cases; the de facto Linux CPU profiler since the early 2010s.
ftrace: A Linux kernel tracing framework, merged in 2.6.27 (2008), originally written by Steven Rostedt. Provides function tracing, event tracing, latency measurement and stack tracing through a /sys/kernel/debug/tracing interface. Coexists with eBPF for narrower kernel-tracing use cases; particularly useful for measuring interrupt and scheduling latency.
tracepoints: Static kernel-level instrumentation points compiled into the Linux kernel at well-defined event sites (scheduler, syscalls, network, block layer). Defined in source via the TRACE_EVENT macro; consumable by ftrace, perf and eBPF/bpftrace. The stable Linux equivalent of DTrace's static probe surface; supplemented at runtime by kprobes and uprobes for dynamic probe attachment.
kprobes: A Linux kernel mechanism, present since 2.6.9 (2004), that allows a tracer to dynamically attach a probe handler at almost any kernel instruction. Provides the dynamic-probe surface used by perf, ftrace and bpftrace where a static tracepoint does not exist. The functional Linux equivalent of DTrace's fbt provider.
uprobes: A Linux kernel mechanism, present since 3.5 (2012), that allows a tracer to dynamically attach a probe handler at any userland instruction in any running process. The userland counterpart to kprobes; used by perf and bpftrace to instrument applications without source-code modification. The functional Linux equivalent of DTrace's pid provider.
Sun Microsystems: American computer company, founded by Vinod Khosla, Andy Bechtolsheim, Bill Joy and Scott McNealy at Stanford in 1982. Created Solaris, ZFS, DTrace, Zones, NFS, Java, the SPARC architecture and many other foundational technologies. Acquired by Oracle in 2010 for ~$7.4 billion. Many ex-Sun engineers went on to Joyent, Delphix, Oxide Computer Company and the wider illumos ecosystem.
Isovalent: An American cloud-networking company founded in 2017 by Dan Wendlandt, Thomas Graf and Martin Mao around the eBPF-based Cilium project. Employer of Daniel Borkmann among the upstream BPF maintainers. Acquired by Cisco in April 2024.
The Unix Way Episode 17: The seventeenth episode of Vivian Voss's 'The Unix Way' series, published 18 May 2026. Compares DTrace and eBPF as the two production-tracing answers in active use in 2026. Documents DTrace's design at Sun by Bryan Cantrill, Mike Shapiro and Adam Leventhal (2001-2003), the Solaris 10 GA in January 2005, the John Birrell port to FreeBSD in 7.1-RELEASE on 6 January 2009, the Turing-incomplete D script language and the production safety guarantees. Names the licence asymmetry (CDDL coexistence vs GPL absorption) as the structural reason Linux could not adopt DTrace upstream. Documents the Linux rebuild: BPF (McCanne, Jacobson, Berkeley, 1992), eBPF (Starovoitov, Borkmann, Linux 3.18 on 7 December 2014), BCC (Brenden Blanco at IO Visor, 2015), bpftrace (Alastair Robertson, announced by Brendan Gregg as 'DTrace 2.0 for Linux' in October 2018). Names Brendan Gregg as the biographical bridge between the two ecosystems: DTrace book with Jim Mauro in 2011 at Sun/Joyent, BPF Performance Tools in 2019 at Netflix.
grep: A Unix command-line utility that searches plain-text data for lines matching a regular expression and prints the matches on stdout. Written by Ken Thompson at Bell Labs in November 1973 after Doug McIlroy commissioned a standalone version of ed's g/re/p command (global, regular-expression, print). Ships in the base system of every Unix-derived operating system in production fifty-three years later.
Lee E. McMahon: American computer scientist at Bell Labs in the 1970s. Co-author of the Bell Labs sed command (with Lorinda Cherry, 1974) and of awk's earliest predecessor. Best-remembered today as the user whose request to analyse the text of the Federalist Papers by pattern triggered Doug McIlroy's commission to Ken Thompson that produced grep in November 1973.
Doug McIlroy (continued): See entry 'Doug McIlroy'. Cross-reference for the November 1973 commission that produced grep: McIlroy asked Thompson to extract the ed g/re/p command into a standalone tool so that Lee McMahon could analyse the Federalist Papers by pattern.
Federalist Papers: A series of 85 essays published in 1787 and 1788 in New York newspapers, written by Alexander Hamilton, James Madison and John Jay (under the shared pseudonym Publius) to promote ratification of the United States Constitution. The text became the working corpus for Bell Labs computer scientists in the early 1970s; Lee McMahon's request to analyse the Papers by pattern was the immediate trigger for the creation of grep in November 1973.
ed (editor): The original Unix line editor, written by Ken Thompson at Bell Labs in 1969. Line-oriented (no full-screen mode), accepts regex addresses, and contributed the g/re/p (global, regular-expression, print) command from which grep took its name. Still shipped on every Unix-derived system; treated by most users today as a curiosity, by a few as a working tool.
QED editor: An early line-oriented editor written by Butler Lampson, Peter Deutsch and L. Peter Deutsch at Berkeley around 1965-1967, and later rewritten by Ken Thompson at Bell Labs for CTSS. The first program to integrate regular expressions as a syntax for text search; Thompson's 1968 CACM paper 'Regular Expression Search Algorithm' described the construction used in QED. Direct ancestor of ed and, through ed, of grep.
Mike Haertel: American software engineer. Long-time principal author of GNU grep through the late 1980s, 1990s and into the 2000s (afaik). Author of the influential August 2010 freebsd-current note 'why GNU grep is fast', which stated the performance-engineering principle: 'The key to making programs fast is to make them do practically nothing.' Combined Boyer-Moore for fixed strings, two-way string matching for harder patterns, Thompson's NFA for full regex, and mmap on the input.
Boyer-Moore algorithm: A string-matching algorithm published by Robert S. Boyer and J Strother Moore in October 1977 in CACM 20(10). Pre-computes a skip table from the pattern and scans the input right-to-left within each pattern window, allowing the matcher to advance by the pattern length on a mismatch. Sub-linear on average for typical English-text inputs; the inner loop in GNU grep for fixed-string searches.
Robert S. Boyer: American computer scientist, professor emeritus at the University of Texas at Austin. Co-author with J Strother Moore of the 1977 Boyer-Moore string-matching algorithm and of the Boyer-Moore theorem prover (Nqthm, ACL2). Recipient of the Herbrand Award (1999) for contributions to automated reasoning.
J Strother Moore: American computer scientist, professor emeritus at the University of Texas at Austin. Co-author with Robert Boyer of the 1977 Boyer-Moore string-matching algorithm and of the Boyer-Moore theorem prover. The 'J' is his entire first name, not an initial.
Two-way string matching (Crochemore-Perr: A string-matching algorithm published by Maxime Crochemore and Dominique Perrin in the Journal of the ACM, 38(3), July 1991. Splits the pattern into two parts and matches each in opposite directions, achieving linear time in the input and constant additional space. Used by GNU grep when fixed-string matching does not apply but the pattern has a fixed prefix or suffix.
Maxime Crochemore: French computer scientist, professor at King's College London and at the University of Paris-Est Marne-la-Vallée. Specialist in string algorithms and combinatorics on words; co-author with Dominique Perrin of the 1991 two-way string matching algorithm used inside GNU grep, and of several standard texts on algorithms for string processing.
Stephen Cole Kleene: American mathematician (1909-1994), professor at the University of Wisconsin-Madison. Formalised regular sets and finite automata in his 1956 paper 'Representation of Events in Nerve Nets and Finite Automata' for the RAND Corporation. Gave the field of formal languages its core theoretical object; the Kleene star (`*`) in regular-expression syntax is named after him.
Thompson's NFA construction: An algorithm published by Ken Thompson in June 1968 in CACM 11(6), 'Regular Expression Search Algorithm', that builds a non-deterministic finite automaton (NFA) directly from a regular-expression syntax and simulates it efficiently against an input string. The first practical implementation of Kleene's mathematical regular sets; first used in the QED editor, inherited by ed, extracted by grep, and still the algorithm underneath the full-regex code path in GNU grep and RE2.
POSIX BRE: Posix Basic Regular Expressions. The default regular-expression dialect required by the POSIX specification of grep, sed and other Unix tools. Uses backslash-escaped metacharacters for grouping and alternation (\(, \), \| are special; (, ), | are literal). Distinct from POSIX ERE, which inverts the convention.
POSIX ERE: POSIX Extended Regular Expressions. The dialect accepted by grep -E (formerly egrep) and several other Unix tools. Uses unescaped metacharacters for grouping and alternation (`(`, `)`, `|` are special) and supports the `+` and `?` quantifiers natively. The more familiar dialect for engineers coming from JavaScript, Python or Perl.
mmap: A POSIX system call (mmap(2)) that maps a file or device into a process's memory address space, exposing it as a region of memory that the process can read or write directly. The kernel handles paging on demand; no userland copy and no stdio buffering are required. Used by GNU grep for its input, and by many other I/O-heavy tools that benefit from skipping the standard-library buffering tier.
bsdgrep: A BSD-licensed reimplementation of grep, present in the FreeBSD base system since around 2010 (afaik). Replaced the GNU-licensed version in FreeBSD's base tree as part of the project's long-running effort to keep its base userland under BSD licences. Leaner than GNU grep and aimed at POSIX correctness rather than last-mile performance optimisation; GNU grep is one pkg install gnugrep away when needed. Also shipped, in spirit, by OpenBSD and NetBSD.
GNU grep: The GNU project's implementation of grep, originally written by Mike Haertel from the late 1980s and maintained by the GNU project since. The most widely deployed grep implementation on Linux distributions and the reference for the 'why GNU grep is fast' performance literature. Released under the GNU General Public License.
ack (search tool): A grep-like source-code search tool written in Perl by Andy Lester, first released in 2005. Skips version-control directories, build artefacts and binary files by default; recurse-searches by default. The first widely-adopted developer-ergonomic descendant of grep; later partially superseded by ag and ripgrep on performance grounds.
ag (silver searcher): A grep-like source-code search tool written in C by Geoff Greer, first released in 2011 (afaik). A reimplementation of ack focused on raw performance, achieved by C, mmap, parallel directory walking and clever string-matching. The middle entry in the lineage from grep through ack to ripgrep.
ripgrep: A grep-like source-code search tool written in Rust by Andrew Gallant (BurntSushi on GitHub), first released in 2016. Honours .gitignore by default, uses SIMD instructions for fast searching, and is competitive with or faster than GNU grep on most workloads. The most widely-adopted of the modern grep descendants; the de facto default in many developer tools (VS Code, Helix, fzf) since the late 2010s.
Andy Lester: American Perl programmer and author. Created ack, the developer-ergonomic source-code search tool, in 2005. Author of 'Land the Tech Job You Love' and of long-running Perl tooling. Maintains the ack project at beyondgrep.com.
Geoff Greer: American software engineer. Created ag, the silver searcher, in 2011 as a C rewrite of Andy Lester's ack. Long-time maintainer of the ag project on GitHub. Currently runs Floobits, a real-time collaborative editing service for programmers.
Andrew Gallant: American software engineer at Astral, formerly at Cloudflare. Maintains the Rust regex crate, ripgrep (the search tool, released 2016), the xsv CSV tool and a long list of other Rust command-line tools under the BurntSushi GitHub handle. One of the most influential individual contributors to the modern Rust command-line ecosystem.
BurntSushi: Andrew Gallant's GitHub handle and informal pen name. Used as the maintainer identity on ripgrep, the Rust regex crate, xsv, fd-find and a long list of high-performance Rust command-line utilities. See entry 'Andrew Gallant'.
Technical Beauty Episode 36: The thirty-sixth episode of Vivian Voss's 'Technical Beauty' series, published 19 May 2026. Documents the origin of grep at Bell Labs in November 1973 (Doug McIlroy commissions Ken Thompson to extract ed's g/re/p as a standalone tool so that Lee McMahon can analyse the Federalist Papers by pattern; the story told by Brian Kernighan has Thompson disappearing into his office for about an hour). Documents the implementation interior: Boyer-Moore (1977) for fixed strings, Crochemore-Perrin two-way string matching (1991) for harder patterns, Thompson's 1968 NFA construction for full regex, mmap on the input, Mike Haertel's 'do practically nothing' principle. Notes bsdgrep in the FreeBSD base system since around 2010, and the unchanged interface across the modern descendants: ack (Andy Lester, 2005, Perl), ag (Geoff Greer, 2011, C), ripgrep (Andrew Gallant, 2016, Rust). Closes with the note on Kleene (1956) and Thompson (1968) as the path by which regex became the default search interface for the working programmer.
Mars Climate Orbiter: A NASA robotic space probe launched on 11 December 1998 to study the Martian atmosphere and serve as a communications relay for the Mars Polar Lander. Lost on 23 September 1999 during its Mars Orbit Insertion burn when a unit mismatch at a software interface brought it to approximately 57 km altitude instead of the planned 226 km, deep within the atmosphere. The 193-million-dollar spacecraft (327.6 million dollar total mission) is the canonical engineering example of a unit-mismatch failure.
Mars Climate Orbiter Mishap Investigatio: The NASA board convened after the loss of the Mars Climate Orbiter, which released its Phase I report on 10 November 1999. The report identified the root cause as a unit mismatch at a software interface: Lockheed Martin's ground software produced thruster impulse in pound-force seconds (imperial) where NASA JPL's navigation software expected newton-seconds (SI), a factor of 4.45.
NASA Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL): A federally funded research and development centre in Pasadena, California, managed by Caltech for NASA. Responsible for many of NASA's robotic planetary missions, including the Mars rovers, Voyager and the Mars Climate Orbiter. JPL's navigation software for the orbiter expected thruster impulse in newton-seconds; the mismatch with Lockheed Martin's imperial output caused the 1999 loss.
Lockheed Martin: An American aerospace and defence company, formed in 1995 by the merger of Lockheed and Martin Marietta. Built the Mars Climate Orbiter spacecraft and the ground software that calculated thruster impulse. The ground software output the impulse in pound-force seconds, an imperial unit, where the interface specification required metric, leading to the 1999 loss.
SI (Système International): The International System of Units, the modern coherent metric system maintained by the International Bureau of Weights and Measures (BIPM) and used as the standard measurement system across science and engineering worldwide. Defines seven base units (metre, kilogram, second, ampere, kelvin, mole, candela) and derived units including the newton. The Mars Climate Orbiter was lost because half its programme computed in imperial units rather than SI.
newton: The SI derived unit of force, named after Isaac Newton. One newton is the force that accelerates a mass of one kilogram at one metre per second squared. A newton-second is that force applied for one second, the SI unit of impulse that NASA JPL's navigation software expected from the Mars Climate Orbiter's thruster firings.
pound-force: An imperial unit of force, the force exerted by Earth's standard gravity on a mass of one pound (approximately 4.448 newtons). A pound-force second is that force applied for one second, the imperial unit of impulse that Lockheed Martin's ground software produced for the Mars Climate Orbiter, where the interface required SI newton-seconds. The conversion factor of 4.45 is what destroyed the spacecraft.
Rust newtype: A Rust idiom in which a single-field tuple struct wraps an existing type to create a distinct new type that the compiler treats as incompatible with the original, for example struct NewtonSeconds(f64) and struct PoundForceSeconds(f64). The compiler refuses to pass one where the other is expected, turning a unit mismatch into a compile error. The cheapest place a unit mistake can possibly be caught.
F# units of measure: A feature of the F# programming language that adds physical units to the type system, so that a value typed float<N*s> (newton-seconds) cannot be assigned to or combined with a value typed float<lbf*s> (pound-force seconds) without an explicit conversion. Unit mismatches become compile-time errors. One of the few mainstream languages with first-class dimensional analysis in the type checker.
gibibyte (GiB): A binary unit of digital information defined by the IEC in 1998 as 2 to the power of 30, that is 1,073,741,824 bytes. The binary counterpart to the decimal gigabyte (10 to the power of 9, 1,000,000,000 bytes); the two differ by about 7.4 per cent. Part of the IEC binary-prefix ladder (kibibyte, mebibyte, gibibyte, tebibyte, pebibyte) that disambiguates powers of two from powers of ten.
IEC binary prefixes: A set of binary unit prefixes standardised by the International Electrotechnical Commission in 1998 (IEC 60027-2) to disambiguate powers of two from the decimal SI prefixes: kibi (Ki, 2^10), mebi (Mi, 2^20), gibi (Gi, 2^30), tebi (Ti, 2^40), pebi (Pi, 2^50) and beyond. A '1 TB' decimal drive displays as roughly 931 GiB; the gap between decimal and binary widens with each rung. Recommended wherever powers of two are meant.
Tales from the Bare Metal Episode 04: The fourth episode of Vivian Voss's 'Tales from the Bare Metal' series, published 20 May 2026. Documents the loss of the Mars Climate Orbiter on 23 September 1999: a 193-million-dollar spacecraft brought to ~57 km altitude instead of the planned 226 km because Lockheed Martin's ground software output thruster impulse in pound-force seconds (imperial) where NASA JPL's navigation software expected newton-seconds (SI), a factor of 4.45. Identifies three systemic conditions (the spec was a document not a runtime check; the warning was visible months early but below the escalation threshold; no end-to-end test crossed the boundary) and two guards (measure in metric in science and engineering; make the unit travel with the number via Rust newtypes, F# units of measure, or named variables). Extends the lesson to the decimal-versus-binary gigabyte-versus-gibibyte mismatch standardised by the IEC in 1998.
