Visual Studio Code: A free, open-source-cored code editor released by Microsoft in 2015, built on the Electron framework. The most widely used code editor in the world by the mid-2020s. Extends its functionality through extensions installed from the Visual Studio Code Marketplace; the editor and its extension model are central to the May 2026 GitHub breach.
VS Code Marketplace: Microsoft's distribution platform for Visual Studio Code extensions. One-click install, automatic updates by default, and no enforced provenance: the Marketplace does not require a publisher to prove control of the upstream project an extension claims to extend. Functionally a package registry with far less scrutiny than npm; the distribution channel for the poisoned Nx Console build in the May 2026 GitHub breach.
Nx Console: The official Visual Studio Code extension for the Nx build system, published under the Marketplace identifier nrwl.angular-console. A trojanised build, version 18.95.0, was published to the VS Code Marketplace on 18 May 2026 and was live for approximately eleven minutes; it reached a GitHub employee's device and led to the exfiltration of around 3,800 internal repositories.
Nx (build system): An open-source build system and monorepo tool for JavaScript and TypeScript projects, originally created by Nrwl (now Nx) and first released in 2017. Provides task orchestration, caching and code generation across large repositories. Its Visual Studio Code companion extension, Nx Console, was the vehicle for the May 2026 GitHub supply-chain breach.
TeamPCP: A threat actor group that claimed responsibility for the May 2026 GitHub breach via a poisoned Visual Studio Code extension. Tracked by Google Threat Intelligence as UNC6780. Specialises in supply-chain attacks against open-source security utilities and developer tooling, active across npm, PyPI and PHP package ecosystems earlier in 2026; reportedly asked more than 50,000 US dollars for the ~3,800 stolen GitHub repositories.
UNC6780: Google Threat Intelligence's tracking designation for the threat actor that publicly operates as TeamPCP. The UNC (UNCategorised) prefix marks an activity cluster not yet promoted to a named APT. Attribution for the May 2026 GitHub breach via a trojanised Nx Console VS Code extension.
Google Threat Intelligence: Google's consolidated threat-intelligence organisation, formed in 2024 by merging Mandiant, the VirusTotal team and Google's own threat-analysis groups. Maintains the UNC actor taxonomy inherited from Mandiant; attributed the May 2026 GitHub breach to UNC6780 (publicly TeamPCP).
BleepingComputer: An American technology and cybersecurity news publication, founded by Lawrence Abrams in 2004. Widely cited as a primary outlet for ransomware, data-breach and supply-chain reporting; carried the confirmation that GitHub had lost around 3,800 internal repositories via a malicious VS Code extension in May 2026.
The Record (Recorded Future): A cybersecurity news publication operated by Recorded Future, the American threat-intelligence company founded in 2009. Publishes incident reporting and threat-actor analysis at therecord.media; carried the GitHub confirmation that the TeamPCP breach did not affect customer data in May 2026.
Help Net Security: A long-running cybersecurity news publication, founded in 1998 and based in Croatia. Covers vulnerability disclosure, breach reporting and industry analysis; one of the Tier-1 outlets that carried the May 2026 GitHub-via-TeamPCP breach.
ambient authority: A security term for the situation in which a process automatically holds all the permissions of the user who runs it, rather than being granted only the specific authority it needs for its task. The default model on conventional operating systems and the reason a VS Code extension can reach every file and credential the developer can. The capability model (Capsicum, capability-based security) is the structural alternative: a process holds only the authority it was explicitly handed.
Wire Fire Episode 02: The second episode of Vivian Voss's 'Wire Fire' series, published 21 May 2026. Documents the May 2026 GitHub breach: on 18 May a trojanised build of Nx Console (nrwl.angular-console v18.95.0) was published to the VS Code Marketplace, live ~11 minutes, long enough to reach a GitHub employee's device and exfiltrate around 3,800 internal repositories; GitHub detected the intrusion on 19 May and confirmed it on 20 May. The attacker, TeamPCP (Google Threat Intelligence: UNC6780), asked 50,000+ USD for the haul. Explains the mechanism (a VS Code extension runs with the developer's full privileges, no sandbox, activation code runs automatically on workspace open and can read any file, run any command, reach any credential), the exposure check, and the pattern: the editor is now part of the supply chain; an extension marketplace is a package registry with the same arbitrary-code risk as npm and far less scrutiny. Names Capsicum capability-mode sandboxing as the structural fix that editors have not adopted on any platform.
VMware: An enterprise virtualisation company founded in 1998, the originator of mainstream x86 server virtualisation. Its ESXi hypervisor and vSphere management suite ran a large share of the world's enterprise data centres for two decades, sold as a perpetual licence. Acquired by Broadcom in a 69 billion dollar deal that closed on 22 November 2023, after which its licensing model was converted from perpetual to subscription.
Broadcom: An American semiconductor and enterprise-software company, led by Hock Tan, known for acquiring established technology firms and restructuring their commercial models toward high-margin subscriptions. Acquired CA Technologies (2018), Symantec's enterprise division (2019) and VMware (completed 22 November 2023 for around 69 billion US dollars). The VMware acquisition is the subject of In the Net Episode 04 as a case of licence weaponisation through acquisition.
ESXi: VMware's bare-metal (type-1) hypervisor, the component that runs directly on server hardware and hosts virtual machines. Long offered in a free edition for labs and small deployments; the free edition was withdrawn in early 2024 after the Broadcom acquisition and quietly reinstated in April 2025 as ESXi 8.0 Update 3e, a non-production build with no support that cannot connect to vCenter.
vSphere: VMware's server-virtualisation platform, combining the ESXi hypervisor with the vCenter management server and features such as vMotion and Distributed Resource Scheduler. The product an enterprise data centre actually deploys; sold as a perpetual licence for two decades, converted to subscription-only after the Broadcom acquisition and repackaged into the VVF and VCF bundles.
vMotion: A VMware vSphere feature that migrates a running virtual machine from one physical host to another with no downtime and without dropping a packet. One of the engineering marvels that made VMware the default enterprise hypervisor; cited in In the Net Episode 04 as evidence that the product itself was never the problem.
vCenter: VMware's centralised management server for a vSphere environment, providing a single control plane for clusters, hosts, virtual machines, vMotion and resource scheduling. The reinstated free ESXi build (April 2025) is deliberately unable to connect to vCenter, limiting it to standalone non-production use.
NSX: VMware's software-defined networking and security product, providing network virtualisation, micro-segmentation and distributed firewalling. Bundled into the post-acquisition VMware vSphere Foundation (VVF) and VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) packages, so customers are billed for it whether or not they run it.
vSAN: VMware's software-defined storage product, aggregating local disks across a cluster into a shared datastore. Like NSX, bundled into the post-acquisition VVF and VCF packages and billed whether or not the customer uses it.
hypervisor: The software layer that lets one physical computer run many virtual machines, each believing it has its own hardware. Type-1 (bare-metal) hypervisors such as VMware ESXi, Xen and bhyve run directly on the hardware; type-2 hypervisors run on top of a host operating system. The commodity layer at the centre of the VMware lock-in story.
perpetual licence: A software licensing model in which the customer pays once and owns the right to run a given version of the software indefinitely, with support and upgrades as a separate optional subscription. The model under which VMware was sold for two decades; ended by Broadcom after the 2023 acquisition in favour of subscription-only licensing, which is the central grievance of In the Net Episode 04.
Proxmox VE: An open-source virtualisation platform (Proxmox Virtual Environment) built on KVM and QEMU for full virtual machines and LXC for containers, with clustering, live migration, software-defined storage (including Ceph) and a web interface. Free to use with no feature gates; optional support subscription from around 115 euro per CPU socket per year. The mature mass-migration target for organisations leaving VMware after the Broadcom acquisition.
KVM: Kernel-based Virtual Machine. The Linux kernel's built-in virtualisation infrastructure, merged in 2.6.20 (2007), which turns the kernel into a type-1 hypervisor. Paired with QEMU for device emulation. The hypervisor underneath Proxmox VE and most Linux-based virtualisation platforms.
QEMU: Quick Emulator. An open-source machine emulator and virtualiser, created by Fabrice Bellard in 2003. Provides device emulation and, with KVM, near-native virtual-machine performance. The userland half of the KVM/QEMU pairing used by Proxmox VE and many other platforms.
XCP-ng: An open-source virtualisation platform based on the Xen hypervisor, forked from Citrix XenServer in 2018 and maintained by Vates. Paired with the Xen Orchestra management interface. One of the mature alternatives for organisations migrating off VMware.
Xen: An open-source type-1 hypervisor, originally developed at the University of Cambridge and first released in 2003. The basis of XCP-ng, Citrix XenServer and the early Amazon EC2 platform. Uses a privileged control domain (dom0) to manage unprivileged guest domains.
Nutanix AHV: The native hypervisor of the Nutanix hyperconverged-infrastructure platform, based on KVM. Sold as an integrated appliance-style platform combining compute, storage and virtualisation. One of the commercial alternatives cited for organisations leaving VMware.
bhyve: The FreeBSD hypervisor (the BSD Hypervisor), introduced in FreeBSD 10.0 in 2014, written largely by Neel Natu and Peter Grehan. A lean type-2 hypervisor in the FreeBSD base system, requiring hardware virtualisation extensions. Part of the FreeBSD virtualisation stack (bhyve for VMs, jails for containers, ZFS for storage) presented in In the Net Episode 04 as the acquisition-proof escape route from VMware.
Sylve: An open-source, Proxmox-style web management interface for the FreeBSD virtualisation stack (bhyve VMs, jails, ZFS, networking), developed by AlchemillaHQ and written in Go and SvelteKit, with clustering and scheduled backups via zelta. First release (v0.1.0) in early 2026, at v0.2.x at the time of writing; requires FreeBSD 15.0 or later. Honestly young and not yet a drop-in for enterprise estates, but already productive for homelabs and FreeBSD-first shops.
zelta: A ZFS replication and backup tool used by Sylve for scheduled backups of the FreeBSD virtualisation stack. Wraps ZFS send and receive into a higher-level snapshot-and-replicate workflow.
OVF: Open Virtualization Format. An open, vendor-neutral standard (DMTF) for packaging and distributing virtual machines, describing the VM's metadata (CPU, memory, disks, network) in an XML descriptor alongside its disk images. The portable export format that makes leaving VMware a bounded migration rather than a rebuild; paired with qcow2 disk conversion for KVM-based targets.
qcow2: QEMU Copy-On-Write version 2. The native disk-image format of QEMU and KVM, supporting copy-on-write snapshots, thin provisioning and compression. The conversion target for VMware VMDK disks when migrating to Proxmox VE or other KVM-based platforms; together with OVF, the open path off VMware.
AT&T: An American multinational telecommunications company. Filed suit against Broadcom in the New York State Supreme Court in August 2024, alleging that Broadcom declined to honour a pre-acquisition VMware support-renewal option unless AT&T purchased new subscription bundles under a three-year commitment. Cited in In the Net Episode 04 as the dignity-dimension evidence: if a telecom giant needs a courtroom to obtain support it believes it contracted for, the small estate has rather less leverage.
Ingram Micro: One of the largest technology distributors in the world. Announced in December 2024 that it would end its Broadcom and VMware distribution relationship in select regions from early January 2025. Cited in In the Net Episode 04 as a standing signal: distributors do not walk away from a 70-per-cent-market-share product unless the terms have become unfriendly.
Gartner: An American technology research and advisory firm, founded in 1979. Its market-share figures and projections are widely cited in enterprise IT. Reported VMware at roughly 70 per cent of the server-virtualisation market in 2024 and projected that share falling to around 40 per cent by 2029 as a consequence of the post-acquisition strategy.
In the Net Episode 04: The fourth episode of Vivian Voss's 'In the Net' series, published 22 May 2026. A distinct lock-in genus from the first three: licence weaponisation through acquisition. Documents Broadcom's 69 billion dollar acquisition of VMware (completed 22 November 2023) and the conversion of perpetual licences into subscriptions; the withdrawal and quiet April 2025 reinstatement of free ESXi as a non-production build; the move from per-CPU to per-core licensing in VVF and VCF bundles that include NSX and vSAN; VMware's ~70 per cent 2024 market share and Gartner's projected fall to 40 per cent by 2029; the Ingram Micro distribution exit; the March 2025 72-core minimum that appeared and vanished ('never announced a price change'); and the AT&T lawsuit of August 2024. The escape route is a stack, not a product: bhyve, jails and ZFS in the FreeBSD base, Sylve as the optional Proxmox-style UI, Proxmox VE as the mature mainstream target, XCP-ng and Nutanix AHV as alternatives, migration over the open OVF and qcow2 path.
Data for Generative AI Improvement: A LinkedIn account setting that permits LinkedIn to use a member's profile information and public content (posts, articles, comments) to train generative AI models, both its own and those of its affiliate Microsoft; private messages are stated to be excluded. Located under Settings & Privacy, Data Privacy. Arrived already enabled with the 18 September 2024 privacy-policy update, opt-out only, and is forward-only: switching it off stops future training but does not retract content already used. The subject of Not in the Brief Episode 04.
legitimate interest: One of the six lawful bases for processing personal data under Article 6 of the GDPR. It permits an organisation to process data where it has a genuine interest that is not overridden by the data subject's rights and freedoms, without requiring prior consent, provided the processing is disclosed and an objection (opt-out) is offered. The basis LinkedIn relies on for AI training on member data; lawful, but the courteous phrasing for 'we may, unless you tell us not to'.
Information Commissioner's Office (ICO): The United Kingdom's independent data-protection regulator, responsible for enforcing the UK GDPR and the Data Protection Act 2018. On 20 September 2024 it issued a statement welcoming that LinkedIn had paused training generative AI on data from members in the UK, the EEA and Switzerland, after raising concerns about the opt-out, default-on rollout.
404 Media: An American technology-journalism publication founded in 2023 by former Vice Motherboard journalists Jason Koebler, Emanuel Maiberg, Samantha Cole and Joseph Cox, operated as a journalist-owned cooperative. Reported in September 2024 that LinkedIn had begun using member data for AI training before its terms of service had been updated to disclose it.
Azure OpenAI: Microsoft's managed cloud service that provides API access to OpenAI's models (GPT, embeddings and others) through the Microsoft Azure platform, with enterprise data-governance and regional-hosting guarantees. Referenced in Not in the Brief Episode 04 as the model infrastructure LinkedIn's affiliate Microsoft draws on; the Data for Generative AI Improvement toggle governs sharing of member data to Microsoft for model training.
opt-out (default-on): A consent design in which a feature or data use is enabled by default and the user must take action to disable it, as opposed to opt-in, where the user must act to enable it. From an identical population, opt-out and opt-in produce wildly different participation rates, because the default is the decision for everyone who does not make one. The structural subject of Not in the Brief Episode 04: default-on with a quiet notice treats the absence of objection as the presence of consent.
Not in the Brief Episode 04: The fourth episode of Vivian Voss's 'Not in the Brief' series, published 23 May 2026. Documents LinkedIn's Data for Generative AI Improvement setting, which permits LinkedIn to use member profile information and public content to train generative AI models, its own and those of affiliate Microsoft, with private messages excluded. Covers the chronology (toggle appeared already enabled with the 18 September 2024 privacy-policy update, opt-out only; 404 Media reported data used before the terms disclosed it; the UK ICO secured a pause for the EEA, Switzerland and the UK on 20 September 2024; LinkedIn switched those regions on as well, with Canada and Hong Kong, on 3 November 2025), the mechanics (default-on enrolment, the legitimate-interest legal basis under the GDPR, the forward-only opt-out that does not retract content already used), and the verification path (Settings & Privacy, Data Privacy, Data for Generative AI Improvement). The judgement is about architecture and process rather than motive: this is not a breach or surveillance, but a design that treats silence as a yes and places the burden of awareness on the member.
SUnit: The original unit-testing framework, written by Kent Beck for Smalltalk in 1994 and described in his essay 'Simple Smalltalk Testing'. The ancestor of the xUnit family (JUnit, NUnit, PyUnit and the rest). Designed to test library code (parsers, algorithms, pure functions) where there is nothing to mock because there is no world to touch.
JUnit: The Java unit-testing framework, adapted from Kent Beck's SUnit by Beck and Erich Gamma in 1998. The most widely used testing framework in the Java ecosystem and the template for the broader xUnit family. JUnit 5 (2017) is the current major generation.
mock (testing): A test double that stands in for a real collaborator (a database, an API, a clock) and, in the mockist style, records and asserts on how it was called. Distinct from a stub (which only returns canned values) and a fake (a working lightweight implementation). The central instrument of mock-heavy testing; the subject of On Second Thought Episode 09, which reframes a high mock count as a reading of how far computation and I/O have been stirred together in the code under test.
dependency injection: A design technique in which an object receives its collaborators from outside (through its constructor or parameters) rather than constructing them itself, so that a different implementation, including a mock, can be substituted. Indispensable to mockist testing: you cannot mock what you cannot replace. Often introduced, in practice, primarily so that fakes have a seam to clip on.
Mocks Aren't Stubs: A 2007 essay by Martin Fowler distinguishing the two schools of test design: classicists, who use real objects where they can and assert on final state, and mockists (the London school), who test interactions by mocking collaborators. The canonical reference for the mockist-versus-classicist distinction; cited in On Second Thought Episode 09.
London school TDD: The mockist school of test-driven development, codified by Steve Freeman and Nat Pryce in 'Growing Object-Oriented Software, Guided by Tests' (2009). Tests an object by mocking its collaborators and asserting on the interactions between them, working outside-in from the system's boundaries. A coherent, disciplined practice; its generalisation is how 'unit test' came, for much of the industry, to mean 'test with the dependencies mocked out'.
Gary Bernhardt: American software developer, creator of the Destroy All Software screencast series and the much-shared 'Wat' lightning talk (2012). Best known in this context for his 2012 SCNA talk 'Boundaries', which named the functional core, imperative shell architecture: put all decisions and logic in a pure value-in-value-out core and confine side effects to a thin imperative shell.
functional core, imperative shell: An architectural pattern named by Gary Bernhardt in his 2012 talk 'Boundaries': all the decisions and logic live in a pure, value-in-value-out functional core that touches nothing, and the side effects (database, network, clock, UI) are confined to a thin imperative shell wrapped around it. The pure core needs no mocks because it touches nothing; the thin shell is covered by a small number of real integration tests. The modern name for the shape the Unix filter model had since the 1970s.
Alistair Cockburn: British-American computer scientist, one of the seventeen signatories of the 2001 Agile Manifesto. Originator of the hexagonal architecture pattern (conceived 1994), which he renamed 'ports and adapters' in 2005: the application core talks only to abstract ports, while adapters that touch the database, the network and the user interface live at the edges.
ports and adapters: Alistair Cockburn's name (2005) for his hexagonal architecture pattern: the application core communicates only through abstract ports, and concrete adapters at the edges connect those ports to the database, the network, the message bus and the user interface. Draws the same computation-versus-I/O boundary that functional core, imperative shell draws, with the emphasis on swappable edge adapters. Keeps the core testable without mocks.
Unix filter: A program that reads a stream of text on standard input, transforms it, and writes the result to standard output, touching nothing else: grep, awk, sort, cut, tr and the other small sharp tools that fill the FreeBSD and Unix base systems. Pure by construction (text in, text out), which makes a filter the most testable shape of program there is: you hand it input and read what returns, with no fake filesystem and no mock kernel, because the kernel and the shell handle the world. The original functional core with an imperative shell.
On Second Thought Episode 09: The ninth episode of Vivian Voss's 'On Second Thought' series, published 24 May 2026. Examines the routine of mocking everything to test our own logic. Traces the origin (Kent Beck's SUnit in 1994 tested pure library code with nothing to fake; the practice was carried into application code, which is nothing but world, and the world was faked with mocks and dependency injection; Martin Fowler's 2007 'Mocks Aren't Stubs' and the London school made it respectable until 'unit test' came to mean 'mocked test'), the three-layer cost (daily friction of 40 lines of setup to 2 of assertion and refactors turning 50 tests red; false confidence of 400 green tests and a broken checkout; the hidden design cost of seams and interfaces that exist only to receive a mock), and the answer (the Unix filter as the most testable software, pure text in and text out; McIlroy's pipe of 1973 as the original functional core; Gary Bernhardt's functional core, imperative shell of 2012 and Alistair Cockburn's ports and adapters of 2005). Closes with the reframing: a mock is not a tool but a reading on a gauge of how far the logic was allowed to wander into the world. Builds explicitly on The Copy Cat Episode 02, 'Unit Tests for Everything'.
GELI: FreeBSD's disk-encryption framework, implemented as a GEOM class rather than a standalone product. Initialised with geli init (default cipher AES-XTS, default AES-XTS key length 128 bits so -l 256 selects AES-256-XTS) and attached with geli attach, presenting an encrypted provider such as /dev/ada1.eli. Supports per-sector authenticated encryption via the -a HMAC/SHA256 flag (confidentiality and integrity in one tool), PKCS#5v2 (PBKDF2) key strengthening, multiple key files, and one-time-keyed encrypted swap through geom_eli. Composes uniformly with the rest of the GEOM stack.
GEOM: FreeBSD's modular block-storage framework. Every transformation of a disk (mirroring, striping, labelling, journalling, encryption) is a GEOM class that consumes one or more providers and presents a new provider through a uniform interface, so the classes stack in any sensible order with a single command vocabulary. The reason GELI encryption composes with gmirror, gstripe and glabel without bespoke glue: encryption is just another transform in one framework.
GEOM classes: The individual transforms in FreeBSD's GEOM block-storage framework: geli (encryption and integrity), gmirror (RAID-1 mirroring), gstripe (RAID-0 striping), glabel (persistent device labels), gjournal (block-level journalling) and others. Each consumes and presents providers with the same interface, so they compose uniformly. The architectural contrast with Linux's separate device-mapper subsystems.
LUKS: Linux Unified Key Setup. The standard on-disk format and key-management scheme for full-disk encryption on Linux, driven by the cryptsetup tool. A LUKS header holds the cipher parameters and up to thirty-two key slots, so multiple passphrases or key files can unlock the same master key and any one can be revoked without re-encrypting. LUKS2 (default since cryptsetup 2.1, 2019) defaults to AES-256-XTS and the memory-hard argon2id key-derivation function. Sits on top of the kernel's dm-crypt target via device-mapper.
cryptsetup: The user-facing command-line tool for managing LUKS and raw dm-crypt encrypted volumes on Linux. luksFormat writes a LUKS header; open (formerly luksOpen) decrypts the master key and creates a /dev/mapper device through device-mapper; --integrity adds authenticated encryption via dm-integrity. The Linux counterpart, in role, to FreeBSD's geli.
dm-crypt: The Linux kernel's disk-encryption subsystem, a device-mapper target that transparently encrypts and decrypts a block device. The crypto engine underneath LUKS; can also be used directly without a LUKS header (plain dm-crypt). Defaults, under LUKS2, to AES-256-XTS.
dm-integrity: A Linux device-mapper target that provides block-level data integrity, detecting modification or corruption of stored data. Stacked beneath dm-crypt (via cryptsetup --integrity) to provide authenticated encryption, the Linux equivalent of GELI's built-in HMAC flag, but as a second layer rather than a flag on the first.
device-mapper: The Linux kernel framework for constructing virtual block devices by mapping them onto other block devices, the basis of dm-crypt, dm-integrity, dm-verity, LVM and software RAID. Each capability is a separate device-mapper target with its own tooling, composed into a stack; the architectural counterpart to FreeBSD's single GEOM framework of uniform classes.
AES-XTS: The XTS-AES block-cipher mode (IEEE 1619), the standard mode for full-disk encryption. Designed for the random-access, fixed-sector pattern of disks, where each sector is encrypted independently using its position as a tweak. Used by both GELI and LUKS; AES-256-XTS uses a 256-bit key per XTS half. Provides confidentiality but not, by itself, integrity, which is why both systems add a separate authentication mechanism (GELI's HMAC flag, Linux's dm-integrity).
PBKDF2: Password-Based Key Derivation Function 2 (PKCS#5v2, RFC 8018). Derives a cryptographic key from a passphrase by applying a pseudo-random function many times, with the iteration count tuned to slow brute-force attacks. Compute-hard but not memory-hard, so it can be parallelised cheaply on GPUs and custom hardware. GELI's key-strengthening function; sound, but weaker against large-scale brute force than the memory-hard argon2id used by LUKS2.
argon2id: A hybrid variant of the Argon2 password-hashing function, winner of the 2015 Password Hashing Competition, combining resistance to side-channel and GPU attacks. Memory-hard: each guess requires provisioning a large block of memory (around one gibibyte by default in LUKS2) as well as computation, which makes large-scale brute-forcing expensive on GPUs and custom hardware. The default key-derivation function in LUKS2 and its genuine advantage over GELI's PBKDF2.
HMAC: Hash-based Message Authentication Code (RFC 2104). A keyed construction that verifies both the integrity and the authenticity of data using a cryptographic hash function such as SHA-256. In GELI, the -a HMAC/SHA256 flag stores a keyed HMAC per sector and verifies it on read, so a tampered or degraded sector is detected and reported rather than silently decrypted into corrupted plaintext; this is what turns GELI from confidentiality-only into authenticated encryption.
UFS: The Unix File System (also FFS, the Fast File System), the traditional native filesystem of FreeBSD and the BSDs, descended from the Berkeley Fast File System of 1984. Created with newfs. The default non-ZFS filesystem on FreeBSD; in the GELI workflow, UFS is laid on the attached /dev/ada1.eli provider, or the provider is handed to ZFS as a vdev instead.
The Unix Way Episode 18: The eighteenth episode of Vivian Voss's 'The Unix Way' series, published 25 May 2026. Compares full-disk encryption on FreeBSD and Linux. GELI is a GEOM class (geli init -e AES-XTS -l 256 -a HMAC/SHA256, then geli attach to get /dev/ada1.eli, taking UFS or a ZFS vdev), so encryption, per-sector HMAC integrity and one-time-keyed swap compose uniformly with gmirror, gstripe and glabel under one vocabulary, with integrity a flag rather than a subsystem. LUKS writes a LUKS2 header and opens the device through device-mapper as dm-crypt; its argon2id key derivation is memory-hard and genuinely ahead of GELI's PBKDF2, while on-disk integrity is a second layer (dm-integrity beneath dm-crypt). Both default to AES-256-XTS and both turn a stolen drive into noise. The architectural contrast: one framework of uniform GEOM transforms, or several specialised device-mapper subsystems (LUKS header, dm-crypt, dm-integrity, dm-verity, LVM, mdadm) composed. The modularity is why LUKS2 adopted argon2id ahead of GELI; the coherence is why the FreeBSD answer to encrypt, mirror and detect tampering is three flags and one mental model. The Unix way prefers the version you can hold in one hand.
find: A Unix command-line tool that walks a directory tree and evaluates an expression against each file, performing actions where the expression is true. Unlike most Unix tools, which take flags, find takes an expression: a small query language of primaries (tests and actions) and operators (implicit AND, -o for OR, ! for NOT, parentheses for grouping). Written by Dick Haight for Version 7 Unix in 1979. BSD find ships in the FreeBSD base system, BSD-licensed; GNU find is part of the GPL-licensed findutils.
Dick Haight: American software engineer at Bell Labs who, in the Unix Support Group (rather than the research group), wrote find, cpio and expr for Version 7 Unix, released in 1979. The expression-grammar design of find was, by the aesthetic of the research room, slightly foreign and read oddly compared with the other Unix tools, but it was kept because it was useful and has remained essentially unchanged for over four decades.
Unix Support Group: The part of Bell Labs, distinct from the research group that created Unix, charged with turning the research system into something AT&T could support and ship. Source of several Version 7 Unix tools including find, cpio and expr (Dick Haight). The USG tools were sometimes regarded by the researchers as stylistically foreign to the rest of Unix.
Version 7 Unix: The last version of Research Unix released by Bell Labs' Computing Science Research Center to be widely distributed, released in 1979. Hugely influential: it introduced the Bourne shell, find, awk (in a sense), and a mature C toolchain, and was the common ancestor of both the BSD and the System V lineages. Often cited as the canonical 'classic' Unix.
BSD find: The implementation of find that ships in the FreeBSD, OpenBSD, NetBSD and macOS base systems, BSD-licensed and located at /usr/bin/find on FreeBSD. The lean, POSIX-clean implementation, present on a fresh install with no package required. Distinct from GNU find, which adds more primaries and is GPL-licensed.
GNU findutils: The GNU project's package of file-finding utilities, including GNU find, xargs and locate, licensed under the GPL. GNU find grew a larger set of primaries over the years (-printf with its own format language, several regex variants and more) than POSIX or BSD find. On FreeBSD it is a pkg install findutils away, where GNU find is installed as gfind to avoid clashing with the base BSD find.
xargs: A Unix tool that reads items from standard input and builds and runs command lines from them, batching many items into as few invocations as possible. Paired with find -print0 and invoked as xargs -0, it reads null-terminated filenames, so filenames containing spaces or newlines pass through safely. The canonical safe way to move a list of arbitrary filenames from find to another tool.
fd (find tool): A modern command-line file-finding tool, written by David Peter (sharkdp) in Rust and first released in 2017, MIT and Apache licensed. Faster than traditional find, with friendlier defaults (it ignores .git and respects .gitignore) and prettier output, but reproduces the same fundamental idea: a small set of predicates over a directory-tree walk. The contemporary descendant of Dick Haight's 1979 find.
David Peter (sharkdp): German software engineer, known on GitHub as sharkdp. Author of a family of fast, friendly Rust command-line tools including fd (a find replacement, 2017), bat (a cat replacement with syntax highlighting) and hyperfine (a benchmarking tool). His tools are widely adopted as modern, ergonomic versions of the classic Unix utilities.
Technical Beauty Episode 37: The thirty-seventh episode of Vivian Voss's 'Technical Beauty' series, published 26 May 2026. Celebrates find as a conceptual reduction: most Unix tools take flags, but find takes an expression, a small query language of primaries (-name, -type, -mtime, -size, -newer) and operators (implicit AND, -o, !, grouping) that the tool evaluates against each file during a tree walk, firing actions where true. Small vocabulary to large expressivity. Covers the surface (the daily one-liners, -exec {} + batching versus {} ; per-file, and the safe -print0 piped to xargs -0 for arbitrary filenames), the FreeBSD anchor (BSD find in base, BSD-licensed and POSIX-clean, versus GNU findutils under the GPL), and the lineage (Dick Haight wrote find with cpio and expr for Version 7 Unix in 1979 in the Unix Support Group; the researchers were put off by the syntax and kept it because it was useful; the grammar is unchanged forty-seven years on, and the modern descendant fd by David Peter reproduces the same idea). find is the rare Unix tool that is a little language pretending to be a command.
OVHcloud Strasbourg fire: A fire at OVHcloud's Strasbourg (SBG) data-centre campus that began shortly before 01:00 on 10 March 2021 in a power room. SBG2 was destroyed entirely, SBG1 badly damaged, SBG3 and SBG4 powered down; roughly 3.6 million websites and around 464,000 domains were unavailable at the peak, close to 18 per cent of OVH's active IP addresses, and data was permanently lost for customers whose only copy lived on-site. The French BEA-RI report (June 2022) records high humidity near a power inverter before ignition and stops short of a single definitive cause; the spread is attributed to SBG2's free-cooling tower design (a chimney once alight), wooden floors rated for about an hour, and the absence of any automatic fire-extinguishing system. The subject of Tales from the Bare Metal Episode 05.
BEA-RI: The French Bureau d'Enquêtes et d'Analyses sur les Risques Industriels, the bureau of investigation and analysis on industrial risks. Published the official investigation report into the OVHcloud Strasbourg data-centre fire in June 2022, recording high humidity readings near a power inverter in the hour before ignition while deliberately stopping short of asserting a single definitive cause.
free cooling: A data-centre cooling strategy that uses outside air or ambient conditions to remove server heat instead of mechanical chillers, greatly reducing energy use. OVHcloud's SBG2 used a free-cooling tower design (sometimes called auto-ventilation): waste heat rose and vented at the top while cooler outside air was drawn in at the bottom. Energy-efficient for a decade, but the same natural updraught behaved like a chimney once a fire took hold, feeding and lifting it.
failure domain: The set of components that can fail together as a unit because they share a common dependency: a power supply, a rack, a room, a building, a data centre, a metropolitan area, a region. The central concept of disaster-recovery design: redundancy only helps when the copies live in different failure domains. A backup in the same building as the primary shares a failure domain with it, so a single fire, flood or power event destroys both. The lesson of the OVHcloud Strasbourg fire.
3-2-1 backup rule: A long-standing backup guideline: keep at least three copies of your data, on at least two different kinds of media, with at least one copy off-site. The decisive number is the one: off-site means a different failure domain, far enough that a fire, flood or power event at the primary cannot reach it, not merely a different rack or room. Predates the cloud and remains the answer to the OVHcloud Strasbourg failure mode, where intact backups burned in the same building as the originals.
Availability Zone: An AWS (and, by analogy, other clouds') unit of fault isolation: a cluster of one or more data centres in a single metropolitan area with independent power, cooling and networking. Multi-AZ deployment survives a host, rack or single-data-centre fault, but an Availability Zone is not a unit of geographic separation; a region is. The off-site copy that survives a regional event is cross-region replication, a separate and deliberate setting. A common source of the false confidence that multi-AZ equals disaster recovery.
geo-redundant storage: A cloud storage redundancy tier that replicates data to a second region hundreds of kilometres away, as opposed to locally-redundant storage (LRS), which keeps all copies within a single data centre. On Microsoft Azure the tiers are LRS (one data centre) and GRS (a paired region); the cheaper default keeps the copies in one failure domain. The cloud equivalent of the 3-2-1 rule's off-site requirement.
Velero: An open-source tool for backing up and restoring Kubernetes cluster resources and persistent volumes, originally developed by Heptio (acquired by VMware) and now a CNCF project. Frequently misconfigured so that the backups live on the same cluster or in object storage in the same region as the cluster, leaving them in one failure domain; the disaster-recovery practice is to ship Velero and etcd snapshots off-cluster and off-region.
restic: An open-source backup program written in Go by Alexander Neumann, first released in 2015. Provides encrypted, deduplicated, incremental backups to local disk, SFTP or object storage (S3, B2, Azure, Google Cloud and others). Commonly used to replicate backups to a different region or provider, satisfying the off-site requirement of the 3-2-1 rule.
borg (BorgBackup): An open-source deduplicating backup program, written largely in Python with a C core, forked from Attic in 2015. Provides compressed, encrypted, incremental backups with strong deduplication across backup sets. Paired with a remote repository (often via borgmatic and SSH), it satisfies the off-site copy of the 3-2-1 rule.
Uptime Institute: An American advisory organisation founded in 1993, best known for its data-centre Tier Classification System (Tiers I to IV) and its operational-resilience research. Published a widely-cited analysis of the OVHcloud Strasbourg fire ('Learning from the OVHcloud data center fire') examining the free-cooling design, the construction and the absence of automatic suppression.
Tales from the Bare Metal Episode 05: The fifth episode of Vivian Voss's 'Tales from the Bare Metal' series, published 27 May 2026. Documents the OVHcloud Strasbourg data-centre fire of 10 March 2021: a power-room fire that destroyed SBG2 entirely, badly damaged SBG1, took ~3.6 million websites offline and permanently lost data for customers whose only copy lived on-site. Traces the spread to three design facts (a free-cooling tower that behaved like a chimney, wooden floors rated for about an hour, no automatic fire-extinguishing system in any of the five buildings) and three systemic conditions (the mental model that 'in the data centre' means safe, hosting panels that present backup as a feature rather than a question of geography, and the location of a backup being a checkbox nobody owned). The principle is the 3-2-1 rule, with the off-site copy meaning a different failure domain; distinguished from Episode 01 (GitLab), which was about restorability, since separation and restorability are independent axes and a backup needs both. The FreeBSD answer is zfs send to a remote pool, or restic or borg to another region. Closes: redundancy that shares a postcode is decoration; the fire does not read your architecture diagram, it reads the floor plan.
cPanel: A commercial Linux-based web-hosting control panel, first released in 1997, that gives hosting customers a graphical interface to manage email, databases, files and websites. Paired with WHM (Web Host Manager) for the server administrator side. The default control panel for a large share of the world's commercial shared hosting. The user-facing surface through which the CVE-2026-48172 LiteSpeed plugin flaw was reachable.
WHM: Web Host Manager, the server-administration counterpart to cPanel. Where cPanel gives individual customers control of their own account, WHM gives the hosting operator control of the whole server: creating accounts, setting limits, installing plugins. The LiteSpeed WHM plugin (5.3.1.0) carried the 2.4.7 fix for CVE-2026-48172.
LiteSpeed: A commercial high-performance web server (LiteSpeed Web Server, LSWS) developed by LiteSpeed Technologies, marketed as a drop-in replacement for Apache with better performance under load. Integrated into cPanel and WHM through the LiteSpeed cPanel plugin, the component in which CVE-2026-48172 was found.
CVE-2026-48172: A critical privilege-escalation vulnerability in the LiteSpeed cPanel plugin, disclosed in May 2026. The plugin's lsws.redisAble function, reachable through the normal cPanel JSON API by any logged-in customer, did not verify the caller's identity and ran with root authority (CWE-266). Affected plugin versions 2.3 through 2.4.4; fixed in 2.4.7 (WHM plugin 5.3.1.0). cPanel pulled the plugin on 19 May; LiteSpeed shipped a fix on 21 May; CISA added it to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalogue on 26 May as an actively exploited zero-day. Rated 10.0 on CVSS v4.0. The subject of Wire Fire Episode 03.
CISA KEV catalogue: The Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalogue maintained by the United States Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA). A continuously updated list of vulnerabilities for which CISA has reliable evidence of active exploitation in the wild, used to prioritise remediation for federal agencies and widely consulted by the industry. CVE-2026-48172 was added on 26 May 2026.
CVSS: Common Vulnerability Scoring System, the industry-standard framework for rating the severity of software vulnerabilities on a 0 to 10 scale. CVSS v3.1 and the newer v4.0 use different metric sets and can produce different numbers for the same flaw: CVE-2026-48172 scored 10.0 (the maximum) on v4.0 and 9.8 on v3.1.
CWE-266: Incorrect Privilege Assignment, an entry in the Common Weakness Enumeration (CWE), the catalogue of software-weakness types. Describes code that runs with more privilege than the caller was entitled to, typically because a privileged operation is exposed without an adequate check of who is invoking it. The class of flaw behind CVE-2026-48172, where lsws.redisAble ran as root for any logged-in customer.
privilege escalation: A class of attack in which an actor gains capabilities beyond those they were granted: from an unprivileged user to an administrator, or from a guest to root. Vertical escalation raises privilege level (the CVE-2026-48172 case, an ordinary customer reaching root); horizontal escalation reaches another peer's resources. On a shared server, escalation to root collapses every tenant boundary at once.
shared hosting: A web-hosting model in which many customer accounts, each a separate website and owner, run side by side on one server under one operating system, one kernel and one root account, sharing the machine's resources. Cheap and ubiquitous, it separates tenants by user permissions and control-panel logic rather than by a kernel-enforced boundary, so a privilege-escalation flaw in shared software can expose every account at once. The structural subject of Wire Fire Episode 03.
zero-day: A vulnerability that is exploited in the wild before the vendor has released a patch, leaving defenders zero days to prepare. CVE-2026-48172 was a zero-day: CISA confirmed active exploitation, and cPanel's emergency removal of the plugin on 19 May preceded the LiteSpeed fix on 21 May.
Wire Fire Episode 03: The third episode of Vivian Voss's 'Wire Fire' series, published 28 May 2026. Documents CVE-2026-48172, a critical privilege-escalation flaw in the LiteSpeed cPanel plugin that let any logged-in shared-hosting customer become root. Covers the timeline (cPanel pulled the plugin on 19 May citing unauthorized root access, LiteSpeed fixed it on 21 May, CISA listed it as actively exploited on 26 May; rated 10.0 on CVSS v4.0), the scope (versions 2.3 to 2.4.4, shared-hosting servers across industry, retail, media and a long tail), the mechanism (lsws.redisAble reachable via the normal cPanel JSON API ran as root without checking the caller, CWE-266, so one account or one phished password held the whole machine and every site on it), the exposure check (upgrade to 2.4.7 or remove the plugin, grep for cpanel_jsonapi_func=redisAble, audit new accounts, ask your host), and the pattern (shared hosting sells a boundary it does not enforce; the FreeBSD jail enforces the same boundary in the kernel, as do Linux containers and illumos zones). On shared hosting your security boundary is not your account; it is the weakest account on the same machine.
Java: A general-purpose programming language and runtime created at Sun Microsystems and released in 1995, built on the promise of 'write once, run anywhere': compile to bytecode once and run on any machine with a Java Virtual Machine. The language the enterprise quietly settled on for banking, logistics, payment processing and middleware. Open-sourced as OpenJDK in 2006-2007; Sun was acquired by Oracle in 2010. The subject of In the Net Episode 05, which traces how the licence under it changed from per-user to per-employee.
Oracle: An American enterprise-software and database company founded by Larry Ellison, Bob Miner and Ed Oates in 1977. Owner of the Oracle Database, the Java platform (via the 2010 acquisition of Sun Microsystems), and a large enterprise applications portfolio. Known for an aggressive licence-audit practice; the per-employee Java SE Universal Subscription (2023) and the VMware-cluster Database licensing position are the canonical examples. The platform of In the Net Episode 05.
OpenJDK: The official open-source reference implementation of the Java Platform, Standard Edition, from which Oracle's own branded JDK is built. Open-sourced by Sun in 2006-2007 under the GPLv2 with the Classpath Exception. From Java 11 onward, the Oracle build and a current OpenJDK build are, for almost all purposes, the same binary with different labels and support terms; switching away from Oracle's JDK is a reinstall, not a rewrite. Production builds include Eclipse Temurin, Amazon Corretto, Azul Zulu and others.
Java SE Universal Subscription: Oracle's Java licensing model introduced on 23 January 2023, which retired the older per-user (Named User Plus) and per-processor metrics for new subscriptions and replaced them with a per-employee metric. 'Employee' is defined as the entire organisation (full-time, part-time and temporary staff plus contractors, consultants and agents), not the people who use Java, so a single server running Oracle's Java makes the whole headcount billable. List pricing starts around 15 US dollars per employee per month, stepping down by volume. Gartner estimated it at two to five times the cost of the previous model.
Oracle JDK: Oracle's commercial branded build of the Java Development Kit, built from the OpenJDK source. From Java 11 (2019) its use in commercial production required a paid subscription; Java 17 (2021) softened this with the free No-Fee Terms and Conditions licence. Functionally near-identical to a current OpenJDK build from Java 11 on. Its download and installation history is the first exhibit in an Oracle Java licence audit.
TCK: Technology Compatibility Kit, the test suite that certifies a Java implementation as genuinely compatible with the Java SE specification. Oracle controls the Java TCK, which is one of the three things (alongside the trademark and the official commercial binary) that gives Oracle standing over an otherwise open language. A build that passes the TCK, such as Eclipse Temurin or Amazon Corretto, is genuine Java.
JVM: Java Virtual Machine, the abstract runtime that executes Java bytecode and is the mechanism behind the 'write once, run anywhere' promise: a program compiled to bytecode runs on any machine with a conforming JVM, regardless of operating system or processor. The component whose licence, not whose behaviour, is the subject of In the Net Episode 05.
Eclipse Temurin: A free, vendor-neutral production build of OpenJDK from the Eclipse Adoptium project (formerly AdoptOpenJDK). TCK-tested, licensed under GPLv2 with the Classpath Exception, and free for commercial use. One of the canonical drop-in replacements for Oracle's JDK; from Java 11 it is built from the same source tree as Oracle's binary.
Amazon Corretto: Amazon's free, multi-platform, production-ready distribution of OpenJDK, released in 2018. TCK-certified, free for commercial use including long-term support, and used internally across Amazon's own services. One of the standard OpenJDK builds to which organisations migrate away from Oracle's JDK.
Azul Zulu: A build of OpenJDK from Azul Systems, available in a free Community edition and a commercially supported edition. Azul is a commercial OpenJDK vendor and an Oracle competitor; it commissioned the Dimensional Research survey cited in In the Net Episode 05 that reported 73 per cent of Oracle Java users audited in three years.
BellSoft Liberica: A build of OpenJDK from BellSoft, TCK-tested and free for commercial use, notable for offering a wide range of platforms and a full-JRE-with-JavaFX variant. One of the production OpenJDK distributions organisations adopt when leaving Oracle's JDK.
Dimensional Research: An American market-research firm that conducts technology surveys, often commissioned by vendors. Conducted the survey, commissioned by Azul (a commercial OpenJDK vendor and Oracle competitor), that reported 73 per cent of Oracle Java users had been audited in the previous three years and 81 per cent had moved or planned to move to open-source Java. Cited in In the Net Episode 05 with the commissioning interest disclosed.
Oracle Database licensing: Oracle's licensing regime for its flagship database, known for an audit position that, on a VMware cluster, can require licensing every physical core the software could move to (the whole cluster), not only the host it runs on, because Oracle does not recognise VMware as a valid partitioning boundary. The same per-metric, audit-enforced pattern as the per-employee Java subscription; PostgreSQL and MariaDB carry no equivalent per-core, whole-cluster exposure.
MariaDB: An open-source relational database, forked from MySQL in 2009 by MySQL's original author Michael Widenius after Oracle's acquisition of Sun (and thereby MySQL). Drop-in compatible with MySQL for most uses, licensed under GPLv2. Cited alongside PostgreSQL in In the Net Episode 05 as a database with no per-core, whole-cluster audit exposure.
Classpath Exception: A clause added to the GNU General Public License version 2 (GPLv2) under which OpenJDK is released, permitting programs to link against the Java class libraries without the linking program itself being subject to the GPL's copyleft. The reason OpenJDK builds such as Eclipse Temurin and Amazon Corretto are free for unrestricted commercial use, including in proprietary applications.
In the Net Episode 05: The fifth episode of Vivian Voss's 'In the Net' series, published 29 May 2026. The fifth distinct lock-in genus in the series: lock-in by definition rather than by format, with licence-compliance audit as the sales channel. Documents Oracle's Java SE Universal Subscription (23 January 2023), which replaced the per-user and per-processor metrics with a per-employee metric that counts the entire organisation, not Java users, so one install makes the whole headcount billable at around 15 US dollars per employee per month. Covers Oracle's standing (brand, commercial builds, the TCK), the audit enforcement (a Dimensional Research survey commissioned by Azul reporting 73 per cent audited and 81 per cent migrating; Gartner's two-to-five-times estimate), the genuinely open but past-trapped exit (OpenJDK is a reinstall from Java 11, but download history feeds a retroactive workforce-scoped claim), the arithmetic (a 12,000-staff firm pays over 2 million euros a year regardless of use), and the escape route (free OpenJDK builds such as Eclipse Temurin, Amazon Corretto, Azul Zulu, BellSoft Liberica; inventory legacy Oracle JDK first; PostgreSQL and MariaDB for the same Oracle Database audit pattern). Write once, run anywhere. Licence once, pay for everyone.
Android: Google's mobile operating system, based on a modified Linux kernel and open-source via the Android Open Source Project (AOSP), with vendor-specific layers added by manufacturers (Pixel, Samsung One UI, others). Releases referenced in Not in the Brief Episode 05: Android 14 (October 2023), Android 15 (October 2024), Android 16 (2025).
iOS: Apple's mobile operating system, the iPhone's platform since 2007. A closed-source vendor-controlled OS where the assistant role (Siri), the wake word, the home gesture and the system microphone are kept by Apple and not exposed to third-party extensions. iOS 27, due in autumn 2026, introduces an Extensions framework for sandboxed third-party AI chatbots (Gemini, Claude, ChatGPT) at the application layer.
iOS 27: The 2026 release of Apple's mobile operating system, expected in autumn 2026. Introduces a new Extensions framework for third-party AI chatbots (Gemini, Claude, ChatGPT) as sandboxed extensions at the application layer, opt-in and isolated from the system assistant role. Cited in Not in the Brief Episode 05 as the architectural counterpoint to Android's slot-level installation of Gemini.
Gemini: Google's family of multimodal foundation language models, successor to Bard, used across Google's product surface (Search overviews, Workspace, Pixel system apps) and shipped as the standalone Gemini app and assistant. On Android, Gemini replaced Google Assistant as the OS-level digital-assistant role holder; on iOS 27 it ships as a sandboxed Extension.
Google Assistant: Google's earlier conversational assistant, available since 2016 across Android, Google Home, Wear OS, Android Auto and Google TV. Architected as a search agent that routed voice queries to known endpoints. Replaced by Gemini in the Android digital-assistant role; the standalone product retires on or around 31 March 2026.
assistant role (Android): A system-level hook on Android that exposes a single 'digital assistant' slot to all applications. Exactly one application at a time holds it; the long-press of the home button, the wake word and the assistant intent from any third-party app all route to the holder. Until 2024 the holder was Google Assistant; since the Gemini rollout the holder is Gemini, or None.
Pixel 10: Google's flagship smartphone, announced 20 August 2025 at the Made by Google event in Brooklyn and shipping from 28 August 2025. The first Pixel to ship with Gemini as the default assistant in the OS slot from day one. Powered by the Tensor G5 SoC on a TSMC 3 nm process; runs Gemini Nano on-device.
Tensor G5: Google's fifth-generation custom mobile system-on-chip, used in the Pixel 10. Manufactured by TSMC on a 3 nm process; designed to run Gemini Nano locally for on-device features such as Magic Compose, Call Notes, Recorder summaries and Scam Detection.
Magic Compose: A feature in Google Messages that drafts suggested replies based on recent conversation history. On Pixel-class hardware it runs on Gemini Nano on-device and works without an internet connection, offering style variants such as Formal, Excited and Chill.
Call Notes: A Pixel-exclusive feature that records a phone call and uses Gemini Nano to produce a written summary of the conversation, available immediately after the call.
Pixel Recorder: Google's voice-recording app on Pixel devices. Uses Gemini Nano on-device to produce automatic summaries of recordings, including three-bullet summaries for recordings longer than thirty minutes (introduced 2024-2025).
Scam Detection: A Pixel feature launched in 2025 that uses Gemini Nano on-device to analyse phone calls in real time and warn the user when the patterns of a fraud script appear in the conversation. No call audio leaves the device.
Wear OS: Google's wearable operating system, derived from Android, used in smartwatches including the Pixel Watch. Part of the broader Gemini-rollout transition path from Google Assistant.
Android Auto: Google's in-car projection platform that mirrors a smartphone's interface onto a vehicle's head-unit display. Part of the broader Gemini-rollout transition path from Google Assistant.
Google TV: Google's smart-TV platform, the successor to Android TV's user experience, used on Chromecast with Google TV and TVs from various manufacturers. Part of the broader Gemini-rollout transition path from Google Assistant.
Google Home: Google's smart-home platform and assistant-speaker ecosystem (Nest speakers and displays). Part of the broader Gemini-rollout transition path from Google Assistant.
Siri: Apple's voice assistant, introduced with the iPhone 4S in 2011. The system-level assistant on iOS, iPadOS, watchOS and macOS; the wake word, the home gesture and the system microphone all route to it. Following the January 2026 Apple-Google partnership, iOS 27 lets users add Gemini (and Claude, ChatGPT) as sandboxed Extensions alongside Siri rather than in its slot.
Alphabet: The holding company that owns Google, listed on NASDAQ (GOOGL, GOOG). 2025 revenue approximately 402 billion US dollars, with advertising (Google Search around 224 billion and YouTube around 40 billion) at approximately three quarters and Google Cloud at about 14.57 per cent (around 58 billion). Cited in Not in the Brief Episode 05 as the filings-based basis for the 'placement is the product' end of the architectural-spectrum argument.
Google Cloud: Alphabet's cloud-computing division, which competes with Amazon Web Services and Microsoft Azure. About 14.57 per cent of Alphabet's 2025 revenue (around 58 billion US dollars), per the FY2025 SEC 10-Q filing.
Apple FY2025: Apple's fiscal year 2025, ending late September 2025. Revenue mix approximately 74 per cent hardware (iPhone, Mac, iPad, Wearables) and 26 per cent services (App Store, iCloud, Apple Music, Apple TV+, AppleCare, advertising, payments). Services margin above 70 per cent; hardware margin approximately 35 to 40 per cent. Cited in Not in the Brief Episode 05 as the filings-based basis for the 'device is the product' end of the architectural-spectrum argument.
Extensions framework (iOS 27): A new application-layer framework Apple introduced in iOS 27 (autumn 2026) for sandboxed third-party AI chatbots (Gemini, Claude, ChatGPT). Extensions are opt-in, isolated from the assistant role, and invoked only when the user explicitly calls them; the wake word, the home gesture and the system microphone remain Apple's.
Made by Google event: Google's annual hardware-launch event. The 20 August 2025 edition, held in Brooklyn, announced the Pixel 10 family, the Pixel Watch and a range of Gemini-powered features.
Bard: Google's first publicly released conversational AI product (March 2023), based on the LaMDA and later PaLM 2 models. Rebranded as Gemini in February 2024 when the underlying model family changed name.
9to5Google: An independent news site covering Google products and the broader Android ecosystem. Cited in Not in the Brief Episode 05 as the source reporting Google's December 2025 confirmation of the full Google Assistant sunset for March 2026.
MacRumors: An independent news site covering Apple products and platforms. Cited in Not in the Brief Episode 05 as the source reporting Apple's iOS 27 Extensions framework for third-party AI chatbots.
Joe Armstrong: Swedish computer scientist (1950 to 2019), co-creator of Erlang at Ericsson's Computer Science Laboratory in Stockholm in 1986 together with Robert Virding and Mike Williams. Author of the 2003 PhD thesis 'Making Reliable Distributed Systems in the Presence of Software Errors'; co-author of the OTP supervisor model. Coined the phrase 'let it crash' as the operational principle for fault-tolerant Erlang systems.
Robert Virding: Swedish computer scientist, co-creator of Erlang at Ericsson in 1986 together with Joe Armstrong and Mike Williams. Co-author of the original Erlang reference book and a long-time contributor to the language and the BEAM ecosystem.
Mike Williams: Swedish computer scientist, co-creator of Erlang at Ericsson in 1986 together with Joe Armstrong and Robert Virding. Worked on the early Erlang runtime and the AXD301 ATM switch.
Ericsson: Swedish multinational telecommunications-equipment company founded 1876, originator of Erlang. The Computer Science Laboratory in Stockholm where Joe Armstrong, Robert Virding and Mike Williams began Erlang in 1986; the company also developed the AXD301 ATM switch built on roughly two million lines of Erlang.
OTP: Open Telecom Platform, the standard library and framework shipped with Erlang since 1998. Provides the documented supervisor model, generic-server (gen_server) and other behaviours; the practical implementation of let-it-crash architecture used by every production Erlang and Elixir system. The 'Erlang/OTP' distribution is what most users install.
AXD301: Ericsson's ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode) telephone switch, built on roughly two million lines of Erlang. The system most often cited as evidence for Erlang's nine-nines reliability claim (on the order of 31 milliseconds of downtime per year); the figure is contested in the way every figure of that shape is contested (whether the measurement was apples-to-apples, whether it included planned maintenance), but the supervisor architecture that produced it is uncontested.
let it crash: An operational principle in Erlang and OTP coined by Joe Armstrong: when a process gets into a bad state, terminate it and let its supervisor decide what to do next. Read as a slogan it sounds like the consumer 'have you tried turning it off and on again' tradition; read as architecture it is the opposite, because it presumes isolated processes, a supervisor with a written restart strategy and an escalation limit, and a contract that was written before the outage.
supervisor tree (Erlang/OTP): The OTP architecture in which every worker process has a supervisor process, every supervisor has a parent supervisor, and a crash is delivered as a message up the tree. Each supervisor has a written restart strategy (one-for-one, one-for-all, rest-for-one) and a maximum restart frequency; when the frequency is exceeded the supervisor itself crashes and the failure escalates one level up. A failure is data, handled by a rule written years before the outage.
one-for-one: An OTP supervisor restart strategy: when a child process crashes, only that child is restarted; siblings keep running. The most common strategy for independent workers under a single supervisor.
one-for-all: An OTP supervisor restart strategy: when any child process crashes, all child processes under the same supervisor are terminated and restarted. Used when siblings share state or have to come up together.
rest-for-one: An OTP supervisor restart strategy: when a child crashes, that child and every child started after it in the dependency order are restarted; earlier children keep running. Used for ordered start-up dependencies.
liveness probe: A Kubernetes probe (HTTP, TCP or exec) that the kubelet runs periodically to determine whether a container is still alive. A container that fails its liveness probe is killed and replaced. Cited in On Second Thought Episode 10 as the structural locus of the modern restart reflex: the orchestrator rotates the symptom while the cause goes unexamined.
readiness probe: A Kubernetes probe (HTTP, TCP or exec) that the kubelet runs periodically to determine whether a container is ready to receive traffic. A container that fails its readiness probe is temporarily removed from service endpoints but not killed.
self-healing (cloud-native marketing ter: The marketing label applied to systems in which an orchestrator (typically Kubernetes) automatically kills and replaces failing workloads in response to liveness-probe failures. Accurate in the sense that the symptom is repeatedly removed; misleading in the sense that the cause is not addressed. Compared in On Second Thought Episode 10 to taking paracetamol every four hours and calling the headache self-healing.
init (FreeBSD): Process 1 on a FreeBSD system, started by the kernel; the parent of every user-space process. Reads /etc/ttys and brings the system to multi-user mode by running rc, which executes rc.d scripts in dependency order. The unix-tradition counterpart of the Stockholm supervisor model: explicit start, dependency, recovery.
rc.d (FreeBSD): The FreeBSD subsystem for starting and managing services. Each service has a shell script in /etc/rc.d (base) or /usr/local/etc/rc.d (packages) declaring REQUIRE and PROVIDE for dependency ordering, plus start, stop and restart actions. Used in On Second Thought Episode 10 as the operator's half of the supervisor picture: explicit start, explicit dependency, explicit recovery.
DTrace (FreeBSD): A dynamic tracing framework originally developed by Sun Microsystems for Solaris (2003-2005), ported to FreeBSD where it is part of the base system. Allows inspection of kernel and user-space behaviour live in production with low overhead, via probes written in the D scripting language. Cited in On Second Thought Episode 10 as one of the FreeBSD diagnostic surfaces that make the reboot rarely the first reach when a service misbehaves.
ktrace: A FreeBSD command that enables kernel-level tracing of system calls and other events for a given process. Output is written to a trace file (ktrace.out) and read with kdump. The lower-level inspection tool when DTrace is not enough; cited in On Second Thought Episode 10 alongside DTrace as part of the FreeBSD diagnostic surface.
procstat: A FreeBSD command that displays detailed information about a running process: file descriptors, mappings, signals, threads, kernel stack. Reads kernel-side process state at low cost. Cited in On Second Thought Episode 10 alongside fstat as a way to see what a process is actually holding when a service misbehaves.
fstat (FreeBSD): A FreeBSD command that lists open files for processes: who has which file descriptors, including network sockets, pipes and devices. The classical way to discover that a daemon ran out of file descriptors. Cited in On Second Thought Episode 10 alongside procstat as part of the FreeBSD diagnostic surface.
core dump: A file written to disk when a process terminates abnormally, containing the contents of the process's memory and register state at the moment of the crash. On FreeBSD core dumps are produced for SIGSEGV, SIGBUS, SIGABRT and similar signals (subject to ulimit and kern.corefile); read with lldb, gdb or kgdb. Cited in On Second Thought Episode 10 as the post-mortem half of the FreeBSD diagnostic surface that survives the crash and can be examined at leisure.
nine nines: A reliability claim of 99.9999999 per cent availability, equivalent to about 31 milliseconds of downtime per year. The figure most often associated with the Ericsson AXD301 ATM switch; contested in the way every figure of that shape is contested (whether the measurement was apples-to-apples, whether it included planned maintenance, whether the operational data was systematically collected), but the supervisor architecture that produced it is uncontested.
The IT Crowd: British sitcom created by Graham Linehan and broadcast on Channel 4 (2006 to 2013), set in the basement IT support office of Reynholm Industries. The show's catchphrase 'have you tried turning it off and on again' (delivered by the character Roy Trenneman, played by Chris O'Dowd) became the canonical shorthand for the consumer-electronics restart tradition. The cult opening-scene compilation is at https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rksCTVFtjM4. Referenced in On Second Thought Episode 10 as the joke that became a runbook.
Sky+ box: Sky's personal-video-recorder set-top box, launched in 2001 and widely deployed in the United Kingdom and Ireland through the 2000s. Used in On Second Thought Episode 10 as the canonical example of the consumer-electronics tradition: a device offering no language in which to be asked where it had gone wrong, only a power button.
Kafka: Apache Kafka, an open-source distributed event-streaming platform originally developed at LinkedIn (2011) and later donated to the Apache Software Foundation. A persistent, partitioned, replicated log used for high-throughput pub-sub messaging and stream processing. Mentioned in On Second Thought Episode 10 as one of the systems where a consumer falling off a partition triggers the modern restart reflex.
libprocstat: A stable, well-documented C library on FreeBSD for retrieving information about running processes from the kernel: file descriptors, memory mappings, threads, kernel stacks, security credentials, sockets. Introduced by Robert N M Watson and shipped with FreeBSD 9.0 in 2012. Every FreeBSD program that needs process state (procstat, top, gstat, debugger front-ends, monitoring tools) can link against libprocstat directly, so the user-facing tools are thin clients of one common source rather than each parsing text or scraping /proc.
lsof: List Open Files. A Unix utility that reports information about files opened by processes (regular files, directories, sockets, pipes, anonymous inodes, character and block devices, the synthetic entries the kernel exposes through /proc, and a great many other kinds). Written by Vic Abell at Purdue University and first released to comp.sources.unix in 1991. On Linux, almost everything lsof reports is derived from /proc; the binary is the interface and there is no liblsof. Daily reaches: lsof -p pid (one process), lsof +D /path (recursive), lsof -i :port (network), lsof +L1 (deleted-but-held files, link count below one), lsof -n -P (skip DNS and service-name lookups).
Vic Abell: Computer scientist at Purdue University in Indiana, author of lsof. In 1989 he published ports of BSD's fstat and ofiles to DYNIX, SunOS and ULTRIX; in 1991 he released lsof 1.0 to comp.sources.unix, a generalisation of the BSD idea written to work across the many Unix-likes of the early 1990s. Maintained lsof as a single program through its long life; the binary's sustained existence is what makes its breadth possible.
Robert N M Watson: British computer scientist and FreeBSD developer, currently at the University of Cambridge Computer Laboratory. Among many other contributions, the introducer of procstat(1) and libprocstat(3) in FreeBSD 9.0 (2012), of the Capsicum capability framework, and of the MAC framework. The architect responsible for FreeBSD's layered, library-mediated approach to process introspection.
4.3BSD-Tahoe: A 1988 release of the Berkeley Software Distribution, the version of Unix produced at the University of California, Berkeley's Computer Systems Research Group. Notable for adding support for the Power 6/32 minicomputer (codename Tahoe) and for shipping fstat(1) in the base system; fstat has remained in the FreeBSD base ever since.
fstat(1) (FreeBSD): A FreeBSD command, in the base system since 4.3BSD-Tahoe in 1988, that lists open files by every process: file descriptors, the path the fd points at (or a dash when the file has been unlinked), the user, the command and the mode. Narrowed by -p pid, -u user, an exact path, or -f mountpoint. The classical first reach for the deleted-but-held diagnostic: fstat -p pid piped to awk dollar-five equals dash.
DYNIX: A Unix-like operating system developed by Sequent Computer Systems in the mid-1980s for symmetric multiprocessor machines, based on 4.2BSD. One of the Unix-likes to which Vic Abell ported BSD's fstat and ofiles in 1989, the work that became lsof.
SunOS: Sun Microsystems' Unix operating system, sold on Sun workstations and servers from the early 1980s. SunOS 1.x to 4.x were BSD-based; SunOS 5.x was rebranded Solaris and switched to a System V base. One of the Unix-likes Vic Abell targeted with the 1989 BSD-tools port that became lsof.
ULTRIX: Digital Equipment Corporation's Unix variant for VAX and DECstation hardware (1984 to early 1990s), based on 4.2BSD. One of the Unix-likes Vic Abell targeted with the 1989 BSD-tools port that became lsof.
comp.sources.unix: A Usenet newsgroup of the late 1980s and early 1990s used for distributing source code of Unix utilities. The original publication channel for many widely used Unix tools, including lsof 1.0 (Vic Abell, 1991).
Purdue University: A public research university in West Lafayette, Indiana, founded 1869. The institution at whose Computing Center Vic Abell wrote and released lsof; the development home of the tool through much of its lifetime.
procstat (FreeBSD, full entry): FreeBSD's modern per-process introspection utility, introduced by Robert N M Watson and shipped with FreeBSD 9.0 in 2012. Built as a thin client of libprocstat(3), the stable C library every other FreeBSD tool can link against. Flags: -f file descriptors, -t threads with states, -v virtual-memory map, -k kernel stack, -s security credentials, -a all processes (often with grep or pid filter). One tool, one flag per question, same library underneath.
/proc (Linux): A pseudo-filesystem on Linux exposing kernel and process state as a directory tree. Each running process has a directory /proc/pid/ containing fd/ (symbolic links to the actual files, sockets or pipes the file descriptors point to), maps/ (memory mappings), status (a textual summary) and many other entries; /proc/net/ exposes socket tables (tcp, udp, unix) and /proc/sys/ exposes runtime kernel parameters. The source from which lsof, ss and many other Linux observability tools derive their output.
gstat: A FreeBSD command-line tool for displaying live statistics for GEOM providers (disks, partitions, GEOM transforms). Reads from libgeom and libprocstat in the FreeBSD layered model. The disk-I/O counterpart of top for processes; useful for spotting a disk that is saturating or a transform that is taking unexpected load.
file descriptor: A small non-negative integer that a Unix-like kernel returns from open(), socket(), pipe() and similar system calls, used as an opaque handle the process passes to subsequent read(), write(), close() and other calls. Each process has its own file-descriptor table; fd 0, 1 and 2 are by convention stdin, stdout and stderr. The unit fstat, procstat and lsof report on, and the resource that runs out when a daemon leaks them.
inode: A Unix filesystem data structure describing a single file: owner, permissions, timestamps, link count, block pointers. The directory entry is a name plus an inode number; multiple names (hard links) can point at the same inode. When the link count reaches zero and the in-memory reference count reaches zero, the kernel frees the blocks. The deleted-but-held file is the case where the link count is zero but the reference count is still above zero because a process holds an fd.
SIGHUP: Signal 1 on POSIX systems, historically delivered to a process when its controlling terminal hangs up. Long since repurposed by convention as a reload-config signal for daemons: nginx, syslogd, sshd, BIND and many others treat SIGHUP as a request to reread configuration or rotate logs. The conventional cure for a deleted-but-held logfile, where the daemon knows how to close the old fd and reopen the path.
On Second Thought Episode 10: The tenth episode of Vivian Voss's 'On Second Thought' series, published 31 May 2026. Forensic examination of the modern restart reflex: when the pager fires and memory on a service is climbing, the on-call engineer runs kubectl delete pod, memory is fresh, the alert clears and nobody asks what was wrong. The reflex has two parents and the industry kept one: the consumer-electronics tradition (a device with no language to be asked has only its power switch) and the Stockholm tradition built deliberately to replace it (Erlang, 1986, Joe Armstrong, Robert Virding and Mike Williams at Ericsson, for telephone exchanges that could not go down). The AXD301 ATM switch is most often cited at nine nines (the figure is contested, the architecture is not). Armstrong's let-it-crash principle, read as architecture, has three properties: processes are isolated (no shared mutable state to corrupt); every worker has a supervisor (defined in OTP, the standard Erlang library) delivered the crash as a message; the supervisor decides according to a written restart strategy (one-for-one, one-for-all, rest-for-one) with an escalation limit. A failure is data, handled by a rule written years before the outage. What was kept was the crash; what was left in Stockholm was the supervisor.
