The costs of keeping only the crash: the restart erases the question that distinguishes memory leak, file-descriptor exhaustion, lock contention and downstream throttling, which share the same heartbeat-recovery signature on a Kubernetes liveness probe; the orchestrator rotates the symptoms while the cause goes unexamined; teams that restart to fix never get good at diagnosing. The alternative: WhatsApp serves over a billion users on around fifty engineers on Erlang on the Stockholm supervisor architecture; the FreeBSD base provides the operator's half with init, rc.d, dtrace, ktrace, procstat, fstat and post-mortem core dumps, so the reboot is rarely the first reach because the system is willing to speak. The honest question is not whether to keep the restart; the question we did not write down is whether the restart was the answer, or the moment the question got dropped. A restart, on second thought, is not a tool. It is a measurement of how much of the cause you decided you could afford to leave unknown.
Wayne Davison: American programmer, author of the unified-diff format. Posted unidiff to comp.sources.misc volume 14 in August 1990, a new diff format that interleaved deletions and insertions into a single block and shared the surrounding context between them, saving roughly a quarter of the bytes on a typical patch. Richard Stallman folded unidiff support into GNU diff 1.15 in January 1991; the patch tool learned to read it shortly after, and the unified format has been the lingua franca of code review ever since. Davison later became known as the long-time maintainer of rsync.
unidiff (unified diff): A diff output format introduced by Wayne Davison in August 1990 via comp.sources.misc volume 14. Combines deletions and insertions into a single block with shared context, prefixed with minus and plus respectively (space-prefixed lines are unchanged context). Roughly twenty-five per cent smaller than the older context-diff format. Folded into GNU diff 1.15 in January 1991 by Richard Stallman. The format every modern code-review tool (git, GitHub, GitLab, Phabricator) produces and reads.
context diff: The older diff output format that preceded unidiff: each hunk lists several lines of context before and after the change, with the old and new versions in separate blocks (each marked with !) rather than interleaved. Verbose but human-readable; the format Larry Wall's patch 1.3 (1985) was originally designed to apply. Supplanted by unidiff (1990) for daily use, still supported by both BSD patch and GNU patch.
mod.sources: A Usenet newsgroup of the early-to-mid 1980s, used for distributing moderated source code of Unix utilities. Larry Wall posted patch 1.3 to mod.sources on 8 May 1985. The predecessor of comp.sources.unix and comp.sources.misc, both of which became the principal distribution channels for free Unix software before the web.
Jet Propulsion Laboratory (NASA JPL): A federally funded research and development centre managed by Caltech for NASA, located in Pasadena, California. Famous for robotic interplanetary missions (the Voyagers, the Mars rovers, the Galileo and Juno orbiters). The institution at which Larry Wall worked when he wrote rn (1984), patch (1985) and Perl (1987).
Perl: A general-purpose programming language created by Larry Wall and first released in December 1987. Originally designed for text processing and system-administration tasks, with a syntax drawing from C, sed, awk and shell. The successor in Wall's tool lineage to patch (1985) and rn (1984).
rn (news reader): Read News, the Usenet news-reader Larry Wall wrote in 1984 (one year before patch). Replaced the earlier readnews; introduced the concept of a kill file and influenced every subsequent text-mode news-reader (trn, nn, slrn). The first widely deployed Wall tool.
Richard Stallman: American programmer and free-software activist, founder of the GNU project (1983) and the Free Software Foundation (1985). Wrote GNU Emacs, GNU Compiler Collection and large parts of the GNU userland; the GPL (1989) and its later versions are largely his work. Folded Wayne Davison's unidiff support into GNU diff 1.15 in January 1991.
GNU diff: The diff implementation maintained as part of the GNU project, part of the GNU diffutils package, GPL-licensed. Version 1.15 (January 1991) added support for Wayne Davison's unified-diff format; today's git diff and most modern code-review tools produce output in that format. Interoperable with BSD diff at the format level despite different lineages and licences.
hunk: A single contiguous unit of change inside a patch file. Begins with a hunk-range header of the form @@ -oldstart,oldcount +newstart,newcount @@ describing where the change lands in the old and new files, followed by the affected lines: context lines space-prefixed, deletions minus-prefixed, additions plus-prefixed. A patch is a sequence of hunks; if one fails to apply cleanly it can be rejected to a .rej file while the others succeed.
line-fuzz: A tolerance the patch tool applies when a hunk's context does not match the target file exactly: it allows the context lines to be a little wrong, within a configurable number of lines (the fuzz factor, default 2), on the theory that the file has drifted but not changed beyond recognition. Controlled with patch -F; setting it to zero forces strict matching. If even fuzz cannot find a match the hunk is rejected to a .rej file.
.rej file: A file written beside the target by the patch tool when a hunk cannot be applied even with line-fuzz. Contains the failing hunk in the original patch format, so a human can read it, edit the target by hand, and try again. The explicit, plainly-named failure surface that makes patch honest about its limits.
Warner Losh: FreeBSD developer and computer historian. Among many other contributions, the maintainer of the FreeBSD bootloader and the curator of the FreeBSD-classic-OS restorations. In August 2020 traced a thirty-five-year-old corner case in the original 1985 patch parsing while restoring 2.11BSD; the fix was applied identically to both the BSD and GNU patch lines, which had drifted apart only at the surface.
2.11BSD: A 1991 release of Berkeley Software Distribution Unix, the last 2.x BSD; targeted at PDP-11 hardware long after VAX-based 4.x BSD had taken over the mainline. Used by Warner Losh in 2020 as the source tree against which the original 1985 patch corner case was rediscovered.
comp.sources.misc: A Usenet newsgroup of the late 1980s through the 1990s, the moderated distribution channel for miscellaneous Unix source code that did not fit into comp.sources.unix or the language-specific groups. The publication channel for Wayne Davison's unidiff (volume 14, August 1990), among many other widely used tools.
Knight Capital Group: An American financial-services firm and one of the largest market makers on the United States equities markets in the early 2010s. On 1 August 2012 a deployment error in its SMARS routing system caused approximately $440 million in realised pre-tax losses in forty-five minutes; the firm raised around $400 million of emergency capital from Jefferies within days, was fined $12 million by the SEC for violations of the Market Access Rule, and was acquired by Getco Holdings before the year was out, becoming KCG Holdings. The Knight name effectively ended that morning.
SMARS: Smart Market Access Routing System. Knight Capital's order-routing system, which decided how to forward orders into the various American equity venues. Ran on eight production servers in August 2012; the failed deployment to one of those eight on the night of 31 July 2012 is the proximate cause of the Knight Capital incident of 1 August 2012.
Power Peg: A SMARS module written by Knight Capital in 2003 to manage parent-order execution: an algorithmic test routine for slicing large orders into many smaller ones. Used in production briefly, judged unfit for purpose, and disabled after 2005 by a single flag in the order-routing protocol; the code was never deleted from the SMARS source tree. In 2012 the same flag bit was repurposed to signal Retail Liquidity Program eligibility; on the eighth Knight server, which still ran 2003 code, the bit still meant start Power Peg. The module had no production-grade concept of order completion and bought at the ask and sold at the bid until told to stop; nobody had told it to stop in nine years.
Retail Liquidity Program (RLP): A New York Stock Exchange programme launched on 1 August 2012 for matching retail orders against retail-focused liquidity providers at sub-penny price improvements. Introduced a new flag in the order-routing protocol to mark RLP-eligible orders; the same flag bit position had once carried the Power Peg activation signal in Knight Capital's SMARS routing system.
NYSE: New York Stock Exchange. The largest stock exchange in the world by listed-company market capitalisation, founded 1792, owned by Intercontinental Exchange since 2013. The venue at which the Retail Liquidity Program launched on 1 August 2012, the same morning Knight Capital's eighth SMARS server began running 2003 code against the new RLP flag.
SEC: United States Securities and Exchange Commission. An independent federal agency created by the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 to regulate the United States securities markets. The body that investigated and fined Knight Capital $12 million for violations of the Market Access Rule following the 1 August 2012 incident (SEC Press Release 2013-222, October 2013).
Market Access Rule (SEC Rule 15c3-5): An SEC rule adopted in 2010 and effective from 14 July 2011, requiring broker-dealers with market access (and broker-dealers providing market access to customers) to maintain risk-management controls and supervisory procedures designed to manage the financial, regulatory and other risks of that market access. The rule under which the SEC fined Knight Capital $12 million for the 1 August 2012 incident, citing inadequate controls and procedures around the SMARS deployment.
Getco Holdings: An American high-frequency trading firm founded in 1999 in Chicago. Acquired Knight Capital Group in late 2012 following the 1 August 2012 incident; the combined firm became KCG Holdings, later acquired by Virtu Financial in 2017.
Jefferies: An American investment bank and financial-services firm founded in 1962, headquartered in New York. Led a syndicate that provided approximately $400 million of emergency capital to Knight Capital within days of the 1 August 2012 incident, in exchange for convertible preferred stock that effectively ceded control of the firm.
LaunchDarkly: A feature-flag and experimentation platform founded in 2014, used to gate code paths in production behind named flags with per-user, per-segment and per-environment evaluation. Cited in Tales from the Bare Metal Episode 06 as one of the modern surfaces on which the Knight Capital pattern (a flag whose semantic meaning shifts between releases) can recur.
ConfigCat: A feature-flag service founded in 2017, similar in shape to LaunchDarkly and Unleash. One of the modern feature-flag libraries listed in Tales from the Bare Metal Episode 06 as places the Knight pattern can recur if flag semantics quietly shift.
Unleash: An open-source feature-flag platform founded in 2014 by Finn Bjornerud at FINN.no in Oslo, available as a hosted service and as self-hosted software (Apache 2.0). One of the modern feature-flag libraries cited in Tales from the Bare Metal Episode 06.
Flagsmith: An open-source feature-flag platform founded in 2018, available as a hosted service or self-hosted (BSL/MIT). Cited alongside LaunchDarkly, ConfigCat and Unleash in Tales from the Bare Metal Episode 06.
AMI: Amazon Machine Image. A pre-configured virtual-machine image used by Amazon EC2 to launch new instances; identified by an AMI ID. Used by EC2 launch templates and Auto Scaling groups to specify which image new instances should boot from. Cited in Tales from the Bare Metal Episode 06: a launch template pinning an outdated AMI ID lets newly scaled-out instances run the old binary while freshly-deployed ones run the new.
EC2: Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud, AWS's virtual-machine service launched in 2006. The foundation of much of AWS; instances are launched from AMIs, often managed through launch templates and Auto Scaling groups.
Kustomize: A Kubernetes-native configuration management tool, originally a standalone project from Google and merged into kubectl in version 1.14 (2019). Generates Kubernetes manifests by overlaying patches on a base; an alternative to Helm's templated approach. Cited alongside Helm in Tales from the Bare Metal Episode 06 as a deployment surface where a stale registry cache can let an older image run while the manifest pins a newer tag.
imagePullPolicy: A Kubernetes pod-spec field that controls how the kubelet fetches a container image: Always (pull every time), IfNotPresent (use the locally cached image if available, otherwise pull) or Never (only use the local cache). The default is IfNotPresent for tags other than latest. Cited in Tales from the Bare Metal Episode 06 as the mechanism by which a rolling update can leave one node still running an old image cached under the same tag as the new build.
kubelet: The Kubernetes agent that runs on every node in a cluster, responsible for starting, stopping and monitoring containers per the PodSpecs received from the control plane. Reads imagePullPolicy and decides whether to pull a container image or use the local cache; runs liveness and readiness probes; reports node status back to the API server.
Plan 9 from Bell Labs: A research operating system developed at Bell Labs by Rob Pike, Ken Thompson, Dave Presotto, Phil Winterbottom and others, with first edition released in 1992. Pushes the Unix everything-is-a-file idea to its logical end: the window system, the keyboard, mouse and remote machines are all file systems; the 9P protocol mounts remote resources as local file hierarchies; even computation is a file system. Dennis Ritchie said of Plan 9 that 'Unix had the right idea but didn't follow through' and that Plan 9 did. Still actively maintained at 9front.org as 9front.
9P protocol: A network filesystem protocol designed for Plan 9 from Bell Labs, used to expose any resource (a file, a window, a remote machine, a synthetic service) as a mountable file hierarchy. The protocol underneath Plan 9's everything-is-a-file-system architecture; later adopted in the Linux kernel as v9fs and in QEMU/KVM for paravirtualised filesystem sharing.
9front: An actively maintained fork of Plan 9 from Bell Labs, released under the MIT licence and developed since 2011 by a community of Plan 9 users. The reference implementation by which Plan 9's everything-is-a-file design continues to be tested against modern hardware. The pure version of the idea Unix gave us, still maintained, still running.
Rudd Canaday: American computer scientist at Bell Labs in the late 1960s. Together with Ken Thompson and Dennis Ritchie, one of the early Unix designers; contributed to the design of the hierarchical filesystem during the system's first months on the PDP-7. Less famous than Thompson and Ritchie, but in the room when the foundations were laid.
Dave Presotto: American computer scientist, Bell Labs. Co-designer of Plan 9 from Bell Labs (with Pike, Thompson and Winterbottom, 1992) and a contributor to the 9P protocol and the Plan 9 networking stack. Later worked at Google.
Phil Winterbottom: Computer scientist, Bell Labs. Co-designer of Plan 9 from Bell Labs (with Pike, Thompson and Presotto, 1992); designed the Alef and Limbo programming languages used in the Plan 9 and Inferno operating systems.
4.2BSD: A 1983 release of the Berkeley Software Distribution Unix produced at the University of California, Berkeley. Introduced the BSD sockets API (Berkeley sockets), bringing TCP/IP networking to Unix in a form that used the same file-descriptor abstraction as files and pipes; introduced the Fast File System (FFS); and added long file names and symbolic links. The release that brought networking under the everything-is-a-file roof.
devfs (FreeBSD): FreeBSD's in-kernel devices filesystem, mounted on /dev/. Presents the kernel's device tree as a regular hierarchical filesystem; device nodes are created and removed dynamically by the kernel as drivers attach and detach, rather than being kept on disk. In the base system since FreeBSD 5.0 (2003). The faithful FreeBSD expression of the everything-is-a-file decision.
ioctl: Input/Output Control. A POSIX system call (ioctl(2)) for operations on a file descriptor that do not fit the read/write byte-stream model: setting terminal modes, configuring network interfaces, controlling block devices, talking to GPU drivers. The honest escape hatch in the Unix everything-is-a-file design: the file model is kept as the default, ioctl carries everything that refuses to be bytes.
Plan 9 window system (rio): Plan 9's window system, in which each window is a file. Drawing primitives, mouse events and keyboard input are read and written through files in the window's per-instance namespace; a program writes to a file to draw, reads from a file to see input. The reference example of the everything-is-a-file decision taken to its logical end.
D-Bus: A message-bus system originating at freedesktop.org in 2002, used on most Linux desktops and in many Linux service stacks for inter-process communication. Provides a typed RPC protocol over Unix-domain sockets, with system and session buses, well-known names and signal broadcasting. Cited in By Design Episode 07 as one of the corners where Linux has drifted from the everything-is-a-file decision: D-Bus carries the message bus that earlier Unix work would have built as files and sockets.
Netlink: A Linux kernel-to-userspace IPC mechanism designed by Alexey Kuznetsov for structured queries against kernel subsystems, implemented as a socket family (AF_NETLINK). Used for routing (NETLINK_ROUTE), socket diagnostics (NETLINK_SOCK_DIAG), audit (NETLINK_AUDIT), firewall configuration (nftables) and many others. The alternative kernel channel that traditional Unix would have done through paths and ioctls.
sysfs: A pseudo-filesystem on Linux, added in kernel 2.6 (2003), mounted at /sys. Exposes kernel objects (devices, drivers, buses, kernel modules) as a hierarchical filesystem with one file per attribute. In the spirit of the everything-is-a-file decision, extending /proc's process-state surface with a structured kernel-object surface.
DRM (Direct Rendering Manager): A kernel subsystem on Linux and FreeBSD that manages access to GPUs. Presents the GPU as a character device in /dev/dri/, opened like any other file, with almost all the actual work happening through ioctl calls for command queues, buffer allocation, mode setting and synchronisation. The practical compromise: the GPU is a file in /dev/, the operations are too structured to be byte streams, ioctl carries them.
stty: A POSIX command and library function for setting terminal-line settings: baud rate, character size, control characters, echo, canonical or raw mode, and many other line-discipline parameters. Implemented as a thin wrapper around tcgetattr/tcsetattr (the modern POSIX surface) or ioctl(TIOCGETA/TIOCSETA) (the historical surface). The canonical example of file-descriptor state that does not fit a read() and needs ioctl.
/dev/null: A special device file on every Unix-like system since the early 1970s. Writes to /dev/null are silently discarded; reads return end-of-file. The canonical example of a non-storage resource exposed as a regular file; used in shell scripts to suppress output (e.g. cmd > /dev/null 2>&1) and as a placeholder file when a path is required but no data is.
/dev/random: A special device file on Unix-like systems that returns cryptographically random bytes drawn from the kernel's entropy pool. On Linux historically blocking when entropy is low (replaced largely by /dev/urandom for most uses); on FreeBSD non-blocking and equivalent to /dev/urandom since the entropy pool is seeded once at boot. The everything-is-a-file expression of a hardware/PRNG service.
/dev/zero: A special device file on Unix-like systems that returns an inexhaustible stream of zero bytes. Used to create files of a given size (dd if=/dev/zero of=blob bs=1M count=100), to wipe a block device, and as a source for memory-mapped regions.
CACM (Communications of the ACM): The flagship monthly publication of the Association for Computing Machinery, founded 1958. The venue in which Ritchie and Thompson's The UNIX Time-Sharing System appeared in July 1974 (volume 17 number 7), codifying the everything-is-a-file design that had been running on the PDP-7 since 1969.
Vault (HashiCorp): HashiCorp's secrets-management tool, first released in 2015. Provides storage, dynamic generation and lifecycle management of secrets (database credentials, API keys, TLS certificates) with pluggable authentication backends, transit encryption and audit logging. Moved from MPL 2.0 to BSL 1.1 on 10 August 2023; the OpenBao fork at the Linux Foundation is the MPL-licensed successor as of December 2024.
Consul (HashiCorp): HashiCorp's service-discovery and service-mesh product, first released in 2014. Provides a distributed KV store, service catalogue, health checks, and (in later versions) a service mesh with mTLS. Moved from MPL 2.0 to BSL 1.1 on 10 August 2023. The service-mesh capability has largely shifted to Istio and Linkerd in the post-BSL migration path; the KV-store responsibility maps onto etcd or onto Consul OSS pinned to a pre-BSL version.
Nomad (HashiCorp): HashiCorp's workload orchestrator, first released in 2015. A simpler alternative to Kubernetes, scheduling containers, virtual machines and binaries across a cluster with a single binary per node. Moved from MPL 2.0 to BSL 1.1 on 10 August 2023.
Packer (HashiCorp): HashiCorp's tool for creating identical machine images (AMIs, Docker images, qcow2) for multiple platforms from a single source configuration, first released in 2013. Moved from MPL 2.0 to BSL 1.1 on 10 August 2023.
Boundary (HashiCorp): HashiCorp's identity-aware proxy and remote-access product, first released in 2020. Provides session-recorded access to hosts and services with role-based access control. Moved from MPL 2.0 to BSL 1.1 on 10 August 2023.
Waypoint (HashiCorp): HashiCorp's developer application-deployment tool, released in 2020. Wraps build-deploy-release for multiple runtimes (Kubernetes, Nomad, AWS Lambda, ECS). Moved from MPL 2.0 to BSL 1.1 on 10 August 2023.
Vagrant: A virtual-machine development environment manager by HashiCorp, first released in 2010 (HashiCorp's first product). Provisions reproducible developer VMs on VirtualBox, VMware, Hyper-V or libvirt. Moved from MPL 2.0 to BSL 1.1 on 10 August 2023.
OpenBao: An open-source fork of HashiCorp Vault, created after HashiCorp's August 2023 BSL relicence. Held under MPL 2.0 by the Linux Foundation since the November 2023 announcement; forked from Vault 1.14.x; general-availability release in December 2024. Drop-in for most Vault workloads; some enterprise features (HSM integration, MFA, OSS-sense namespaces) require additional development or paid alternatives.
HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language): HashiCorp's declarative configuration language, first released alongside Terraform 0.1 in July 2014. A JSON-compatible syntax with block-and-attribute structure, designed to be readable by humans and parseable by machines. Used by Terraform, Vault, Consul, Nomad and Packer for resource specifications; also adopted by tools outside the HashiCorp stack (Terragrunt, OpenTofu, Atlantis).
Sentinel (HashiCorp): HashiCorp's policy-as-code framework, used in Terraform Enterprise, Vault Enterprise and Consul Enterprise to enforce policies on resource changes, secret access and service registration. Sentinel policies are written in a Python-like DSL and evaluated against runtime data; an enterprise-tier feature, not available in the open-source/BSL editions.
Spacelift: A SaaS infrastructure-as-code management platform founded in 2020, providing CI/CD, policy and collaboration for Terraform, OpenTofu, Pulumi, Kubernetes and Ansible. One of the five founding sponsors of OpenTofu at the Linux Foundation in September 2023 after the HashiCorp BSL relicence.
Harness: A continuous-delivery and CI/CD platform founded in 2017 by Jyoti Bansal. One of the five founding sponsors of OpenTofu at the Linux Foundation in September 2023 after the HashiCorp BSL relicence.
Gruntwork: An IaC-tooling and consulting firm founded in 2016 by Yevgeniy Brikman and Josh Padnick, authors of Terragrunt and a Terraform-modules library. One of the five founding sponsors of OpenTofu at the Linux Foundation in September 2023.
env0: A SaaS infrastructure-as-code management platform founded in 2018, similar in shape to Spacelift and Scalr. One of the five founding sponsors of OpenTofu at the Linux Foundation in September 2023.
Scalr: An infrastructure-as-code orchestration platform founded in 2007 (originally as a cloud-management product), pivoted to Terraform/OpenTofu collaboration tooling. One of the five founding sponsors of OpenTofu at the Linux Foundation in September 2023.
Linux Foundation: A non-profit consortium founded in 2000 (merged from the Open Source Development Lab and the Free Standards Group) that hosts and supports open-source projects under a neutral governance model. Funded by member companies; hosts the Linux kernel, Kubernetes (via the CNCF), node.js, OpenTofu, OpenBao and many others. The neutral home to which OpenTofu was accepted on 20 September 2023, forty-one days after HashiCorp's BSL announcement.
IBM: International Business Machines Corporation, an American multinational technology company founded 1911. Acquired Red Hat in 2019 for $34 billion, HashiCorp in February 2025 for $6.4 billion. The licensor of record for Terraform, Vault, Consul, Nomad, Packer, Boundary, Waypoint and Vagrant since 27 February 2025.
Open Source Initiative (OSI): A non-profit organisation founded in 1998 by Bruce Perens and Eric Raymond, formalising the term 'Open Source' and curating the Open Source Definition (the OSD). Approves licences as Open Source if they meet the OSD's ten criteria; rejects source-available licences such as the BSL because they fail criterion 6 (no discrimination against fields of endeavour) and criterion 2 (modifications must be redistributable for any purpose).
Additional Use Grant (BSL): A paragraph in the Business Source License in which the licensor names the things the licensee may not do; the design point of BSL 1.1 over a pure proprietary licence. HashiCorp's Additional Use Grant for the post-August-2023 stack forbids 'production use that competes with HashiCorp's commercial offering'. Ambiguous on the face of the text and ambiguous by design; the grey zone (consultancies, CI providers, wrappers) is the disputed surface.
Terragrunt: A thin wrapper around Terraform (and now OpenTofu) for managing multi-environment, multi-module Terraform configurations with less duplication. Created by Yevgeniy Brikman at Gruntwork, open-source under MIT. Part of the Terraform ecosystem that grew up on the MPL-2.0 assumption.
Atlantis: An open-source Terraform pull-request automation server, written in Go and licensed under Apache 2.0. Runs Terraform plans against pull requests and posts the output as comments. Created in 2017 by Anubhav Mishra at HootSuite, donated to Runatlantis. Part of the Terraform ecosystem that grew up on the MPL-2.0 assumption.
U.K. Competition and Markets Authority (: The United Kingdom's non-ministerial government department responsible for strengthening business competition and preventing and reducing anti-competitive activities. Established in 2014, replacing the Competition Commission and the Office of Fair Trading. Reviewed the IBM acquisition of HashiCorp and granted clearance in late February 2025, allowing the deal to close on 27 February 2025.
U.S. Federal Trade Commission (FTC): An independent agency of the United States federal government, established in 1914, whose principal mission is the enforcement of antitrust law and the promotion of consumer protection. Reviewed the IBM acquisition of HashiCorp as part of the regulatory clearance process that completed in February 2025.
In the Net Episode 06: The sixth episode of Vivian Voss's 'In the Net' series, published 5 June 2026. Names the sixth distinct lock-in genus the series has documented: lock-in by retroactive adoption, in which the licence on the source of a tool you had already adopted changes under your feet by press release. Forensic reading of HashiCorp's 10 August 2023 move from Mozilla Public License v2.0 to Business Source License 1.1 across Terraform, Vault, Consul, Nomad, Packer, Boundary, Waypoint and Vagrant; the community fork (OpenTofu, accepted by the Linux Foundation on 20 September 2023 with founding sponsors Spacelift, Harness, Gruntwork, env0 and Scalr; OpenTofu 1.6 shipped January 2024); the IBM acquisition (announced 24 April 2024 at $6.4 billion, closed 27 February 2025 after CMA and FTC clearance). The BSL is not OSI Open Source; the Additional Use Grant forbids production use that competes with HashiCorp's commercial offering, ambiguous by design; each release reverts to MPL 2.0 four years after publication; Terraform 1.5.7 is the last MPL version and the only legal fork-target; HashiCorp's APIs, SDKs and provider plugins remain MPL 2.0. The price: Terraform Cloud per applied resource per concurrent run per workspace, Terraform Enterprise at five-figure annual commitments, Vault Enterprise per client per month, large estates at six and seven figures yearly; the licence is the leverage, the pricing is the price of staying. The escape routes are concrete: OpenTofu (drop-in for Terraform, state files forward-compatible), OpenBao (drop-in for Vault, GA December 2024), Pulumi (Apache 2.0, IaC in Python/Go/TypeScript/C-sharp, real port), Crossplane (Apache 2.0, Kubernetes-native composition). The six lock-in genera so far: Adobe took the file format, LinkedIn took the reach, AWS took identity and egress, VMware took the perpetual licence in an acquisition, Oracle took Java users and billed the entire workforce, HashiCorp now IBM took the licence on the source. You wrote infrastructure as code so the next engineer could read it. You did not promise the next licence-holder would let them.
By Design Episode 07: The seventh episode of Vivian Voss's 'By Design' series, published 4 June 2026. A reading of the file as the universal Unix interface, the architectural decision Ken Thompson and Dennis Ritchie made on the PDP-7 in the summer of 1969 (with Rudd Canaday on the early team) and that has carried half a century without much fading. The hero is Thompson and Ritchie (Bell Labs, 1969-1973); the pure follow-through is Plan 9 from Bell Labs (Pike, Thompson, Presotto, Winterbottom, 1992). The complaint sockets are not files and GPUs are not files is older than the design and not unfounded; ioctl is the honest escape hatch; the trade is that the file model is cheap because the alternatives are expensive. On FreeBSD the discipline is intact: devfs (in base since 5.0, 2003), GELI as /dev/<name>.eli, ZFS volumes under /dev/zvol/, bhyve under /dev/vmm/. Linux has, in some corners, drifted: D-Bus (2002) for the message bus, systemd (2010) building its own interfaces, Netlink as the alternative kernel channel, eBPF as a programmable layer rather than a path. None are wrong; the cumulative effect is that the Linux of 2026 has, in important corners, become a system in which the file is one of several interfaces rather than the interface. Ritchie's verdict on Plan 9 was that Unix did not follow through; the same sentence, read against 2026 Linux, has rotated by 180 degrees. One interface, infinite implementations. A Unix one-liner reads almost like a sentence because every noun in it is a file.
Tales from the Bare Metal Episode 06: The sixth episode of Vivian Voss's 'Tales from the Bare Metal' series, published 3 June 2026. Forensic reading of the Knight Capital incident of 1 August 2012: at 09:30 the NYSE opened the Retail Liquidity Program, by 10:15 Knight had ceased to be a going concern; forty-five minutes cost the firm roughly $440 million, drew a $12 million SEC fine for violations of the Market Access Rule, and led to acquisition by Getco within months. The proximate cause was a deploy to Knight's SMARS routing system that reached seven of eight production servers; the eighth kept a 2003 module called Power Peg, disabled after 2005 by a flag and never deleted. In 2012 the same flag bit was repurposed to signal RLP eligibility; on the seven up-to-date servers the bit meant RLP, on the eighth in 2003 code it still meant start Power Peg, which had no concept of order filled and bought at the ask and sold at the bid until told to stop. Three quiet drifts compounded, none foolish at the time: gating dead code rather than deleting it, treating file-copy as deploy success, documenting a flag's new use without auditing every prior one. Two architectural disciplines would have prevented it: delete dead code do not gate it, and verify after deploy on the property of the deployed code (every host reports the new build hash via a /version probe). The pattern travels through Kubernetes rolling updates with cached images, feature-flag libraries with quietly-shifted semantics, EC2 launch templates with stale AMIs, Helm and Kustomize manifests served from older registry caches, and CI/CD job matrices that report success on exit codes without verifying any running version. A deploy that succeeds on seven of eight is one that failed quietly on one. Production does not give partial credit; the market gives none at all.
Technical Beauty Episode 38: The thirty-eighth episode of Vivian Voss's 'Technical Beauty' series, published 2 June 2026. A reading of the patch utility and the unified-diff format as a piece of conceptual reduction: a patch is a recipe, not a snapshot, so a fix to a million-line codebase can travel as twenty lines of email; the vocabulary is a header, hunks (@@ -42,7 +42,9 @@), context space-prefixed, deletions minus-prefixed, additions plus-prefixed, and the whole standard fits on a page. The daily idiom is diff -u to make and patch -p1 to apply; line-fuzz tolerates small drift, the .rej file records honest failure. FreeBSD ships patch in base at /usr/bin/patch, BSD-licensed, descended directly from Larry Wall's original 1985 source line; OpenBSD, NetBSD and macOS carry the same lineage. GNU patch is a separate fork from the same root, GPL-licensed; the on-disk format both tools read and write is the same. Larry Wall posted patch 1.3 to mod.sources on 8 May 1985 from NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory, two years before Perl; Wayne Davison posted unidiff to comp.sources.misc in August 1990, saving roughly a quarter of the bytes; Richard Stallman folded unidiff into GNU diff 1.15 in January 1991. Warner Losh's 2020 2.11BSD restoration found a thirty-five-year-old corner case in the original parsing that was triaged identically against both lines. Forty-one years on, every code review on the planet still speaks unified diff. A format becomes universal when it is small enough to be a sentence. Wall's was.
The Unix Way Episode 19: The nineteenth episode of Vivian Voss's 'The Unix Way' series, published 1 June 2026. A side-by-side reading of how FreeBSD and Linux answer the question every long-running-systems engineer eventually has to ask: which process is holding this open? The class of error message that precipitates the question is universal (Cannot remove text file busy, Address already in use, Mount point is busy, and the silent contradiction where df reports a filesystem at ninety per cent while du can only account for half the bytes). FreeBSD splits the work into procstat(1) (the modern per-process introspection front-end, shipped with FreeBSD 9.0 in 2012 by Robert N M Watson), libprocstat(3) (the stable C library every tool can link against, so top, gstat, debuggers and monitoring agents all read the same kernel reality), and fstat(1) (the classical system-wide ledger in the base system since 4.3BSD-Tahoe in 1988); the deleted-but-held file is identified by fstat -p pid piped to awk dollar-five-equals-dash. Linux folds the same job into lsof(8), written by Vic Abell at Purdue University and first released to comp.sources.unix in 1991, two years after he had ported BSD's fstat and ofiles to DYNIX, SunOS and ULTRIX; the universal hammer reads from /proc, kernel symbol tables and netlink, and the deleted-but-held diagnostic is lsof +L1. Two shapes of the same answer: parts that compose (libprocstat as the shared library, several thin clients above it) versus parts that arrive together (one lovingly maintained binary, one consistent output). Both ship with the OS. Neither asks you to write JSON. Learn the flags before the page goes off.
Copy Fail (CVE-2026-31431): A Linux kernel local-privilege-escalation vulnerability disclosed by Theori on 29 April 2026. CVSS v3.1 score 7.8 (High). The exploit, written in standard Python, fits in roughly 732 bytes and runs on Ubuntu 24.04, Amazon Linux 2023, Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10.1, SUSE 16, Debian, Fedora, Arch and any distribution shipping a kernel from the last nine years. The component is the authencesn template inside the Linux kernel crypto subsystem, exposed to userspace through the algif_aead module of the AF_ALG socket interface. The bug is composite: a four-byte scratch write in authencesn (present since 2011 for IPsec ESN), in combination with AF_ALG's AEAD splice support (2015) and the in-place AEAD optimisation (2017, changeset 72548b093ee3), gives a deterministic four-byte write primitive into the page-cache page of any file the user can read. The Linux kernel security team triaged and patched the flaw in eight days; mainline patch landed on 1 April 2026.
AF_ALG: Address family AF_ALG, the Linux kernel socket family added in 2010 that gives userspace programs access to the kernel's cryptography subsystem. A program opens a socket of family AF_ALG, binds it to a named algorithm and key, then performs the operation through ordinary read/write or splice on the socket. Genuinely useful (no need to embed a userspace crypto library) and the surface through which CVE-2026-31431 was reached. The subject of a 2025 LKML patch series titled Document the deprecation of AF_ALG.
algif_aead: The Linux kernel module that implements AEAD (authenticated encryption with associated data) operations on top of the AF_ALG socket interface. Since 2015 supports splice as the input path; since 2017 supports in-place AEAD where input and output share one scatterlist. The combination is the surface through which the authencesn four-byte scratch write becomes a deterministic page-cache write primitive in CVE-2026-31431.
authencesn: The Linux kernel crypto-subsystem template for AEAD with Extended Sequence Number support, added in 2011 to back IPsec's ESN feature. Contains a small piece of housekeeping that writes four bytes of scratch space into the destination buffer; correct in the original out-of-place AEAD context, but turned into a memory-corruption primitive once the destination buffer was the page-cache mapping of a spliced file (CVE-2026-31431).
AEAD (cryptography): Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data. A class of symmetric ciphers (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305, AES-CCM) that combines confidentiality of the message body with integrity protection of both the body and a separate piece of associated data (AAD) such as a packet header. The standard primitive in modern protocols (TLS 1.3, WireGuard, IPsec ESP, Signal). The Linux kernel's authencesn template implements an AEAD construction for IPsec.
page cache (kernel): The kernel's in-memory cache of file-system content. When a process reads or executes a file, the kernel pages the relevant blocks into main memory and serves all subsequent accesses from there until memory pressure or an explicit invalidate evicts them. The page cache is shared across all processes on a host, including across container boundaries on Linux. The cinema where /usr/bin/su actually runs; the disk is the receipt.
kmap_local_page: A Linux kernel API for obtaining a short-lived virtual mapping of a physical page, in a CPU-local fashion, without disabling pre-emption for the duration. The successor to kmap_atomic in modern Linux. The mapping into which the authencesn four-byte scratch write lands during the CVE-2026-31431 exploit.
scatterlist (kernel): A Linux kernel data structure describing a sequence of physical-memory regions for DMA or in-kernel data transfer. Used by the crypto subsystem to express the input and output buffers of an operation as scatter-gather lists. In the 2017 in-place AEAD optimisation, the input and output of an AEAD operation share one scatterlist; this sharing is the structural condition that turns the authencesn scratch write into a primitive against the input pages.
splice (syscall): A Linux system call that moves data between two file descriptors without copying it through user space, by passing references to kernel page-cache pages. The mechanism by which AF_ALG can take a file as its input buffer without copying its contents; in CVE-2026-31431 the splice references pin page-cache pages of an attacker-chosen file as the input region of an AEAD operation.
Theori: A security research firm and consultancy specialising in offensive research, vulnerability discovery and exploit development. Published the full technical write-up of CVE-2026-31431 (Copy Fail) on 29 April 2026, including the 732-byte Python proof of concept and the composite-bug analysis.
Tripwire (file-integrity monitor): A file-integrity monitoring tool originally written by Gene Kim and Eugene Spafford at Purdue in 1992, later commercialised by Tripwire Inc. (acquired by Belden in 2015). Maintains a baseline of cryptographic hashes for files on disk and reports any deviation. Watches the receipt, not the cinema; blind to page-cache corruption such as CVE-2026-31431.
AIDE (file-integrity monitor): Advanced Intrusion Detection Environment. A free-software file-integrity tool, originally written by Rami Lehti and Pablo Virolainen in 1999, released under GPLv2. Functionally similar to Tripwire: maintains a database of file hashes and reports deviations. Like Tripwire, it watches the on-disk content and cannot detect modifications to the in-memory page-cache copy.
EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response): A class of security product that combines real-time monitoring of endpoint activity (process creation, file modification, network connections) with telemetry collection and response automation. Major vendors: CrowdStrike Falcon, SentinelOne Singularity, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, Carbon Black. Host-integrity layers in modern EDR compare disk hashes; like Tripwire and AIDE, they are blind to page-cache corruption.
OpenCrypto framework (FreeBSD): FreeBSD's kernel cryptography framework, descended from OpenBSD's OCF (OpenBSD Cryptographic Framework, 2002). Exposes kernel cryptography to userspace through /dev/crypto, a device node, rather than through a socket family. Different idiom from Linux's AF_ALG; the in-place AEAD optimisation that opens CVE-2026-31431 does not exist there in the same form.
CNI (Container Network Interface): A specification and set of plugins for configuring network interfaces in Linux containers, originally part of CoreOS rkt (2015), now a standard at the Cloud Native Computing Foundation. Used by Kubernetes (kubelet calls the configured CNI plugin to set up pod networking), Podman, containerd and others. External to the kernel; the contrast in Nine Years in the Page Cache is with FreeBSD VNET, which is a kernel subsystem rather than a plugin layer.
FreeBSD jails (full): FreeBSD's kernel-native containerisation primitive, introduced in FreeBSD 4.0 (2000). A jail is a process subtree, a filesystem subtree and (with VNET) an independent network stack, all enforced directly by the kernel; there is no privileged daemon to compromise, unlike Docker's dockerd. Hardened over twenty-five years of base-system use. The architectural counterpart to the Linux namespace-plus-seccomp-plus-capability-drop arrangement.
FreeBSD-SA (security advisory series): The numbered public security-advisory series of the FreeBSD project, of the form FreeBSD-SA-yy:nn.subject (for example, FreeBSD-SA-25:11.bhyve). Maintained by the FreeBSD Security Officer team; signed; archived on the FreeBSD security page. The honest public record of FreeBSD's own vulnerability history.
Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC: Microsoft's internal product-security and incident-response team, responsible for triaging vulnerability reports against Microsoft products, coordinating disclosure with researchers, and publishing security advisories. Authored a writeup of CVE-2026-31431 (Copy Fail) on 1 May 2026 that was unusually direct about the cloud impact of the cross-container page-cache primitive.
CERT-EU: Computer Emergency Response Team for the EU institutions, bodies and agencies. Established 2012 as a permanent service. Publishes the numbered CERT-EU Security Advisory series; Advisory 2026-005 (1 May 2026) recommended the disabling of algif_aead until the patched kernel had reached every node in the wake of CVE-2026-31431.
Tenable Research: The security-research team at Tenable, the vulnerability-management vendor. Publishes Frequently Asked Questions-style explainers for high-profile CVEs as they break; the 1 May 2026 explainer for CVE-2026-31431 confirmed the disclosure timeline.
Nine Years in the Page Cache (essay, 202: A Vivian Voss Security Review piece for Friday 12 June 2026 on CVE-2026-31431 (Copy Fail), the Linux kernel local-privilege-escalation flaw disclosed by Theori on 29 April 2026. Reads the bug as a composite of three correct decisions taken by three different teams fifteen years apart (AF_ALG 2010, authencesn 2011, AEAD splice 2015, in-place optimisation 2017) meeting in the page cache. The four-byte scratch write of authencesn lands inside the kmap_local_page mapping of a spliced file's page-cache page, giving a deterministic four-byte primitive against any file the user can read. The classical target is /usr/bin/su: disk hash unchanged, page-cache copy corrupted, file-integrity tools (Tripwire, AIDE, EDR host integrity) blind. The page cache is shared between containers and the host, so the corridor crosses container boundaries. The Linux kernel security team triaged and patched the flaw in eight days. The BSD angle: FreeBSD exposes kernel crypto through /dev/crypto rather than AF_ALG (no equivalent splice path), Capsicum (FreeBSD 9, 2012) narrows the corridor structurally, jails are a kernel-native primitive rather than a daemon-mediated namespace arrangement. The recommendation is not patch and move on; it is check the seams.
Next.js: A React-based full-stack web framework, originally released by Vercel (then ZEIT) in October 2016. Bundles server-side rendering, static generation, file-based routing, edge functions, image optimisation and a build pipeline. A typical fresh install in 2026 lands at 700 MB to a full gigabyte on disk, the majority devDependencies; the de facto starting template for React projects after create-react-app was retired.
Vercel: American technology company founded in 2015 (originally as ZEIT), headquartered in San Francisco. Author and primary maintainer of Next.js, of the Vercel hosting platform, and of several adjacent tools (SWR, AI SDK). Drives the commercial momentum behind the modern React-as-default stack.
GOV.UK: The single website for the UK government, built by the Government Digital Service and launched in 2012. Mandated by the GOV.UK Service Manual and the Service Standard to progressive enhancement: every page must work without JavaScript and HTML-first markup is the default. A working counterexample to the assumption that public-sector sites need a heavy client-side framework.
Hotwire (Turbo / Stimulus / Strada): A set of frameworks released by 37signals in December 2020 that delivers fully interactive web applications by sending HTML fragments over the wire rather than JSON to a client-side framework. Three parts: Turbo (page-level navigation and streaming updates), Stimulus (a small JavaScript controller framework for sprinkles of interactivity), Strada (native-bridge layer for mobile). Underpins HEY, Basecamp 4 and the Ruby on Rails 7+ default stack.
transitive closure (npm): The full set of packages installed in node_modules when npm resolves a project's declared dependencies, including the dependencies of dependencies recursively. A modern web project that declares 30 to 50 packages in package.json typically pulls 1,200 to 1,800 packages in the transitive closure, each free to publish a new version overnight and each, in the npm trust model, free to run arbitrary code on the next install.
post-install script: A script declared in a package.json under scripts.postinstall that npm executes automatically after installing a package. Runs with the user's permissions, can read any file the user can read, and can modify any file the user can write. The canonical supply-chain-attack vector in the npm ecosystem; the mechanism by which a single compromised package can pivot into a full developer-machine takeover. Disabled by npm install --ignore-scripts.
axios (March 2026 compromise): The 31 March 2026 supply-chain compromise of the widely-used axios HTTP client library. Versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 were backdoored for approximately three hours via a malicious postinstall dependency (plain-crypto-js@4.2.1), and the malicious versions were tagged latest during the incident window. The retroactive nature of the latest tag in npm meant that fresh installs during the window received the compromised code without any package.json change on the receiving side.
libc: The standard C library, the layer between user programs and the kernel that provides the C-language interface to POSIX system calls (open, read, write, fork, exec), string handling, formatted I/O, malloc and many other foundational facilities. Lives in the base source tree of an operating system, maintained by named engineers in a foundation with a public roadmap, released on a regular cadence, with reproducible builds and signed artifacts. The canonical counter-example to node_modules' missing responsibility line.
service mesh: An infrastructure layer that handles service-to-service communication between microservices, typically by attaching a sidecar proxy to each service that takes over routing, load balancing, mutual TLS, retries and observability. Examples: Istio, Linkerd, Consul Connect. Overhead is per-pod and not free; the trade-off is intelligibility of a many-service architecture against a much heavier per-pod cost. Cited in The Unauditable Ones as one of the layers that hides what it should bound.
Amazon Prime Video monolith (2023): The widely-cited Amazon Prime Video engineering blog post of March 2023, in which the Prime Video video-quality-analysis team described how they had moved their workload from a serverless microservices architecture (AWS Lambda plus Step Functions plus S3) back to a single monolithic process. Result: a ninety per cent reduction in infrastructure cost and the ability to handle higher loads. One of the most prominent public examples of microservices reversal.
Segment (140 services back to one): The Twilio Segment engineering blog post of mid-2018 by Alexandra Noonan, describing how the company consolidated approximately 140 microservices into a single monolithic codebase. The microservices architecture had become operationally expensive (deployment overhead, dependency-update fatigue, cross-service incident response); the consolidation simplified the system and reduced the on-call burden. A classical reference in the microservices-reversal canon.
Edsger W. Dijkstra: Dutch computer scientist (1930 to 2002), pioneer of structured programming, the shortest-path algorithm that bears his name, semaphores and the THE multiprogramming system. Turing Award winner (1972). Author of EWD-archive essays whose precise prose set a standard for technical writing in computer science. Cited in The Unauditable Ones for the 1968 THE Operating System paper where structured layering was first laid down.
THE multiprogramming system (Dijkstra, 1: Edsger W. Dijkstra's paper The Structure of the THE-Multiprogramming System (Communications of the ACM, 1968), describing the operating system developed at the Technische Hogeschool Eindhoven (THE) as a hierarchy of layers, each layer offering a virtual machine to the layer above and isolated from the layer below. The canonical citation for structured layering as a discipline of containing complexity. The verb shifted later from containing to postponing; that drift is the subject of the fourth diagnosis in The Unauditable Ones.
Lehman's laws of software evolution: A set of empirical laws of software evolution proposed by Meir M. Lehman from 1974 onwards, refined with Laszlo Belady through the 1980s. The second law (Increasing Complexity): as a program evolves, its complexity rises unless work is done to reduce or maintain it. The seventh law (Declining Quality): software perceived quality declines unless actively maintained against the changing environment. Cited in The Unauditable Ones as the missing reduction work that the modern stack postponed for decades.
Meir M. Lehman: Israeli-British computer scientist (1925 to 2010), professor at Imperial College London. Founder of the empirical study of software evolution; author with Laszlo Belady of the eponymous Lehman's laws (1974 onwards). His second law (complexity rises unless reduced) is the most-cited foundation of any argument for explicit lean work in software.
Carson Gross: American software engineer at Big Sky Software in Montana, author of HTMX and its predecessor intercooler.js. Public advocate of hypermedia-driven application architecture as an alternative to the SPA-plus-JSON-API model. The mind behind the 14-kilobyte answer to the question of frontend reactivity.
The Unauditable Ones (essay, 2026-06-10): The first of eighteen Vivian Voss Lean Software essays on how we got here, published 10 June 2026. The hook for an investigation that runs through to 7 October 2026. Argues that four things broke at the same time in the modern web stack. One: the count was never justified (a fresh npm create vite@latest lands 250 to 400 megabytes and 1,200 to 1,800 transitively pulled packages, a typical Next.js install 700 megabytes to a gigabyte, none of it required by what the project does; HTMX is 14 KB, MediaWiki runs Wikipedia, GOV.UK is HTML-first, Cloudflare's Pingora runs at one trillion requests per day with seventy per cent less CPU). Two: the boundary was never drawn (node_modules has no responsibility line, post-install scripts can modify each other, the latest tag can shift retroactively as the axios maintainer found on 31 March 2026; libc has a line, node_modules has none). Three: the workaround became architecture (Node.js's single-threaded event loop forced multi-container deployment as the way to use multi-core hardware; two decades later the container is the unit of thought, with dockerd at 183 containers above five gigabytes of memory; Amazon Prime Video, Segment and Istio are publicly walking back). Four: the layers hide what they should bound (ORM hides SQL, cache hides ORM, mesh hides services, operator hides YAML; between Dijkstra's 1968 THE multiprogramming paper and the modern stack the verb shifted from containing to postponing, contradicting Lehman's 1974 second law of software evolution). The roadmap: Phase 1 (17 June to 8 July) takes each axis one Wednesday at a time; Phase 2 (15 July to 12 August) is npm in detail; Phase 3 (19 August to 16 September) is Docker in detail; Phase 4 (23 to 30 September) is teams and the labour market; Phase 5 (7 October) asks what could still be.
The Open Group: An industry consortium formed in 1996 by the merger of X/Open and the Open Software Foundation. Custodian of the UNIX trademark since the 1990s and the body that runs the UNIX certification programme; maintainer of the Single UNIX Specification together with IEEE (POSIX) and ISO/IEC. The body that decides who is allowed to put the word UNIX on a box.
Single UNIX Specification (SUS): The vendor-neutral specification of what a Unix-branded operating system must implement to qualify for the UNIX trademark. Versions: SUSv1 (UNIX 95, 1995), SUSv2 (UNIX 98, 1997), SUSv3 (UNIX 03, 2002), SUSv4 (UNIX V7, POSIX.1-2008). Maintained by the Austin Group (The Open Group + IEEE + ISO/IEC JTC1) and aligned with POSIX since SUSv3. Certification is per product and time-limited.
UNIX 03: The brand level granted to operating systems certified against the Single UNIX Specification version 3 (2002). The brand carried by every version of macOS from 10.5 Leopard (2007) through macOS 15 Sequoia (2024) on the Open Group register. The most widely deployed Unix certification on the planet, hundreds of millions of devices.
UNIX V7 (SUSv4): The brand level granted to operating systems certified against the Single UNIX Specification version 4 (POSIX.1-2008). Only one product on the planet currently holds this certification: IBM AIX 7. macOS remains on UNIX 03; no other vendor has stepped up to V7.
macOS: Apple's desktop and laptop operating system, the successor to Mac OS X. Built on the Darwin foundation (XNU kernel plus FreeBSD-derived userland). Every version from 10.5 Leopard (2007) through macOS 15 Sequoia (2024) has been certified by The Open Group against the Single UNIX Specification version 3 and carries the UNIX 03 brand. The single largest carrier of certified Unix on the planet.
XNU: The kernel of Darwin, macOS, iOS, iPadOS, tvOS and watchOS. A hybrid kernel combining the Mach microkernel (originally from Carnegie Mellon) with components from FreeBSD (process model, network stack, virtual filesystem) and Apple's own I/O Kit. The X is from NeXTSTEP, NU stands for Not Unix; Steve Jobs's NeXT acquired Mach-based work that became the basis of XNU after the 1996 Apple-NeXT merger.
Open Connect (Netflix): Netflix's purpose-built content-delivery network, launched in 2012. Open Connect Appliances are 1U or 2U servers placed inside ISP networks (free of charge) to cache and serve Netflix's catalogue close to the viewer. Each appliance runs a lightly customised build of FreeBSD's development branch; the modifications are upstreamed. At peak the fleet pushes over 100 terabits per second, a substantial share of evening internet globally.
Open Connect Appliance (OCA): An individual server in the Netflix Open Connect content-delivery network. Runs a lightly customised FreeBSD build, with hardware offload (TLS, NIC) tuned for sustained video delivery. A current-generation OCA can push around 375 gigabits per second on roughly half its CPU thanks to TLS offload (a FreeBSD-13 feature). Deployed by the thousand inside ISP racks worldwide.
TLS offload (kernel): The technique of moving the symmetric-cryptography part of a TLS connection (record encryption and decryption) from userspace into the kernel, so it can be performed at line rate by NIC hardware or vectorised kernel code. Added to FreeBSD in version 13 (2021), with work led by Netflix engineer Drew Gallatin in collaboration with Chelsio Communications. The reason a single Open Connect Appliance can push around 375 gigabits per second on roughly half its CPU.
Drew Gallatin: Senior software engineer at Netflix, FreeBSD developer and committer. Architect of the FreeBSD kernel TLS-offload implementation that ships in FreeBSD 13 and lets Netflix's Open Connect Appliances serve encrypted video at around 375 gigabits per second per box. A long-time contributor to the FreeBSD networking stack.
Chelsio Communications: An American semiconductor company headquartered in Sunnyvale, California, designing high-performance Ethernet network interface cards and adapters with hardware offload (TCP, iSCSI, TLS). The collaboration partner with Netflix on the FreeBSD 13 kernel TLS-offload work that powers Open Connect.
Orbis OS: The operating system of Sony's PlayStation 4. A Sony-customised FreeBSD derivative based on FreeBSD 9 (2013 vintage). Sony publishes the modified kernel source on its open-source disclosure page as the BSD licence permits.
Orbis 2.0 (PS5): The operating system of Sony's PlayStation 5. A Sony-customised FreeBSD derivative based on FreeBSD 11.0 (__FreeBSD_version 1100122). Underneath the PS5 menus and the game launcher, a Unix-family machine.
Junos OS: Juniper Networks' operating system for its routing, switching and security products. FreeBSD-based since the first release in July 1998; the classical Junos line tracks the FreeBSD versions (R15.1 on FreeBSD 10, R21.1 on FreeBSD 12, R24.2 on FreeBSD 14). Runs on a substantial part of the internet's backbone routing equipment.
Junos OS Evolved: Juniper's newer operating-system variant, first shipped from R18.3 (2018). Built on a Linux kernel rather than on FreeBSD; runs in parallel to the classical Junos OS line on selected platforms. When someone says Juniper runs BSD they are talking about the classical line; when they say Juniper now runs Linux they are talking about Junos OS Evolved.
4.4BSD-Lite: The 1994 final non-commercial release of the Berkeley Software Distribution, the descendant of AT&T Unix developed at the University of California, Berkeley. Created after the AT&T-vs-BSDi suit settlement, with the AT&T-encumbered files removed. The direct ancestor of FreeBSD, NetBSD, OpenBSD, DragonFly BSD and (through NeXTSTEP) of Darwin and macOS.
AIX: IBM's commercial Unix operating system, first released in 1986 for the IBM RT PC and later for IBM POWER hardware. Actively developed; AIX 7 is the only operating system in the world certified at UNIX V7 (Single UNIX Specification version 4) level. Used by banks, insurance and large-enterprise back-office workloads.
Oracle Solaris: Sun Microsystems' commercial Unix operating system, introduced in 1992 (Solaris 2.x; the SunOS lineage goes back to the early 1980s). Acquired by Oracle with Sun in 2010; since then in what Oracle calls sustaining support, security patches and critical bug fixes only, no major new features. Still in telecom carrier infrastructure and long-cycle replicated databases.
HP-UX: Hewlett-Packard's commercial Unix operating system, first released in 1984. The platform's fortunes were tied to Intel's Itanium processor, which Intel discontinued; HPE still offers extended support contracts but the platform has no forward path. Runs where it runs, and where it runs, it tends to stay until the hardware fails.
Itanium: Intel's 64-bit IA-64 processor family, introduced in 2001 and discontinued in 2021. The platform on which HP-UX ran from the 11i v2 release onwards; the discontinuation removed HP-UX's forward path. Notoriously poor compatibility with x86 software was a recurring theme; the joke about the Itanic stuck.
z/OS: IBM's mainframe operating system, the successor to OS/390 and MVS. Certified Unix in a category most engineers never think about; the UNIX System Services component carries the certification. Runs a substantial part of the world's banking transaction processing on IBM Z hardware. The kind of system whose downtime appears in newspaper headlines because so few people remember it is there.
POWER (IBM): IBM's family of RISC processors (POWER1 through POWER10 and beyond), used in IBM Power Systems servers running AIX, IBM i and Linux. The platform AIX tracks in its public roadmap; new POWER processor generations drive new AIX releases.
Rick Reed: American software engineer, formerly an engineer at WhatsApp through the years when the service scaled to a billion users with a famously small engineering team. Co-presenter of public talks (with Jan Koum) describing how FreeBSD's coherence and its network stack let a small team carry a very large user base. After the Meta acquisition the WhatsApp stack changed; the architectural point survives.
Jan Koum: Co-founder of WhatsApp (with Brian Acton, 2009). Public proponent, during WhatsApp's independent years, of the FreeBSD plus Erlang backend that enabled the service's notoriously small engineering team to carry a notoriously large user base. WhatsApp was acquired by Meta (then Facebook) in 2014.
Where Unix Runs Today (essay, 2026-06-08: The first of three Vivian Voss Unix Universe essays on what Unix is, published 8 June 2026. Surveys where the certified Unix family and its direct BSD relatives are running right now: Apple (macOS 10.5 Leopard through macOS 15 Sequoia, certified UNIX 03; iOS, iPadOS, tvOS, watchOS on the same Darwin foundation), Netflix Open Connect (FreeBSD, around 375 gigabits per second per appliance via FreeBSD-13 TLS offload by Drew Gallatin and Chelsio, over 100 terabits per second at peak across the fleet), Sony PlayStation (PS4 Orbis OS on FreeBSD 9, PS5 Orbis 2.0 on FreeBSD 11, over 200 million units), Juniper Junos OS (FreeBSD-based since July 1998 with Junos OS Evolved as the parallel Linux-kernel variant), the commercial Unix line (AIX 7 the only UNIX V7, Solaris in sustaining support, HP-UX fading with Itanium, z/OS on IBM mainframes for world banking) and the historical WhatsApp-on-FreeBSD footnote. Linux is excluded from the list, not because it does not matter but because Linux is Unix-like, not Unix: written from scratch by Linus Torvalds in 1991 with no inherited Unix or BSD code, GNU userland rather than AT&T or Berkeley, never certified by The Open Group, not on the UNIX trademark register. You have been holding Unix all morning. The standards question is next Monday; the origin question the Monday after.
permissive licence: A class of free-software licence (BSD 2-Clause, BSD 3-Clause, MIT, Apache 2.0, ISC) that lets anyone build on the code, fork it, ship it, sell it, and combine it with other code under any licence at all, with original authors retaining attribution. The receiver is free to relicense their derivative work; ownership transfers cleanly. Contrasted with copyleft, which propagates conditions.
copyleft licence: A class of free-software licence (GPLv2, GPLv3, LGPL, AGPLv3, EUPL-1.2) under which anyone may receive the code and modify it but any distribution of the modified version must arrive under the same licence. The original author's licensing choice propagates indefinitely; the receiver holds the modified work under the conditions the original author set, in perpetuity. Coined by Richard Stallman as a play on copyright; the GNU General Public License is the canonical instance.
network copyleft: A subclass of copyleft (AGPLv3 is the canonical instance) that extends the share-alike requirement to the case where the modified version runs as a service rather than being distributed as a binary. Closes the SaaS loophole of classical GPL: under plain GPL, an operator could modify the code and run it as a hosted service without ever triggering the distribution clause; AGPL Section 13 requires that users interacting with the modified version over a network be offered the modified source.
AGPL (AGPLv3): The GNU Affero General Public License, version 3 (2007). A network-copyleft licence that extends the GPL share-alike requirement to network-hosted modifications via Section 13: an operator who modifies AGPL code and runs it as a service must offer the modified source to users interacting with it over the network. Closes the SaaS loophole of classical GPL while keeping the requirement on the program itself, not on surrounding management or hosting software. Sometimes called the honest middle of the licence landscape.
GPLv2: The GNU General Public License, version 2 (1991). The copyleft licence under which the Linux kernel is published and which has carried much of the free-software ecosystem since the early 1990s. Distribution of modified versions must be under the same licence; linking is the contested boundary at which conditions are deemed to propagate.
GPLv3: The GNU General Public License, version 3 (2007). Successor to GPLv2 with additional clauses on patent grants, tivoization (hardware that refuses to run modified firmware) and DRM. GCC moved to GPLv3 in 2007; the Linux kernel did not. FreeBSD 10.0 (January 2014) replaced GCC with Clang in its base system because GPLv3 did not allow a uniformly permissive base.
LGPL: The GNU Lesser General Public License (originally Library GPL, renamed in 1999). A weaker copyleft than GPL: a program that dynamically links to an LGPL library is not itself required to be LGPL, only changes to the library itself propagate. Used by glibc, GTK and many libraries where strict copyleft would prevent commercial adoption.
Apache License 2.0: A permissive open-source licence published by the Apache Software Foundation in 2004. Allows free use, modification and distribution including in proprietary derivatives; includes an explicit patent grant from contributors to users and a termination clause if the user sues for patent infringement. The licence under which Kubernetes, Android (most of AOSP), Apache HTTP Server, Kafka and many other major projects are released.
MIT License: A permissive open-source licence originating at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology in the late 1980s. Short, simple, and widely used (Node.js, React, jQuery, Rails, much of the npm ecosystem). Permits use, modification, distribution and sublicensing as long as the copyright notice and licence text are preserved.
BSD licence: A family of permissive open-source licences originating at the University of California, Berkeley. The 2-Clause BSD (Simplified) and 3-Clause BSD (New BSD; adds a no-endorsement clause) are the canonical modern forms; the older 4-Clause BSD with its advertising clause is deprecated. FreeBSD, OpenBSD and NetBSD are licensed under the 2-Clause variant; the philosophy is take it, use it, attribute the authors, no other conditions.
EUPL (EUPL-1.2): The European Union Public Licence, version 1.2 (2017). A copyleft open-source licence drafted by the European Commission, OSI-approved, with explicit EU-grounded legal interpretation and parallel legal validity in twenty-three official EU languages. Designed to give European public-sector deployers legal certainty under their own law rather than under foreign law. Compatibility-list-based: derivative works may be released under any of a defined set of compatible copyleft licences (GPL, AGPL, MPL, EUPL).
OSI (Open Source Initiative): The non-profit corporation founded in 1998 by Bruce Perens and Eric S. Raymond to steward the Open Source Definition. Maintains the canonical list of OSI-approved licences; a licence that violates the field-of-use clause (no discrimination against persons, groups or fields of endeavour) is not recognised as open source. Has explicitly declined to recognise SSPL on field-of-use grounds.
SaaS loophole: The gap in classical GPL that an operator can modify GPL code and run it as a network service (SaaS) without triggering the distribution clause, because the operator never distributes a binary to anyone outside their organisation. Users of the service have no right to the modified source. Closed by AGPL Section 13 (2007) for the program itself; SSPL (2018) extends the closure to surrounding management software, which is the move OSI declined to bless.
field-of-use discrimination: A criterion of the Open Source Definition, clause 6: 'The license must not restrict anyone from making use of the program in a specific field of endeavor.' A licence that forbids, for example, commercial use, or military use, or use by a competitor, fails this test. The SSPL has been declined recognition by the OSI on field-of-use grounds because its surrounding-stack requirement effectively forbids one specific kind of commercial activity (competing with the licensor as a managed service).
Clang: A C, C++, Objective-C and Objective-C++ compiler front-end built on top of the LLVM project, BSD-licensed. Started by Chris Lattner at Apple in 2007. Adopted as the FreeBSD base-system compiler in FreeBSD 10.0 (January 2014), replacing GCC, primarily because GCC had moved to GPLv3 and the BSD project wanted a uniformly permissive toolchain.
GCC: The GNU Compiler Collection. A C, C++ and multi-language compiler from the GNU project, originally written by Richard Stallman in 1987. Licensed under GPL: GPLv2 through GCC 4.2.1 (2007), GPLv3 from 4.2.2 onwards. The licence move to GPLv3 led FreeBSD 10.0 to replace it with Clang in 2014; Linux distributions remain on GCC.
Linux Foundation maintainer adjustment (: The October 2024 removal of approximately eleven Linux kernel maintainers from a particular jurisdiction subject to expanded US sanctions. The change appeared on the kernel mailing list under the formulation 'compliance requirements'; Linus Torvalds confirmed it shortly afterwards and declined to reverse it. The specific jurisdiction is incidental; the structural point is that the Linux Foundation is registered in the United States, and US sanctions reach the operations of US-registered organisations.
xz / xz-utils: The xz compression library and command-line utilities, descended from the LZMA SDK. Provides the LZMA2 algorithm used in .xz files, common in Linux distributions for kernel images, package archives and many other compressed payloads. The shared library is liblzma. The project was the target of a multi-year social-engineering attack culminating in CVE-2024-3094 (March 2024).
CVE-2024-3094: The Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures identifier for the xz/liblzma backdoor disclosed by Andres Freund on 29 March 2024. CVSS score 10.0. A maintainer operating under the name Jia Tan inserted a hidden authentication bypass into the build of liblzma (xz 5.6.0 and 5.6.1) that, when linked into the OpenSSH server through systemd's notify-socket path, permitted remote code execution to an attacker holding the corresponding private key. Discovered by curiosity (an unusual SSH CPU spike), not by audit infrastructure.
Schrems II: Court of Justice of the European Union, Case C-311/18, judgment of 16 July 2020. The CJEU held that the EU-US Privacy Shield framework was invalid because US surveillance law under FISA Section 702 and Executive Order 12333 does not meet the proportionality standard required by EU law. Restated the obligation on European data controllers to assess whether transfers to third countries provide essentially equivalent protection. A foundational data-sovereignty ruling.
Privacy Shield (EU-US): A 2016 framework agreement between the European Commission and the US Department of Commerce intended to legalise transatlantic personal-data transfers from the EU to participating US companies. Invalidated by Schrems II (CJEU, Case C-311/18, July 2020) on grounds that US surveillance law (FISA 702, EO 12333) lacks the proportionality protections required by EU law. Replaced in 2023 by the EU-US Data Privacy Framework, the long-term durability of which remains contested.
FISA Section 702: Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (50 U.S.C. § 1881a), authorising the US intelligence community to target non-US persons reasonably believed to be located outside the United States for foreign-intelligence purposes, including by collection from US electronic-communications service providers. Cited by the CJEU in Schrems II as one of the legal bases that fails the EU proportionality standard.
Executive Order 12333: A US Executive Order signed by President Ronald Reagan in 1981, extended subsequently, setting out the framework for US intelligence activities including signals intelligence. Cited alongside FISA Section 702 in Schrems II as one of the legal bases that fails the EU proportionality standard.
CLOUD Act (full): The Clarifying Lawful Overseas Use of Data Act, signed into US law in March 2018, amending the Stored Communications Act. Permits US law enforcement to compel US-based service providers to hand over data the providers possess, regardless of where the data is physically stored; the law thereby reaches into data centres located outside US territory through the corporate connection to the US parent. A foundational data-sovereignty concern for European public-sector procurement.
DINUM: Direction interministerielle du numerique. France's Interministerial Digital Directorate, attached to the Prime Minister's office. The body responsible for the French state's digital transformation strategy. In April 2026 DINUM announced a migration of its own workstations from Windows to Linux and directed all French ministries to formalise plans for eliminating extra-European software dependencies by autumn 2026.
HarmonyOS: Huawei's in-house operating system, originally launched in 2019 for IoT devices after the US Entity List restrictions cut Huawei off from its Android licence and Google services. Expanded to phones (2021) and PCs (HarmonyOS PC, May 2025) after the company's Windows licence was withdrawn under continuing US sanctions. A working example of a sovereignty pivot at the operating-system layer driven by jurisdictional exposure rather than by ideology.
BharOS: An Indian Linux distribution developed at the Indian Institute of Technology Madras (IIT Madras) with government sponsorship. Launched in 2023, targeted at Indian government and public-sector devices, with an emphasis on local control of the software supply chain. One of several jurisdiction-specific sovereignty distributions emerging in parallel to European efforts.
IIT Madras: Indian Institute of Technology Madras, Chennai. A public technical university and one of the original IITs, founded in 1959. The host institution of the BharOS project and a long-standing centre of Indian computer-science research.
Loongson: A family of MIPS-compatible (and later LoongArch) processors developed in China by the Institute of Computing Technology of the Chinese Academy of Sciences and commercialised by Loongson Technology. Adopted in Chinese government procurement directives as part of the move towards domestic instruction-set-architecture work at the chip layer.
Entity List (US): The US Department of Commerce's Bureau of Industry and Security list of foreign persons (including organisations) subject to specific export licence requirements. Inclusion effectively cuts the listed entity off from US-origin technologies. Huawei was added in 2019, leading directly to the company's HarmonyOS programme as a self-developed operating system to replace its lost Windows and Android licences.
Open Is Not Owned (essay, 2026-06-07): The first of three Vivian Voss IT Philosophy essays on European software sovereignty, published 7 June 2026. Sets the question.
